foodFirst.line: If total daily protein hits **1.6-2.2 g/kg** spread across meals, EAAs and BCAAs add essentially nothing — each meal already crosses the leucine threshold.
foodFirst.note: A 25-30g serving of whey or any complete protein supplies all 9 EAAs plus ~2.5g leucine (whey is ~10% leucine), usually cheaper per gram of leucine than isolated EAA powder.

dosing.note: Typical anabolic dose is **~6-15g EAAs providing ~2.5-3g leucine** (the approximate threshold to maximally trigger muscle protein synthesis). BCAA-only doses are commonly 5-10g but lack the full amino pool. Per-kg dosing is not standard for these.

timing.line: Only meaningfully useful **fasted or when a long gap separates protein meals** (e.g. fasted morning training) — take around the session. With protein-adequate meals the timing question is moot.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed** — not habit-forming, no receptor downregulation; these are just food-derived nutrients.

antagonists.role: Large BCAA doses compete with these at the shared large-neutral-amino-acid transporter into the brain, which is the theoretical basis for BCAA use in fatigue — but can blunt serotonin/dopamine precursor uptake.

cost.perDose: ~$1-2 SGD per EAA serving; ~$0.50-1 per 25g whey serving
cost.math: EAA/BCAA tubs run roughly $40-70 SGD for ~30 servings; complete-protein powder is typically cheaper per gram of leucine delivered.
cost.note: For most people the cheapest effective 'EAA supplement' is an extra scoop of complete protein or an egg/dairy serving.

contra.advice: If you hit ~1.6-2.2 g/kg/day of quality protein, EAAs/BCAAs are redundant spend, not a benefit.
Inherited inability to metabolize BCAAs — supplemental BCAAs are dangerous; strictly avoid.
Amino-acid load and nitrogen handling can be clinically relevant; use only under medical supervision.

High-dose BCAAs are contraindicated in existing ALS (trials showed accelerated respiratory decline); those with a personal/family history may prefer to avoid high-dose BCAA loading pending better data.

quality.line: Choose powders that are **third-party tested** (e.g. Informed Sport, NSF Certified for Sport, or USP verified) — amino acid powders have historically shown label-accuracy and contamination issues, and competitive athletes need banned-substance screening.

nonResponders.line: If your baseline protein is already high, expect **no detectable change** in strength, recovery, or body composition from adding EAAs/BCAAs — the anabolic ceiling is already met.

synergy.why: Amino acids only build muscle when paired with a mechanical stimulus; without training the MPS bump is small and transient.
Not additive — whole protein already contains all EAAs, so pairing them is redundant rather than synergistic; pick one.
Leucine is the primary mTOR trigger within the EAA pool; hitting the ~2.5-3g leucine threshold is what actually drives the synthesis signal.

dosing.cap: 400 mg/day for most healthy adults; single doses rarely need to exceed 200 mg for thermogenesis
dosing.note: **3-6 mg/kg** is the genuinely standard evidence-based range for caffeine's ergogenic and thermogenic effect. A 70 kg person = ~200-400 mg. Start at the low end (3 mg/kg) to gauge tolerance and cardiovascular response.

form.buy: **Caffeine anhydrous** in a labelled, fixed-dose tablet or capsule so you know exactly how many mg you take; pharmaceutical-grade, third-party-tested.
form.avoid: **Pure powdered caffeine** sold loose — a level teaspoon holds ~3 g, roughly 2.5x a toxic dose and potentially fatal, and it is impossible to measure safely with kitchen tools. Also avoid proprietary 'blend' pre-workouts that hide the caffeine dose.
form.withFood: Absorption is near-complete either way; food only slightly slows the peak. For fat-oxidation purposes many take it fasted before morning exercise.
form.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability is ~99%, plasma peak at 30-60 min, half-life ~5 h (longer in slow metabolisers, pregnancy, or on oral contraceptives).

timing.line: Take **30-60 min before activity** to align the plasma peak with exercise. Keep the last dose **at least 8-10 h before bed** — caffeine's half-life means an afternoon dose measurably degrades sleep depth even if you fall asleep fine.

cycling.line: **Tolerance to the thermogenic and appetite effects develops within days to a few weeks**, while tolerance to jitter/insomnia lags. Periodic lower-dose or caffeine-free stretches restore sensitivity; the metabolic bump is largest in habitual non-users.

contra.advice: Caffeine raises heart rate and blood pressure acutely; clear thermogenic dosing with a doctor first.
Keep total caffeine **under ~200 mg/day** per obstetric guidance; do not use it as a fat-loss tool during this period.
Caffeine can trigger or worsen anxiety, especially in fast-onset high doses; go low or avoid.

contra.flag: Combining with other stimulants (yohimbine, synephrine, ephedrine, high-dose pre-workouts)
contra.advice: Stacking stimulants compounds cardiovascular strain — this is where most 'fat-burner' adverse events occur. Do not layer them.
Sleep loss impairs fat loss more than caffeine helps it; if caffeine is costing you sleep, the net effect on body composition is negative.

biomarkers.why: Caffeine's acute cardiovascular load shows up as elevated resting/standing HR; a persistent rise signals your dose or timing is too aggressive.
biomarkers.range: Typical resting 60-100 bpm; watch for a sustained upward shift from your own baseline
biomarkers.when: Morning, before dosing, vs. an off-caffeine baseline

biomarkers.why: Acute pressor effect is real; matters if you are near or above the hypertension threshold.
biomarkers.range: Optimal <120/80 mmHg; caffeine can transiently add ~5-15 mmHg systolic in non-habitual users
biomarkers.when: Resting, and note if readings run high after dosing

nonResponders.line: **CYP1A2 genotype** drives clearance: slow metabolisers get prolonged exposure (more jitter, sleep disruption, and in some studies more cardiovascular risk) with little extra fat-ox benefit, while fast metabolisers clear it quickly and may feel little. **ADORA2A** variants are associated with who gets anxiety from it. Habitual heavy users see a blunted thermogenic response overall.

synergy.why: The clearest use case — caffeine reliably raises fat oxidation during exercise and lowers perceived effort, so you train harder and burn more fat than either alone.
Smooths the jittery/anxious edge and stabilises focus without removing the alertness — a well-supported pairing for tolerability, not extra fat loss.
Catechins plus caffeine show a small additive effect on 24 h energy expenditure and fat oxidation vs. caffeine alone; this is the rationale behind green-tea fat-burners.

foodFirst.line: **Black coffee or tea** delivers caffeine plus polyphenols at near-zero cost and self-limits dose better than pills.
foodFirst.note: A cup of brewed coffee is ~80-100 mg, tea ~30-50 mg — enough to hit thermogenic doses without ever touching a supplement.

cost.math: A bottle of ~100 x 200 mg caffeine tablets runs ~S$8-15 locally, so a few cents per dose.
cost.note: One of the cheapest active supplements that exists; brewed coffee is comparably cheap. There is no reason to pay a premium for a branded 'fat-burner'.

quality.line: Choose a product with a **stated caffeine dose in mg** and third-party testing (e.g. NSF, USP, Informed Sport); avoid proprietary-blend fat-burners that conceal how much stimulant you are actually taking.

form.buy: **Yohimbine HCl** in a standardized, third-party-tested form — you want a known milligram content per capsule.
form.avoid: Crude **yohimbe bark extract** — total alkaloid content is variable and unpredictable, which drives erratic dosing and worse side effects.
form.withFood: Take **fasted**. Even a small insulin rise from food blunts the lipolytic effect — in controlled studies the lipid-mobilising action of yohimbine was completely suppressed after a meal.
form.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability is **highly variable (~7–87%, mean ~33%)**, largely due to first-pass hepatic metabolism and CYP2D6 activity — this is why the same dose can do little in one person and spike anxiety in another.

dosing.cap: ~20 mg/day total, split into 2 doses
dosing.note: **0.2 mg/kg** is the dose used across the oral lipolysis studies; the Ostojic 2006 fat-loss trial used a flat **20 mg/day in two doses** for 21 days. Start far lower (2.5–5 mg) to test tolerance before scaling — cardiovascular and anxiety effects are dose-dependent.

timing.line: Take **30–45 min before fasted cardio** (typically morning). The effect depends on low insulin plus catecholamine drive from exercise, so pairing it with a fasted training window is the whole point.

cycling.line: Best used during a **defined cutting phase**, not year-round — there's little rationale for chronic use, and stimulant-type side effects compound with continuous dosing.

contra.advice: Yohimbine is an alpha-2 antagonist that raises noradrenergic tone — it can provoke or sharply worsen anxiety and trigger panic attacks. Avoid.
At higher doses it raises heart rate and blood pressure and can be arrhythmogenic. Do not use with uncontrolled hypertension, arrhythmia, or known cardiac disease.
Combining with MAO inhibitors risks a dangerous hypertensive/adrenergic reaction. Avoid entirely.
Additive noradrenergic and serotonergic load; discuss with a clinician before combining.
Not established as safe — avoid.

Yohimbine is prescription-only or not permitted in supplements in several jurisdictions (e.g. not allowed in supplements sold in Singapore, prescription-only/unlicensed in the UK, Australia and Canada). Check local law before sourcing.

biomarkers.why: Yohimbine can dose-dependently raise BP; track it to catch a problematic pressor response early.
biomarkers.range: Aim to stay <130/80 mmHg at rest; do not use if baseline is already elevated
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then periodically after dosing during a cycle

biomarkers.why: Elevated adrenergic tone can raise HR — a rising resting HR flags too much stimulant load.
biomarkers.range: Compare against your personal baseline rather than an absolute cutoff

quality.line: Choose **yohimbine HCl with a verified per-capsule milligram content** and third-party testing; standardized HCl is far more predictable than bark extract, where alkaloid content and contaminants vary widely.

nonResponders.line: Wide differences in **hepatic/CYP2D6 metabolism** mean response is genuinely bimodal — some people feel strong effects (and side effects) at low doses while others notice little. Non-response is not a reason to keep escalating past tolerance.

synergy.why: Insulin blocks lipolysis, so the effect only appears when insulin is low — in studies the effect was abolished by a meal, making fasting a genuine enabler.
Commonly stacked to amplify catecholamine-driven lipolysis, but it also stacks the cardiovascular and anxiety load — start with one before combining.

form.buy: **Standardized green tea extract** stating EGCG content in mg per capsule, third-party tested (NSF/USP). A caffeinated extract better matches the caffeine + catechin synergy behind the fat-oxidation effect.
form.avoid: **High-dose single-catechin bolus** products (>800 mg EGCG/day) and anything taken as one large fasted dose — the main hepatotoxicity trigger.
form.withFood: **Take with food.** Fasted intake raises peak EGCG and is the condition most linked to liver injury; food blunts the spike for a small efficacy trade-off.
form.bioavailability: **Low oral bioavailability**; splitting the daily amount across meals is fine. Fasted dosing raises blood EGCG but also raises liver-injury risk, so it is not worth chasing.

biomarkers.why: Concentrated green tea extract is an established cause of idiosyncratic **drug-induced liver injury**; enzymes catch it early.
biomarkers.range: ALT roughly 7-56 U/L; stop and seek review if rising or >2-3x upper normal
biomarkers.when: Baseline before starting, and periodically if using >300-400 mg EGCG/day regularly

antagonists.role: Catechins **chelate dietary iron** and lower its absorption; separate extract from iron supplements or iron-rich meals by 1-2 h, especially if iron-deficient.

foodFirst.line: **Brewed green tea** (3-4 cups/day) delivers catechins plus caffeine with far lower hepatotoxicity risk than concentrated extract.
foodFirst.note: Whole-tea intake has never been meaningfully tied to liver injury; the risk sits with concentrated capsules.

cost.math: A bottle of ~120 capsules runs roughly S$20-40 depending on standardization
cost.note: Rough Singapore retail range; verify EGCG mg per capsule, not just total extract weight.

dosing.cap: EFSA flags ~800 mg EGCG/day from supplements as the level associated with liver signals; stay below it
dosing.note: Studied effective range is ~300-500 mg EGCG/day; the fat-oxidation effect is real but small, so higher is not better.

timing.line: Pair a dose with **caffeine before training** to lift fat oxidation during the session; still take it with a meal rather than fully fasted.

contra.flag: Liver disease or elevated liver enzymes
contra.advice: Avoid concentrated extract entirely; it is a recognized cause of idiosyncratic liver injury.

contra.flag: Concurrent hepatotoxic drugs or heavy alcohol
contra.advice: Stacking liver stressors raises injury risk; skip the extract or stick to brewed tea.
Catechins further suppress iron absorption; keep well separated from iron intake or avoid.
High-dose EGCG can interfere with folate metabolism; avoid concentrated supplements and prefer moderate brewed tea.

quality.line: Pick extracts that **state EGCG in mg** and carry third-party testing (NSF/USP); avoid proprietary blends that hide the actual catechin dose.

nonResponders.line: The fat-oxidation effect is **small and blunted in habitual high-caffeine consumers**; the clearest responses show up in lower-caffeine users, and COMT genotype may modulate this.

synergy.why: Co-ingestion may **prolong adrenergic fat-cell signaling** (proposed COMT inhibition by EGCG plus caffeine's adenosine blockade); mechanism is largely in vitro and the human thermogenic effect is small.
Catechin intake around training modestly **increases fat oxidation** during and after exercise in several trials.

form.buy: **Micronized DHEA** in a third-party-tested oral form; independent assays have repeatedly found actual content well off the label claim, so USP/NSF verification matters here more than for most supplements.
form.avoid: Don't confuse plain DHEA with **7-keto-DHEA** — 7-keto does not convert to sex hormones and is a different product for a different purpose.
form.withFood: Fat-soluble; take with a meal containing some fat to aid absorption.
form.bioavailability: Micronization (smaller particle size) improves oral absorption over non-micronized powder.

biomarkers.why: The standard, stable serum marker of DHEA status — draw a baseline to confirm you are actually low (levels fall steeply with age) before supplementing, then to titrate dose.
biomarkers.range: Strongly age- and sex-dependent; roughly ~80-560 ug/dL across adult men (higher in the 20s-30s, lower in women) and declining ~10%/decade. Aim to restore toward a healthy young-adult range, not above it.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then 6-8 weeks after starting or changing dose

biomarkers.why: DHEA is a precursor that converts downstream; track sex-hormone levels to catch over-conversion (e.g. rising estradiol in men, rising testosterone/androgens in women causing acne or hair changes).
biomarkers.range: Keep within normal sex-specific reference ranges

biomarkers.why: Because DHEA feeds androgen pathways, older men should have prostate status established before use and monitored during it.
DHEA can modestly lower HDL cholesterol, most notably in women.

foodFirst.line: There is **no meaningful dietary source** of DHEA — the body makes it from cholesterol in the adrenal glands, and 'wild yam' creams do NOT convert to DHEA in humans (that conversion only happens in a lab).
foodFirst.note: Sleep, stress/cortisol management and resistance training support your own endogenous hormone axis and are the first levers before any precursor supplement.

dosing.cap: 50 mg/day for general use; higher doses sharply increase side-effect risk and are research/clinical territory only
dosing.note: Typical studied replacement is 25-50 mg/day in older adults. Women generally start lower (~10-25 mg) because they are far more sensitive to androgenic effects (acne, oily skin, hair changes). Start low, dose to labs, not to a fixed number.

timing.line: Take in the **morning** with food, mimicking the body's natural diurnal peak in adrenal steroid output.

cycling.line: Not a compound to run blind indefinitely — re-check DHEA-S and downstream sex hormones every **3-6 months** and adjust or pause based on labs rather than cycling on a fixed calendar.

contra.advice: Avoid with prostate, breast, ovarian or uterine cancer (or high risk) — DHEA feeds estrogen and androgen pathways. Physician oversight only.
Little to no benefit when your own levels are already ample, and it can suppress your natural axis — this is a tool for age-related decline, not a young-athlete booster.

contra.flag: Women prone to androgenic effects / PCOS
contra.advice: Higher sensitivity to acne, oily skin, facial hair and voice changes; use the lowest dose and monitor.
DHEA is **banned by WADA** at all times (S1 anabolic agents) — a positive test is likely.
Avoid — hormonal effects on the fetus are not established as safe.

HSA treats DHEA as a potent hormonal (medicinal) ingredient, not a permitted general-sale health-supplement ingredient, and has issued safety alerts and at least one recall — treat it as prescription-only and use it only under a doctor.

quality.line: Content varies notoriously between products, so choose a **USP- or NSF-verified, third-party-tested** micronized form; label mg claims cannot be trusted without independent assay.

nonResponders.line: People whose DHEA-S is already in a healthy range (most under-40s, and some older adults) get minimal benefit — response tracks how depleted you were to begin with, which is why a baseline lab is worth more than any dose guess.

synergy.with: Weight-bearing / resistance exercise + vitamin D and calcium
synergy.why: For the bone-density goal, DHEA's modest effect (mainly in older women) is best paired with the mechanical loading and bone nutrients that actually drive mineralization.

cost.perDose: ~$0.15-0.40 SGD per 25 mg dose at international retail
cost.note: Cheap as a raw material, but in Singapore it is not a freely-sold supplement, so realistic access is gated by a doctor's visit and dispensing rather than shelf price.

form.buy: Look for a **water-extracted (aqueous) standardized extract**, ideally standardized to a stated **eurycomanone** percentage (commonly 0.8-2%) or quassinoid/eurypeptide content — this aqueous form is used in nearly all positive human trials.
form.avoid: Raw un-standardized root powder and vague '200:1' ratio claims with no quassinoid assay — potency is unknowable and adulteration/heavy-metal risk is higher.
form.withFood: Can be taken with or without food; a little food helps if it feels stimulating on an empty stomach.
form.bioavailability: Water-soluble quassinoids are reasonably absorbed; there is no need for a fat-based or 'liposomal' delivery form.

biomarkers.why: The primary mechanism is freeing bound T and modestly raising T in stressed or lower-baseline men; a before/after pair shows whether you actually respond.
biomarkers.range: Total T adult male ~10.4-34.7 nmol/L (300-1000 ng/dL); free T ~0.2-0.6 nmol/L (lab-dependent)
biomarkers.when: Morning (7-10am) fasted, before starting and again after 4-8 weeks

biomarkers.why: Sex-hormone-binding globulin binds testosterone; a fall in SHBG is a plausible route to more usable free T, though trial results on SHBG are mixed.
A stress-hormone reduction is one of the better-supported effects; pairing cortisol with the T:cortisol ratio captures the anti-stress angle.
It is a long-term herbal taken daily, rare liver-injury case reports exist, and root products vary in quality; a periodic liver panel is a sensible safety check.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and every ~3 months of continuous use

needs.role: A cofactor in testosterone synthesis; deficiency blunts T and can cap any benefit from freeing bound hormone.
Low vitamin D status is associated with lower testosterone; correcting a deficiency supports the same axis.

antagonists.role: Both suppress testosterone and raise cortisol, working directly against the compound's mechanism.

foodFirst.line: There is no dietary source of the plant's quassinoids, but the **foundations that set your testosterone** — 7-8h sleep, adequate protein and total calories, resistance training, and correcting zinc/vitamin-D deficiency — move free T further than any herb.
foodFirst.note: Fix these first; the extract is a modest add-on, not a substitute.

cost.math: A month of standardized water-extract capsules (200-400 mg/day) runs roughly SGD $20-40.
cost.note: Quassinoid-standardized, third-party-tested extract costs more than raw root powder — worth it for potency and contaminant testing.

dosing.cap: ~400 mg/day of standardized water extract
dosing.note: Human trials typically used 100-400 mg/day of standardized aqueous extract (standardized to roughly 0.8-2% eurycomanone), with 200 mg/day the most common; more is not clearly better. Start at the low end. No validated per-kg dosing exists.

timing.line: Take in the **morning** — it fits the diurnal testosterone/cortisol rhythm and some users find it mildly activating, which is unwelcome at night.

cycling.line: Cycling is commonly practiced (e.g. weekdays on, weekends off, or periodic breaks) but is **not well-studied**; breaks are reasonable for reassessing benefit and giving the liver a rest rather than for any proven tolerance effect.

contra.advice: With prostate cancer, or a personal/strong family history of hormone-sensitive cancer, avoid self-supplementing and discuss with a doctor first.

contra.flag: **Already on TRT or other hormonal therapy**
contra.advice: Stacking a T-modulating herb on exogenous testosterone is redundant and unstudied — clear it with the prescriber.
Avoid — hormonal effects are not characterised for these situations.

contra.flag: **Existing liver disease or other hepatotoxic meds/supplements**
contra.advice: Be cautious with a daily long-term herbal, given documented liver-injury case reports, and monitor liver enzymes.

quality.line: Eurycoma root can **accumulate heavy metals** (mercury, lead) — one survey found ~26% of products exceeded the mercury limit — and the market has documented adulteration (including with sildenafil); buy an extract third-party tested for heavy metals and microbes that states a standardized quassinoid/eurycomanone content.

nonResponders.line: Men with **already-normal, high testosterone and low stress** tend to see little; the clearest effects are in older, lower-baseline, or stressed individuals. If free T, cortisol and libido are unchanged after ~8 weeks, it is not working for you.

synergy.why: Complementary anti-stress route — ashwagandha has its own evidence for lowering cortisol and modestly raising testosterone, reinforcing the same axis.
Correcting these removes a ceiling on testosterone production so freeing bound T actually translates to usable hormone.
Boron has preliminary evidence for lowering SHBG and nudging free testosterone up, overlapping with the free-T mechanism.

form.buy: **NR (nicotinamide riboside)** or **NMN** as high-purity, third-party-tested powder or capsules with a batch certificate of analysis; NR has cleaner regulatory status and more human data.
form.avoid: Cheap unverified **NMN** — independent testing has repeatedly found products under-dosed or degraded, since NMN is unstable to heat and moisture.
form.withFood: Can be taken with or without food; no strong food interaction is established.
form.bioavailability: Oral **NAD+ itself is poorly absorbed** — the precursors work because they are salvaged into NAD+; sublingual/liposomal 'enhanced absorption' claims are largely unproven marketing.

dosing.cap: ~1000 mg/day
dosing.note: **NR** is studied at 250-1000 mg/day and **NMN** at 250-900 mg/day, both raising blood NAD+ dose-dependently; most human trials use 250-500 mg. No per-kg dosing standard exists — start low.

timing.line: Take in the **morning** — NAD+ metabolism is circadian-linked and dosing early may align with the natural daytime peak; there is no proven benefit to evening dosing.

biomarkers.why: The **direct target** — the only marker that confirms the precursor is actually raising NAD+; rises reliably with NR/NMN.
biomarkers.range: Assay-dependent; no universal reference range — track your own change from baseline on the same lab.

biomarkers.why: Nicotinamide breakdown is cleared via **methylation**; a rise is theoretically possible at high doses, though human NR trials (up to 3000 mg) have not shown one — a cheap check for high-dose users.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and after 8-12 weeks at higher doses

needs.role: Excess precursor is excreted as methylated nicotinamide, drawing on the **methyl pool** — a theoretical demand; human trials show methyl balance is largely maintained without added donors, so this is precautionary.

depletes.role: Methylating the nicotinamide breakdown product consumes **S-adenosylmethionine** — a real mechanism, but human high-dose trials have not shown methyl-donor depletion or a homocysteine rise.

foodFirst.line: **Exercise and fasting/caloric restriction** raise NAD+ through the same salvage pathway, and dietary **niacin (B3) and tryptophan** feed NAD+ synthesis — the cheapest levers first.
foodFirst.note: Niacin (nicotinic acid) also raises NAD+ but causes flushing at higher doses.

cost.math: A month of third-party-tested NR/NMN at 300-500 mg/day typically runs ~$40-90 SGD; premium branded lines cost more.
cost.note: NMN pricing is volatile and quality varies widely — the cheapest options are the ones most likely to be under-dosed.

quality.line: Prefer products with a **batch certificate of analysis (COA)** verifying identity and purity, ideally NSF or USP verified where available — independent testing has found many NMN products contain far less than labelled.

synergy.why: Commonly co-taken to **replenish methyl groups** used clearing the precursor; sensible in theory but human trials show methylation stays balanced without it, so outcome data is thin.
Physical activity independently **boosts NAD+ and mitochondrial biogenesis**, reinforcing the same metabolic pathway the supplement targets.

contra.advice: NAD+ fuels cellular metabolism and there is a **theoretical concern** (from tumour-bearing animal models) that precursors could feed existing malignant cells; unresolved and not shown in humans — discuss with your oncologist before use.
**No adequate safety data** — avoid.

nonResponders.line: Blood NAD+ almost always rises, but **clinical benefits (energy, longevity) remain unproven** in humans — a measurable NAD+ increase does not guarantee any felt effect, so judge by outcomes, not the lab alone.

form.buy: **Trans-resveratrol** standardized extract (commonly from Japanese knotweed / Polygonum cuspidatum) or **pterostilbene**, its more absorbable dimethylated cousin; micronized forms can further improve uptake.
form.avoid: Cheap **cis-resveratrol** or vague extracts that don't state a trans-resveratrol %. Trans- is the active isomer and degrades under light and heat.
form.withFood: Take with a **meal** — both molecules are lipophilic. Evidence that dietary fat substantially boosts resveratrol absorption is limited and mixed, so treat it as a sensible default rather than a proven amplifier.
form.bioavailability: Resveratrol is well absorbed but undergoes rapid, extensive **glucuronidation/sulfation**, leaving very low free-compound blood levels; **pterostilbene**'s two methyl groups slow conjugation, giving higher bioavailability and a longer half-life. The oft-cited ~20% vs ~80% figures are from **rat** pharmacokinetics, not humans.

biomarkers.why: A human RCT of **pterostilbene 250 mg/day** monotherapy **raised LDL** (~+17 mg/dL) — worth monitoring on higher pterostilbene doses.
Tracks the modest, **inconsistent anti-inflammatory** effect; results are mixed, so treat as exploratory.
The clearest human signal is improved **glycemic control / insulin sensitivity**, mainly in people with diabetes or metabolic dysfunction.
Meta-analyses show **small systolic reductions**, mostly at higher doses (≥150 mg/day).

needs.role: Both compounds are lipophilic; taking with a meal is sensible, though the absorption benefit for resveratrol is modest and mixed in human data.

antagonists.role: Resveratrol has antiplatelet activity; additive bleeding risk.
Inhibits these enzymes in vitro, potentially raising levels of statins, warfarin and others; clinical magnitude uncertain.
In older adults, high-dose resveratrol (250 mg/day) **blunted** some training-induced cardiovascular gains in one RCT.

foodFirst.line: Red wine, grapes, berries and peanuts carry resveratrol, but only in **trace amounts** — a glass of red wine has roughly 0.5-1 mg versus the 150-1000 mg used in trials.
foodFirst.note: Eat the polyphenol-rich foods for the whole matrix, but understand you cannot reach study doses through diet.

cost.math: A bottle of ~60 caps (250-500 mg trans-resveratrol) typically runs ~$25-45 SGD.
cost.note: Pterostilbene and enhanced blends sit at the higher end.

dosing.cap: Doses above ~1 g/day commonly cause GI distress (cramping, diarrhea) with little added benefit.
dosing.note: Common human ranges: **resveratrol 150-500 mg/day**, **pterostilbene 50-250 mg/day**. Be candid that human longevity/healthspan outcomes have been underwhelming despite strong preclinical hype.

timing.line: Take with the day's **largest meal**; specific time-of-day has no strong evidence base.

contra.advice: Resveratrol is a **phytoestrogen** with mixed estrogenic activity — caution with estrogen-sensitive cancers; clear it with your clinician.
Antiplatelet effect adds bleeding risk — stop well before surgery and discuss if on anticoagulants.
May raise blood levels of statins, warfarin and similar drugs; check for interactions.
Insufficient safety data — avoid.

quality.line: Choose **third-party-tested** products standardized to a stated **trans-resveratrol %** (NSF/USP as neutral quality guidance); store cool and dark since trans- degrades with light and heat.

nonResponders.line: Direct SIRT1 activation is **contested** (early findings were partly an assay artifact of the fluorophore-tagged peptide substrate), and human longevity, cognitive and performance results have been weak — many people see no measurable change.

synergy.why: Sirtuins require NAD+ as substrate, so pairing a sirtuin activator with NAD+ replenishment is mechanistically complementary — human benefit still unproven.
A complementary polyphenol sometimes co-formulated with stilbenes and studied for metabolic endpoints; combined efficacy in humans is not established.

form.buy: Generic **high-purity fisetin** (typically extracted from the wax tree, *Rhus succedanea*, or smoketree, *Cotinus coggygria*); look for ≥98% purity with a certificate of analysis. **Liposomal or phytosome** forms, or co-formulation with a fat carrier, meaningfully improve its otherwise poor uptake.
form.avoid: Cheap low-purity extracts without a COA, and standard powder taken on an empty stomach (most passes through unabsorbed).
form.withFood: Take with a **fat-containing meal** — fisetin is lipophilic and absorption is substantially higher with dietary fat.
form.bioavailability: **Poor and short-lived**: low aqueous solubility, extensive first-pass glucuronidation, and a short plasma half-life. This is why hit-and-run senolytic dosing uses large amounts over 1-2 days rather than daily micro-doses.

dosing.cap: ~1,000-1,600 mg/day at typical body weights (e.g. ~1,400 mg at 70 kg)
dosing.note: The **~20 mg/kg/day** figure comes from human senolytic trials (Mayo/Scripps lineage) given for **2 consecutive days per month**, not daily. It is a research dose, not an established consumer standard — dose-response and long-term safety in humans are still being studied. This is a ceiling to understand, not a target to chase.

timing.line: Take the **full daily dose with the fattiest meal of the day** across the 1-2 dosing days; splitting into 2 portions with separate fatty meals may aid absorption.

cycling.line: The senolytic model is **intermittent 'hit-and-run'**: a short high-dose burst (typically 2 consecutive days) then weeks off, repeated roughly monthly. Continuous daily dosing is not the studied paradigm.

foodFirst.line: Fisetin occurs in **strawberries, apples, persimmons and onions**, but dietary amounts are milligrams — orders of magnitude below the senolytic doses studied.
foodFirst.note: Whole-food fisetin is fine for baseline flavonoid intake but cannot reach a senolytic threshold; don't treat 'eat more strawberries' as equivalent.

biomarkers.why: Senescent cells drive a pro-inflammatory secretory phenotype (SASP); a downward trend in systemic inflammation is one plausible readout, though fisetin's effect on it in humans is not yet confirmed.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then a few weeks after a dosing cycle; interpret trends, not single values.

biomarkers.why: A core SASP cytokine; elevated levels track with inflammaging. Useful as a research-oriented marker rather than a proven fisetin endpoint.

needs.role: Lipophilic carrier that markedly improves fisetin absorption.

synergy.why: A structurally related flavonoid senolytic; quercetin is the partner in the dasatinib+quercetin senolytic regimen. The two are studied in the same senolytic context, though a specific fisetin+quercetin combination is not independently validated.

contra.advice: Flavonoids can have mild antiplatelet effects; discuss with a clinician before high-dose use if on warfarin, DOACs, or aspirin.

Fisetin can inhibit several CYP enzymes and drug transporters in vitro; high doses may alter levels of co-administered drugs (e.g. certain statins, immunosuppressants). Check interactions with a pharmacist.
No safety data at supplemental/senolytic doses — avoid.
Do not self-add around chemotherapy without oncology guidance; senolytic and antioxidant effects could interact with therapy.

quality.line: Because fisetin is plant-extracted, choose products with a **third-party COA covering purity (≥98%) and heavy metals**; NSF or USP verification is a reasonable neutral quality signal.

nonResponders.line: Senolytic effects are largely **subclinical** — you should not expect to 'feel' anything acutely. Perceived non-response is expected; judge by biomarker trends over time, not sensation.

cost.math: A 2-day cycle at ~1,000-1,500 mg/day ≈ 2-3 g total; standard 100 mg capsules run roughly SGD 0.30-0.60 each, so a cycle is ~20-30 capsules. Bulk powder is cheaper per gram; liposomal forms cost more.
cost.note: Rough Singapore estimate; prices vary widely by form and purity.

form.buy: **Wheat germ extract standardized to a stated spermidine content** (typically ~1-6 mg spermidine per daily serving), third-party tested for label accuracy.
form.avoid: **Unstandardized 'wheat germ' powders** that declare only total mg, not actual spermidine — polyamine content varies widely by source and processing.
form.withFood: Well tolerated **with or without food**; aligning the dose with an overnight/fasted window is sometimes suggested to complement autophagy, but this timing benefit is purely theoretical.
form.bioavailability: **Polyamines are absorbed in the small intestine but circulating levels are tightly homeostatically regulated** — a 40 mg/day trial barely shifted plasma polyamines — and gut bacteria also synthesize them, so status varies with both diet and microbiome.

foodFirst.line: **Wheat germ is the richest common food source** (~20-40 mg spermidine/100g); aged/hard cheese, natto, dried soybeans, mushrooms, and legumes also contribute.
foodFirst.note: Higher dietary polyamine intake (Mediterranean-style pattern) is associated with lower mortality in a few observational cohorts — food is a legitimate first line, though this is association, not a demonstrated causal effect.

cost.math: Wheat germ extract supplements run roughly SGD 30-55/month at label doses of ~1-3 mg spermidine.
cost.note: Plain culinary wheat germ is far cheaper per mg but has less predictable, unlabelled spermidine content.

dosing.cap: ~6 mg/day supplemental spermidine in most trials
dosing.note: Human trials use **~0.9-6 mg spermidine/day**. The largest cognition RCT (SmartAge) used 0.9 mg/day and found **no benefit on memory vs placebo**; a small earlier pilot used 1.2 mg/day. A 2024 trial gave 40 mg/day safely for 28 days. No established upper limit, and higher doses are not shown to be better.

timing.line: No timing is clearly required; some users align the dose with an **overnight fast** to complement the autophagy signal, though this is unproven.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed** — supplemental spermidine simply mirrors normal dietary polyamine intake and is taken continuously.

contra.flag: Active cancer or recent cancer history
contra.advice: **Polyamines support cell proliferation** and are elevated in many tumors; anyone with active or recent cancer should clear supplemental polyamines with their oncologist first.
Most supplements are **wheat germ-derived and may contain gluten**; choose a certified gluten-free or synthetic spermidine source.
No safety data for concentrated doses above normal dietary intake — avoid supplements and rely on food.

quality.line: Look for a **declared spermidine content in mg** (not just total wheat germ mg) plus third-party testing; standardization between products varies substantially.

nonResponders.line: Because **gut bacteria also synthesize spermidine** and plasma levels are tightly regulated, people already running high baseline polyamine status may notice less added effect.

synergy.why: Both are described as triggering **autophagy**; spermidine is often called a fasting mimetic and the downstream mechanisms overlap, though additive human benefit is unproven.
Endurance exercise also induces autophagy, so the combination is plausibly additive — not demonstrated in humans.

form.buy: **Direct Urolithin A** as the finished metabolite (a standardized, high-purity, third-party-tested capsule or softgel), not a pomegranate or ellagic-acid extract that only supplies the precursor.
form.avoid: Products labeled 'urolithin A' that are really **ellagic acid or pomegranate extract** — these only help if your gut bacteria convert them, which about half of people can't.
form.withFood: Take **with a fat-containing meal** — it's lipophilic and absorption improves with dietary fat.
form.bioavailability: Rapidly conjugated (glucuronide/sulfate) after absorption; trials dosed it **once daily** and still saw effects at 500-1000 mg.

dosing.cap: 1000 mg/day (highest daily dose in the multi-month RCTs; well tolerated)
dosing.note: Human RCTs used **500 mg or 1000 mg once daily** for up to 4 months: 500 mg improved mitochondrial gene expression/acylcarnitines and hamstring strength (~12%), 1000 mg lowered CRP. The main trial's primary power-output endpoint was NOT met, so treat muscle efficacy as promising rather than proven.

timing.line: **No strict circadian timing** — once daily with a meal; sustained daily use over months matters far more than time of day.

foodFirst.line: **Pomegranate, walnuts, raspberries, and strawberries** supply the ellagitannin/ellagic-acid precursors your gut can turn into Urolithin A.
foodFirst.note: Conversion depends on specific gut bacteria (*Gordonibacter* and related species); roughly **half of Western populations are non-producers** (metabotype 0), so food alone won't reliably raise Urolithin A.

nonResponders.line: The core reason to supplement the finished molecule: about **half of people can't convert dietary precursors** into Urolithin A, so pomegranate juice or ellagic acid does little for them — direct supplementation bypasses this.

cost.math: ~$60-120 SGD/month at 500 mg/day
cost.note: One of the pricier longevity supplements; generic Urolithin A powder tends to run cheaper than branded softgels but demands stricter third-party purity checks.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed** — RCTs dosed continuously for up to 4 months and benefits accrue with sustained daily use rather than pulsing.

contra.advice: **Avoid** — no safety data in these groups.

quality.line: Choose a product that lists an **actual Urolithin A content in mg** and is third-party tested for purity; be skeptical of 'pomegranate complex' blends that imply UA without stating it.

synergy.why: Both training and Urolithin A independently drive **mitophagy and mitochondrial turnover**; the trial benefits layer onto an active lifestyle rather than replacing it.

form.buy: High-purity **calcium alpha-ketoglutarate (Ca-AKG)** powder or capsules, third-party tested with a COA stating actual Ca-AKG content.
form.avoid: Harsh, strongly acidic **free-acid AKG** and unverified 'longevity blends' that don't disclose the real Ca-AKG dose.
form.withFood: Take **with food** — the salt is acidic and can cause GI upset or reflux on an empty stomach.
form.bioavailability: AKG is **rapidly metabolized and endogenously abundant**, so effective oral doses sit in the gram range; the calcium salt is more stable and palatable than the free acid.

biomarkers.why: This was the **primary and only outcome** in the single human study; it is the mechanistic target (TET-driven methylation reset). Treat as exploratory, not diagnostic.
biomarkers.range: Reported relative to chronological age (e.g. Horvath-type DNAm clocks)
biomarkers.when: Baseline and after 6-12 months; interpret cautiously given high test-retest noise

biomarkers.why: Ca-AKG is **~20% elemental calcium by weight**, so 2 g/day adds ~400 mg calcium; monitor to avoid excess total intake, especially if stacking other calcium sources.
biomarkers.when: If combining with calcium supplements, dairy-heavy diet, or vitamin D/K stack

needs.role: Reductant that keeps the iron centre active in the **2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases** (TET, prolyl hydroxylases) that AKG fuels.
These same dioxygenases are **Fe(II)-dependent**; frank iron deficiency limits their activity.

antagonists.role: The calcium load in Ca-AKG can **reduce absorption of iron and zinc** when co-ingested; separate by a couple of hours.

foodFirst.line: There is **no meaningful dietary source** — AKG is an endogenous TCA-cycle metabolite the body makes.
foodFirst.note: Circulating AKG appears to decline with age and rise with metabolic health/exercise, but this rests largely on non-human and observational data; supplementation is the only way to reach the gram-level doses studied.

cost.math: Generic Ca-AKG powder ~SGD 40-60 per 250 g / ~125 days at 2 g/day
cost.note: Branded epigenetic-longevity blends cost several times more per gram for the same molecule.

dosing.cap: ~3 g/day
dosing.note: The single human study delivered **~1 g/day (women's formulation) to 2 g/day (men's)** of Ca-AKG; common self-use range is 1-3 g/day. No validated per-kg dosing exists.

timing.line: Take **with meals**, often split morning/evening, mainly for GI tolerance — no evidence that timing affects the outcome.

cycling.line: No established cycling protocol; the human study dosed **continuously for ~7 months**. Insufficient data to recommend on/off cycling either way.

contra.advice: Count the **~20% elemental calcium** in Ca-AKG toward your daily total and stay within recommended calcium intake.
Be cautious — the added calcium load may be undesirable; discuss with a clinician.

contra.flag: Chronic kidney disease / impaired renal function
contra.advice: Caution with the calcium and mineral load; get medical guidance before use.
**No safety data** at supplemental doses — avoid.

quality.line: Pick **third-party-tested Ca-AKG** with a COA confirming actual content; the salt is hygroscopic and can clump or degrade, and cheap powders may under-deliver the stated dose.

nonResponders.line: Epigenetic-clock readouts are **noisy**, and the supporting human evidence is a single uncontrolled, open-label study — no measurable biological-age change is expected and tells you little about whether it 'worked'.

synergy.why: Recycles the iron centre of the **2-OG-dependent dioxygenases** AKG drives; mechanistic plausibility only, with no outcome data on the combination.

form.buy: High-purity, third-party-tested **N-acetylcysteine** (capsule or powder) plus free-form **glycine** powder (tastes mildly sweet). Look for USP/NSF-verified single-ingredient products so you control the ratio.
form.avoid: Proprietary 'glutathione booster' blends with undisclosed or sub-clinical amounts of each ingredient.
form.withFood: Take with a meal — the large amino-acid load (several grams each) can cause nausea or loose stools on an empty stomach.
form.bioavailability: Oral NAC bioavailability is low (~4-10%) but is enough to raise intracellular cysteine, the rate-limiting glutathione precursor; glycine is well absorbed. Oral glutathione itself is largely broken down, which is why the two precursors are used instead.

biomarkers.why: GlyNAC trials in older adults reported improved insulin resistance; track glycaemic control as a downstream signal.
Reduced systemic inflammation was one of the more consistent findings across the pilot and RCT.
Endothelial function improved in the Baylor older-adult trials; BP is a cheap cardiovascular signal to self-monitor at home.
The amino-acid load is metabolised by the liver; confirm liver enzymes stay in range.
The direct mechanistic target — restoring depleted glutathione is the whole rationale. Not offered by every lab, so treat as optional.
biomarkers.range: Lab-specific; aim to move a low baseline toward the young-adult reference
biomarkers.when: Baseline and 12+ weeks, if your lab offers it

needs.role: Cofactor for glutathione peroxidase — the enzyme that uses glutathione to neutralise peroxides. Adequate selenium lets restocked glutathione actually do its job.
Feeds glutathione reductase (via FAD) which recycles oxidised glutathione back to its active form.
Helps regenerate glutathione from its oxidised state, supporting the redox cycle.

foodFirst.line: **Glycine** occurs in collagen/gelatin (bone broth, skin-on cuts) and NAC's precursor cysteine is high in eggs and whey.
foodFirst.note: Food cannot realistically deliver the multi-gram trial doses of either amino acid, so diet supports but does not replace supplementation here.

cost.math: Bulk glycine powder is very cheap (~$0.10-0.20 for several grams); NAC adds ~$0.40-0.80/day at trial-scale doses.
cost.note: One of the cheaper longevity interventions per gram; buying single-ingredient powders is far cheaper than pre-mixed blends.

dosing.unit: mg/kg/day glycine; NAC dosed higher (~130 mg/kg/day)
dosing.cap: Trials used glycine ~100 mg/kg/day and NAC ~130 mg/kg/day; many people start lower (e.g. glycine 3-5 g + NAC 600-1200 mg/day) and titrate up for tolerance.
dosing.note: The Baylor trials dosed glycine at 1.33 mmol/kg/day (~100 mg/kg, ~7 g for a 70 kg adult) and NAC at 0.81 mmol/kg/day (~130 mg/kg, ~9 g), split across 2-3 servings. The high NAC dose is where GI side effects appear, so ramp up gradually.

timing.line: Split the daily amount into **2-3 divided doses with meals** to cut nausea and smooth amino-acid delivery; there is no strong evidence favouring a specific time of day.

cycling.line: Trials ran continuously for 16-24 weeks with benefits accruing over time, and declining after stopping — there is no established need to cycle. Some users pause NAC periodically, but this is preference, not evidence-based.

contra.advice: NAC can potentiate nitrate vasodilation and cause marked hypotension and headache — avoid the combination unless supervised.

contra.flag: Anticoagulant / antiplatelet therapy or bleeding disorder
contra.advice: NAC has mild antiplatelet activity; discuss with your doctor before combining with blood thinners.
NAC has rarely triggered bronchospasm (mostly with inhaled/IV forms); introduce cautiously and stop if wheezing occurs.
This delivers several grams of amino acids daily; clear it with your physician if renal function is impaired.
High-dose data are lacking — do not self-experiment during pregnancy without medical guidance.

quality.line: Choose single-ingredient **NAC and glycine verified by NSF or USP** (or an equivalent third-party assay) — amino-acid powders can carry heavy-metal or purity concerns from cheap manufacturing.

nonResponders.line: Benefits are clearest in **older or glutathione-depleted people** with elevated oxidative stress; young, healthy individuals with already-normal glutathione may notice little, since you cannot 'over-restock' a full system.

synergy.why: GlyNAC trials paired supplementation with maintained activity; both target mitochondrial function and muscle, and the effects appear complementary.
Support the enzymes that use and recycle the glutathione you're restocking, so the antioxidant cycle runs efficiently.
Another sulphur-related amino acid studied for age-related decline; commonly stacked in longevity protocols (evidence still emerging).

form.buy: **Glucoraphanin standardised to sulforaphane yield**, ideally co-formulated with **active myrosinase** — the enzyme that converts the inert precursor into actual sulforaphane. Third-party tested for stated content.
form.avoid: Supplements listing only **glucoraphanin** with no active myrosinase and no verified sulforaphane yield — the enzyme is heat-inactivated during most processing, so conversion depends entirely on your gut bacteria and can be near-zero.
form.withFood: Food status matters little; if taking a myrosinase-free product, co-ingest a pinch of raw mustard seed powder to supply the enzyme.
form.bioavailability: Real absorbed dose is highly variable. Controlled trials show adding **active myrosinase roughly doubles** sulforaphane absorption versus precursor alone (~40% vs ~19%); glucoraphanin-only capsules with no live enzyme can yield far less than fresh sprouts.

biomarkers.why: **Broccoli sprout extract lowered fasting glucose** in the obese, dysregulated subgroup of a randomised type-2 diabetes trial (via NRF2-linked suppression of hepatic glucose output); a later prediabetes RCT showed only a small, microbiota-dependent effect, so treat this as a modest, worth-tracking signal.
NRF2 activation supports hepatic detox/antioxidant capacity; **modest ALT reductions** have been seen in a small RCT of adults with high-normal liver enzymes. An emerging, non-specific readout.

needs.role: Enzyme that hydrolyses inert **glucoraphanin into active sulforaphane**; destroyed by heat, so cooked broccoli or enzyme-free capsules yield little without a raw source (sprouts, mustard powder) or capable gut bacteria.

antagonists.role: Breakdown products of raw cruciferous glucosinolates can compete with **thyroid iodine uptake**; clinically relevant mainly at very high intakes or with iodine deficiency, so keep iodine adequate if consuming large daily sprout quantities.

foodFirst.line: **Fresh broccoli sprouts** (3-5 days old) are the richest natural source, carrying many times the glucoraphanin of mature broccoli plus their own intact myrosinase.
foodFirst.note: Home-sprouting broccoli seeds is cheap and gives a live-enzyme source no capsule matches; brief light steaming of mature broccoli preserves more myrosinase than boiling, which fully inactivates it.

cost.perDose: ~$0.30-0.80 SGD per capsule dose; sprouting seeds far cheaper per serve
cost.math: A pack of broccoli sprouting seeds (~$10-20 SGD) yields many trays of sprouts over months, dropping cost per serving well below capsules.
cost.note: Rough Singapore ranges; capsule cost varies widely with standardisation and whether active myrosinase is included.

dosing.note: Human trials commonly use broccoli-sprout preparations delivering roughly **glucoraphanin equivalent to ~10-40 mg sulforaphane per day**, or ~50-100 g fresh sprouts. Because conversion is so variable, the yield of active sulforaphane matters more than the milligrams of precursor on the label. No per-kg standard exists.
dosing.cap: No established safe upper limit; food-level intakes are well tolerated, concentrated extracts less studied long-term.

timing.line: **Once daily, any time** works — sulforaphane clears within a few hours but the NRF2 antioxidant gene program it switches on stays elevated for roughly a day or more.

cycling.line: **No cycling required**; daily intake was well tolerated across weeks-to-months trials with no clear tolerance to its NRF2 effect.

contra.advice: Large daily raw-cruciferous intake has mild goitrogenic potential; if you have thyroid disease or low iodine, keep iodine adequate and discuss high-dose use with your clinician.
Culinary amounts are fine, but **concentrated extracts are under-studied** in pregnancy — stick to food-level intake unless advised otherwise.

quality.line: Choose products **standardised to and tested for actual sulforaphane yield** (not just glucoraphanin), ideally stating active myrosinase content, from a maker doing third-party purity/potency testing.

nonResponders.line: With enzyme-free precursor products, active sulforaphane depends on **gut bacteria that carry myrosinase-like activity** — low-converter microbiomes (or recent antibiotics) produce little, which co-ingested mustard powder or sprouts can rescue.

synergy.why: Adding a raw myrosinase source to cooked broccoli or an enzyme-free capsule **restores conversion of glucoraphanin to sulforaphane**, raising the absorbed dose.

form.buy: **Natural algae-derived** astaxanthin from *Haematococcus pluvialis*, supplied as the **esterified form** in an oil suspension (softgel).
form.avoid: **Synthetic astaxanthin** (petrochemical-derived, sold mostly for aquaculture) — different stereoisomer profile and less human safety/efficacy data.
form.withFood: Fat-soluble — always take with a **meal containing dietary fat** to meaningfully raise absorption.
form.bioavailability: Absorption is low and highly fat-dependent; co-ingested fat can multiply plasma uptake several-fold versus a fasted dose.

biomarkers.why: Astaxanthin has modest anti-inflammatory signals in some trials; a cheap, standard way to watch systemic inflammation over months.
A core mechanistic claim is lipid-membrane antioxidant protection; oxLDL is the most directly relevant human oxidative marker, though testing is specialised.
biomarkers.range: Lab-specific; track your own trend, not an absolute cutoff

needs.role: Required carrier for intestinal absorption of this lipophilic carotenoid — a fatty meal is the single biggest lever on how much you actually absorb.

antagonists.role: Compete for the same mixed-micelle absorption pathway; megadosing one fat-soluble carotenoid can blunt uptake of others taken at the same time.

foodFirst.line: **Wild salmon, trout, shrimp, krill and salmon roe** are the natural dietary sources (astaxanthin is what makes them pink-red).
foodFirst.note: Food doses are small — a serving of wild salmon supplies only ~1-5 mg (sockeye is richest, most species less), well below typical supplement doses, so diet complements rather than replaces a supplement here.

cost.math: A 60-count bottle of 12 mg natural astaxanthin runs roughly SGD 20-40, i.e. one to two months at one/day.
cost.note: Rough Singapore retail/online range; higher-mg or premium esterified products sit at the top end.

dosing.cap: Consumer use commonly 4-12 mg/day; trials have gone to ~20-40 mg short-term. No established per-kg dosing.
dosing.note: Benefits plateau — more is not linearly better. Start at 4-6 mg with food and give it 6-8 weeks, as antioxidant/skin effects accrue slowly.

timing.line: Take with the **largest fat-containing meal** of the day; time of day doesn't matter, absorption does.

cycling.line: No cycling needed — it's a dietary carotenoid with no tolerance or dependence; daily continuous use is the studied pattern.

contra.advice: Astaxanthin may modestly lower blood pressure; watch for additive effects and discuss with your doctor.
Theoretical additive effect on bleeding tendency; flag it to your prescriber before adding.
Insufficient safety data at supplemental doses — default to food sources only unless a clinician advises otherwise.
Krill- or algae-source products can matter if you have relevant allergies — check the source.

quality.line: Choose **natural *Haematococcus pluvialis*-derived**, esterified astaxanthin with a stated mg of actual astaxanthin (not total oil), ideally **third-party tested** for content and oxidation; oil-suspension softgels protect the pigment from degradation.

nonResponders.line: People with **fat malabsorption** (very low-fat diet, bile/pancreatic issues, some GI conditions) or who dose it fasted may absorb little and see nothing — fix the fat pairing first.

synergy.why: Both are lipophilic and incorporate into cell membranes; taken together the fish oil also supplies the fat that drives astaxanthin absorption.
Complementary lipid-phase antioxidants; classic membrane antioxidant network where one helps regenerate/spare the other.
Often stacked for skin and eye endpoints as a broader carotenoid approach — take spaced or with ample fat to limit absorption competition.

form.buy: Sold only as **lyophilized (freeze-dried) 'research-use-only' powder**, reconstituted with bacteriostatic water for subcutaneous injection; no pharmaceutical-grade product exists.
form.avoid: Avoid **oral and nasal 'epitalon' products** (a short peptide destroyed by gut proteases, so these are effectively inactive) and any injectable vial lacking a third-party certificate of analysis.
form.bioavailability: As a short peptide it is **degraded by digestive enzymes**, so only injected routes deliver intact peptide; this is a core reason the evidence base is thin and route-dependent.

quality.line: This is a **gray-market peptide with zero regulatory oversight** — mislabeling, under-dosing, and contamination are common; only consider vials with a batch-specific third-party COA showing high purity and low endotoxin, and recognize sterility is still unverifiable at home.

dosing.note: Biohacker protocols commonly cite **5-10 mg subcutaneous per day**, but this is copied from old unreplicated single-group studies, not a validated dose; there is no established safe or effective amount.

cycling.line: Almost always run as **short courses of ~10-20 days, once or twice a year** rather than continuously — but note this schedule comes from convention, not from any evidence showing it is safer or more effective.

foodFirst.line: No nutrient reproduces this peptide, but the **evidence-backed longevity levers — sleep, resistance and aerobic exercise, not smoking, and caloric moderation — have vastly stronger data** and should be maxed out before an unproven injectable.
foodFirst.note: Spending on validated basics returns more healthspan per dollar than a gray-market peptide with no replicated human outcomes.

contra.advice: A **core marketed mechanism is telomerase activation**, which is theoretically concerning for helping tumor cells evade senescence (cell-line work has shown telomerase upregulation in cancer lines); avoid and discuss any peptide use with an oncologist.
**No safety data exist** in these groups; do not use.
Unverified sterility and purity carry real **infection, abscess, and contaminant risk**; understand you cannot confirm what is in the vial.

form.buy: Sold OTC as **lithium orotate** (or aspartate); read the label for **elemental lithium** content, usually 1-5 mg, not the total salt weight.
form.avoid: Do not confuse OTC micro-dose lithium with prescription **lithium carbonate**, which delivers roughly ~55-340 mg elemental daily and requires blood monitoring.
form.withFood: Take with food or a full glass of water to blunt mild nausea.
form.bioavailability: Lithium salts are well absorbed; claims that **orotate** targets the brain better than carbonate are marketing, not shown in humans.

biomarkers.why: At 1-5 mg elemental, levels usually sit far below the assay's limit; a reading approaching the **therapeutic 0.6-1.2 mmol/L range means the dose is far too high**.
biomarkers.when: only if dosing above ~10 mg elemental or symptomatic

biomarkers.why: Therapeutic lithium suppresses thyroid output; micro-dose risk is low and routine monitoring is unproven at this dose, but a **baseline TSH** is a reasonable precaution on chronic use.
biomarkers.when: baseline, then periodically on chronic use

biomarkers.why: Lithium is cleared by the kidney and is nephrotoxic at therapeutic doses; confirm **baseline renal function** before long-term use.

antagonists.role: Low sodium intake or dehydration slows lithium excretion and **raises blood levels** - stay hydrated and avoid crash-cutting salt.

antagonists.nutrient: NSAIDs, thiazide diuretics, ACE inhibitors / ARBs
antagonists.role: All **raise serum lithium** by cutting renal excretion - a genuine interaction, most relevant as the dose climbs.
Increases renal lithium clearance, modestly lowering levels.

foodFirst.line: Lithium is a trace mineral in **drinking water, whole grains, and vegetables**; the longevity signal comes from populations with naturally lithium-rich water at microgram intakes, far below any supplement.
foodFirst.note: Some natural mineral waters carry meaningful lithium, a food-first route, though content varies widely and is rarely labeled.

cost.math: roughly SGD 15-30 for a 100-200 capsule bottle
cost.note: One of the cheapest supplements per day, though OTC availability in Singapore is inconsistent.

dosing.cap: keep to single-digit mg elemental unless clinically supervised
dosing.note: OTC micro-dosing is typically **1-5 mg elemental daily**; water-epidemiology intakes are far lower still. There is no shown benefit to pushing higher, and doses above ~10-20 mg approach ranges that warrant blood monitoring.

timing.line: Timing is not critical; take with a meal. If it feels mildly sedating, shift it to the **evening**.

cycling.line: No established cycling protocol; the hypothesized benefits are chronic and preventive, so continuous low dosing is the norm rather than pulsing.

contra.advice: Impaired renal clearance concentrates lithium - do not self-dose; involve a clinician.

contra.flag: On prescription lithium or a diagnosed mood disorder
contra.advice: Do not self-supplement or stack - dosing must be managed by your prescriber.
Therapeutic lithium is linked to fetal cardiac defects and micro-dose safety is unstudied - avoid.
Lithium can further suppress thyroid function; monitor TSH or avoid.

contra.flag: Taking NSAIDs, thiazides, or ACE inhibitors / ARBs
contra.advice: These raise lithium levels; be cautious and avoid combining with higher supplemental doses.

quality.line: Choose a product that states **elemental lithium** clearly and is third-party tested (USP or NSF) for label accuracy and heavy-metal purity.

nonResponders.line: There is **no acute felt effect** - any benefit is slow and preventive, so 'nothing happening' is expected and is not a reason to escalate the dose.

form.buy: **High-purity powder or capsules with a batch Certificate of Analysis (COA)**; racetams are grey-market in many countries, so third-party identity/purity testing matters more than usual.
form.avoid: **Unverified bulk powder** sold with no COA or lot testing — adulteration and mislabelled potency are the main real-world risks.
form.withFood: **Aniracetam is lipophilic** — take it with a meal containing fat for meaningful absorption; piracetam and oxiracetam are water-soluble and can be taken with or without food.
form.bioavailability: **Piracetam is nearly 100% orally bioavailable** and excreted largely unchanged; aniracetam is rapidly and extensively metabolised (short plasma half-life), so its effects are brief and dosing is often split.

needs.role: Racetams raise acetylcholine turnover; **adequate choline is the substrate** the mechanism draws on.

depletes.role: The classic **'racetam headache'** is commonly (though anecdotally) attributed to accelerated acetylcholine use outrunning choline supply.

foodFirst.line: There is **no dietary source of the racetam molecule itself** — but its required cofactor, choline, is food-first: eggs, liver, and soy are dense sources before you reach for a choline supplement.
foodFirst.note: Fixing baseline dietary choline first often resolves headaches and non-response without adding another pill.

dosing.cap: Phenylpiracetam is potent at 100-200 mg; tolerance builds quickly, so it is not a daily-forever compound.
dosing.note: Typical self-reported ranges by compound: **piracetam 1.2-4.8 g/day (usually split)**, aniracetam 750-1500 mg/day, oxiracetam 800-2400 mg/day, phenylpiracetam 100-200 mg used intermittently. Per-kg dosing is not standard for racetams.

timing.line: **Split piracetam/oxiracetam** across the day; take stimulating phenylpiracetam and short-acting aniracetam **earlier** — phenylpiracetam late in the day commonly disrupts sleep.

cycling.line: **Phenylpiracetam tolerance is reported to develop fast** (within days to weeks), so it is best used intermittently/cycled; piracetam and oxiracetam are reported to show far less tachyphylaxis and are used more continuously.

contra.advice: **Piracetam is cleared almost entirely by the kidneys** (>98% excreted unchanged) and requires dose reduction or avoidance in reduced renal function — check with a clinician.
Piracetam has **antiplatelet/haemorheological activity**; use caution with anticoagulants/antiplatelets and pause before surgery.
**Avoid** — safety data are insufficient.
Racetams are **not approved as dietary supplements** and are prescription-only or unapproved in many jurisdictions (including the US) — check your local legal status before purchasing.

quality.line: Because racetams are grey-market, insist on a **third-party Certificate of Analysis** for identity and purity; avoid anonymous powders with no batch testing.

nonResponders.line: Effects (especially piracetam) are often **subtle rather than stimulating**; some people feel nothing even with a choline source and are genuine non-responders — aniracetam and phenylpiracetam tend to be more noticeable.

synergy.why: Supplies the acetylcholine substrate the racetam draws on and is **anecdotally reported to reduce the racetam headache** — the most consistently recommended pairing.

form.buy: **High-purity powder or capsules** with a batch certificate of analysis (COA) — noopept is largely sold on the loosely-regulated research-chemical market, so third-party purity/identity testing matters more than for mainstream supplements.
form.avoid: Unlabelled bulk powder with **no COA and no accurate scale/scoop** — the active dose is tiny (10-30 mg) and eyeballing an unverified powder invites large dosing errors.
form.withFood: Anecdotally taken **sublingually or with a little fat** for a smoother onset, though human absorption data are limited and this is largely user practice.
form.bioavailability: Rapidly absorbed and **metabolised to cycloprolylglycine**, an endogenous neuropeptide thought to carry much of the effect; the parent compound's plasma half-life is short (~15-25 min), with slower, highly variable elimination reported in humans.

foodFirst.line: The BDNF/NGF upregulation noopept is prized for is driven **far more reliably by aerobic/resistance exercise and adequate deep sleep** — secure those first, since they raise the same growth factors with established long-term benefit.
foodFirst.note: Noopept's BDNF/NGF effect is mostly animal data; no supplement matches the growth-factor response of regular training. Treat it as an optional add-on, not a substitute.

cost.math: A 3-5 g batch at ~$20-35 SGD yields 100-300+ doses at 10-30 mg each (rough estimate).
cost.note: Cost is trivial; the real 'price' is uncertain sourcing quality and thin long-term safety data.

dosing.cap: Typically 30 mg/day; some protocols go to 40 mg, but there is no reason to exceed 30 — more is not better.
dosing.note: Common range is **10-30 mg/day**, often split into 1-2 doses. Start at 10 mg to gauge response. Not dosed per body weight.

timing.line: Take **earlier in the day** (e.g. morning and early afternoon if splitting); some users report late doses feel mildly stimulating or disrupt sleep.

cycling.line: Many users **cycle it** (e.g. a few weeks on, a short break, or intermittent use) as a precaution, since long-term continuous-use data in healthy people are lacking; note animal studies actually show no tolerance over ~28 days.

contra.advice: Avoid — no safety data in these populations.
Noopept is a **prescription drug in Russia and is not an approved supplement or medicine in many countries**. Check local legality before importing; it is not a sanctioned OTC product.
Human trials are small, short, and mostly from one research lineage; long-term safety in healthy people is essentially unstudied. Do not treat as established-safe.
If on antidepressants, stimulants, anticonvulsants or other neuroactive drugs, discuss with a clinician — interaction data are minimal.

quality.line: Because it trades on the research-chemical market, insist on a **batch COA showing identity and purity**; discard anything with off colour, clumping, or no verifiable testing.

nonResponders.line: Effects are **subtle and inconsistent** — a substantial fraction of users notice little to nothing. If nothing changes after a short trial at an adequate dose, stop rather than escalating.

synergy.with: A choline source (e.g. alpha-GPC or CDP-choline)
synergy.why: A **choline source** is often paired with nootropics of this class, mainly by extrapolation from racetam practice, to support acetylcholine supply and possibly ease the mild headaches some users report; noopept's mechanism is largely neurotrophic rather than cholinergic, so the benefit is uncertain. Dietary choline (eggs) works too.

needs.role: Adequate choline may support the cholinergic system and reduce headache in some users, though evidence specific to noopept is weak and borrowed from racetam use.

form.buy: **Citicoline (CDP-choline)** as a defined, high-purity, third-party-tested powder or capsule. It is a single characterised molecule, so verify the label actually says citicoline/CDP-choline.
form.avoid: Do not confuse it with cheaper **choline bitartrate** or plain **choline salts** — these raise choline but do not supply the cytidine/uridine portion that distinguishes citicoline. Alpha-GPC is a related but different choline donor.
form.withFood: Water-soluble and stable; can be taken **with or without food**. No fat needed for absorption.
form.bioavailability: Oral **bioavailability is high (~90%)**; it is hydrolysed to cytidine and choline, absorbed, and these precursors are re-used to resynthesise citicoline in tissues.

needs.role: Precursor of **acetyl-CoA**, which combines with choline to form acetylcholine — the downstream neurotransmitter citicoline feeds.
Support **one-carbon/methylation metabolism** that choline participates in as a methyl donor via the betaine pathway.

antagonists.role: Medications with anticholinergic action (some antihistamines, tricyclics, bladder drugs) **oppose the cholinergic pathway** citicoline supports.

foodFirst.line: **Dietary choline** (eggs, liver, soybeans, fish) covers baseline choline needs and most people under-consume it.
foodFirst.note: Food choline does not supply the cytidine/uridine fraction of citicoline, so the two are not fully interchangeable — but fixing a poor choline intake first is cheaper and higher-yield.

cost.math: A 250mg x 60-capsule bottle typically runs ~SGD 20-35, i.e. ~SGD 0.35-0.60 per 250mg; a 500mg dose is roughly double.
cost.note: Rough Singapore/online (iHerb-style) pricing; varies by supplier and dose size.

dosing.cap: 2000 mg/day
dosing.note: General cognitive use is typically **250-500 mg/day**, taken once or split. Clinical studies have used 500-2000 mg/day; there is no need to exceed the low end for general use. No per-kg dosing is standard.

timing.line: Take in the **morning or midday**; some people find it mildly alerting, so avoid dosing late in the evening. Splitting a 500mg dose AM/early-afternoon is reasonable.

cycling.line: **No established need to cycle** — it is well tolerated for continuous use. Cycling off periodically is only useful to check whether you still notice a benefit.

contra.advice: Headache, nausea, GI upset, jaw tension or insomnia usually signal **too much cholinergic tone** — lower the dose or take it earlier.
Insufficient safety data for supplemental doses — **avoid** unless directed by a clinician.
Compounds affecting cholinergic/neurotransmitter tone warrant caution; discuss with your prescriber before starting.

quality.line: Choose products that are **third-party tested** (USP/NSF where available) and clearly specify citicoline/CDP-choline content, not a generic 'choline complex'.

nonResponders.line: People already at **good choline status** (high-egg/liver diets) may notice little, and genetic differences in choline metabolism (e.g. PEMT variants) can blunt subjective effects.

synergy.why: Citicoline supplies cytidine→**uridine** and choline; combined with DHA these are the building blocks for **phosphatidylcholine membrane synthesis** (the classic phosphatide-precursor stack).
Racetams increase cholinergic demand; citicoline provides **choline substrate**, which is theorised to reduce racetam-associated headaches — largely mechanistic/anecdotal.

form.buy: **High-purity (≥99%) alpha-GPC**, third-party tested for identity and heavy metals; capsules are more practical than loose powder.
form.avoid: **Choline bitartrate or citrate** if the goal is brain effect — they raise plasma choline but reach the brain less efficiently than alpha-GPC.
form.withFood: Works **with or without food**; the powder is intensely hygroscopic (pulls water from air and clumps), so keep it sealed with desiccant.
form.bioavailability: **~40% choline by weight** and well absorbed — 600 mg alpha-GPC delivers roughly 240 mg choline that readily reaches the brain.

needs.role: Precursor to coenzyme A, which supplies the **acetyl group** that combines with choline to form acetylcholine — the point of taking alpha-GPC.

antagonists.role: **Block muscarinic receptors** and blunt the cholinergic effect you are supplementing for.

foodFirst.line: There is no dietary source of alpha-GPC itself, but **egg yolks and liver** are the richest whole-food choline sources — a couple of eggs supplies a meaningful base before any capsule.
foodFirst.note: Food choline is not as brain-targeted as alpha-GPC, but covering baseline intake first is cheaper and lowers how much supplement you need.

cost.math: 60 x 300 mg capsules typically run ~S$25–45 in Singapore, so a single dose lands under a dollar.
cost.note: Mid-priced among choline sources — more than choline bitartrate, less than most branded nootropic blends.

dosing.cap: 1200 mg/day
dosing.note: **300–600 mg** is the common cognitive dose; clinical trials in cognitive decline used up to 1200 mg/day in divided doses. The pre-workout growth-hormone/strength study used a single **600 mg** dose. No per-kg standard exists — do not scale by bodyweight.

timing.line: Take in the **morning or early afternoon** for cognitive use; for the pre-workout power/growth-hormone effect, the trial protocol dosed a single 600 mg **~60–90 minutes before training**.

contra.advice: A large retrospective cohort linked regular alpha-GPC use to **higher 10-year incident stroke risk** (dose-dependent), and choline is converted by gut bacteria to TMAO (a cardiovascular-risk marker). Observational, not proven causal — be cautious with high chronic doses if you have vascular risk.
Excess cholinergic tone is associated with **low mood in sensitive people**; if you notice flat affect or irritability, reduce or stop.
Too much causes **headache, nausea, jaw tension, or GI upset** — signs to lower the dose rather than push through.

quality.line: Because it is strongly **hygroscopic**, poorly packaged product clumps and can degrade — favour sealed capsules or foil-sealed powder with a certificate of analysis (USP/NSF-style third-party testing).

nonResponders.line: A minority feel **foggy, low, or irritable** on choline loading rather than sharper — often a sign of cholinergic sensitivity; these people usually do better on a lower dose or not at all.

synergy.why: Racetams increase acetylcholine turnover; alpha-GPC supplies the **choline substrate** and is anecdotally reported to reduce racetam-induced headaches.
Together they feed the **Kennedy pathway** for synaptic membrane (phosphatidylcholine) synthesis — a membrane-support rationale.
Complementary pre-workout/focus pairing — stimulant drive plus cholinergic substrate; keep total stimulant load modest.
Provides the acetyl-CoA needed to actually **convert the choline into acetylcholine**.

form.buy: **Fruiting-body extract**, hot-water or dual-extracted (water + alcohol), standardized to **beta-glucans (aim >25%)**.
form.avoid: **Mycelium-on-grain** products (labelled 'myceliated brown rice', 'full-spectrum mycelium') — these are largely grain starch with low active content.
form.withFood: Take with a meal containing some **fat** — the hericenones (fat-soluble aromatic compounds from the fruiting body) are lipophilic.
form.bioavailability: **Dual extraction** captures both water-soluble beta-glucans and the more lipophilic hericenones; a single hot-water extract can miss the fat-soluble fraction.

foodFirst.line: Lion's Mane is a genuine **culinary mushroom** — the fresh fruiting body is edible (seafood-like texture) and sold at some grocers and farmers' markets.
foodFirst.note: Study doses (~1-3g/day of concentrated dried extract) far exceed what a normal culinary portion delivers, so eating it is food, not a therapeutic dose.

cost.math: A 60g tub of standardised fruiting-body extract runs ~SGD 30-60; at 1g/day that is roughly SGD 15-30/month.
cost.note: Mycelium-on-grain products are often cheaper per gram but deliver far less active compound, so the effective cost is higher.

dosing.note: Common range is **500-1000mg extract, 1-3x daily**. The best-known human trial (Mori 2009) used ~3g/day of dried fruiting body. No validated per-kg dosing exists — dose by product, not bodyweight.

timing.line: Take in the **morning or midday**; effects are chronic (NGF upregulation over weeks), not acute. Some people report **vivid dreams** or lighter sleep when dosing late, so avoid right before bed if sensitive.

cycling.line: No cycling required — benefits **build over 8-16 weeks** and fade within a few weeks of stopping (cognitive gains reversed after a 4-week washout in the Mori 2009 trial), so consistency matters more than washouts.

contra.advice: Avoid entirely. Case reports include contact dermatitis and, from inhaled spores, respiratory reactions.

contra.flag: Anticoagulant / antiplatelet use or upcoming surgery
contra.advice: Lion's Mane may have mild antiplatelet activity — discuss with your doctor and consider pausing before procedures.
May modestly lower blood glucose; monitor if you are already on glucose-lowering therapy.
