biomarkers.why: Injectable, non-17-alpha-alkylated steroids are less hepatotoxic than oral ones, but transaminases are still checked to separate steroid effect from muscle breakdown.

contra.flag: Women, and anyone pregnant or who may become pregnant
contra.advice: Androgens cause virilization (deepened voice, facial hair, clitoral enlargement) that is often permanent, and are toxic to a developing fetus.

contra.flag: Hormone-sensitive cancers (prostate or male breast cancer)
contra.advice: Androgens can accelerate the growth of androgen-dependent tumors and are absolutely contraindicated.
Boldenone's erythropoietic drive would push blood viscosity into an immediately dangerous, clot-prone range.

contra.flag: Cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, or prior clot/stroke/heart attack
contra.advice: The combination of raised hematocrit, lowered HDL and cardiac remodeling multiplies the risk of thrombosis and cardiac events.

contra.flag: Adolescents and anyone with open growth plates
contra.advice: Androgens can prematurely fuse epiphyses and permanently stunt growth.
Steroid-driven HDL suppression and hepatic strain worsen these conditions.

oxymetholone-anadrol-drostanolone-master.accessNote: All three are Schedule III controlled anabolic steroids. Only oxymetholone retains a recognized medical use (treating certain anemias); drostanolone (formerly used in breast cancer) and methenolone have little to no approved human use in most markets. Using any of them to gain size or "harden" falls entirely outside legitimate medical use.

overdose.line: There is no acute antidote and harm accrues with dose and duration rather than a single lethal event. High doses of the oral 17-alpha-alkylated agent (oxymetholone) can trigger cholestatic liver injury with jaundice, dark urine and right-upper-quadrant pain, and rare peliosis hepatis (blood-filled liver cysts) that can rupture and bleed. A rapid rise in red-cell mass raises blood viscosity and can precipitate stroke, heart attack or venous clots; severe hypertension, fluid overload, and mood disturbance (aggression, mania, psychosis) are also documented. Effects reverse slowly if at all after stopping.

misuse.line: No self-administered dose is validated as safe: these are unlicensed for physique use, sourced from unregulated underground supply of uncertain identity, purity and concentration. Documented harms of non-medical use include hepatotoxicity (worst with oral oxymetholone), a sharp drop in HDL with rising LDL that accelerates atherosclerosis, left-ventricular hypertrophy and cardiomyopathy, hypertension, polycythemia with clot risk, and suppression of the body's own testosterone/LH/FSH that can cause testicular shrinkage, infertility and mood collapse and may not fully recover. Stacking multiple agents, as is common, multiplies these risks rather than balancing them.

biomarkers.range: ALT ~7-56 U/L; flag sustained elevation or rising bilirubin
biomarkers.when: Baseline and periodically during any exposure
biomarkers.why: 17-alpha-alkylated orals (oxymetholone) are directly hepatotoxic, causing cholestasis, peliosis hepatis and hepatic adenomas/carcinoma; transaminase and bilirubin rises are the early lab signal.

biomarkers.range: HDL can fall >50% (often 60-80% with oral 17-alkylated agents) with LDL rising
biomarkers.why: Anabolic steroids, especially oral 17-alkylated ones, suppress HDL and raise LDL, accelerating atherosclerosis and cardiovascular risk.
Androgens stimulate erythropoiesis; a rising red-cell mass increases blood viscosity and the risk of thrombosis, stroke and myocardial infarction.

biomarkers.marker: LH, FSH and endogenous total testosterone
biomarkers.range: LH/FSH suppressed toward undetectable; own testosterone falls
biomarkers.when: Baseline and after cessation to assess recovery
biomarkers.why: Exogenous androgens shut down the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, causing testicular atrophy, low sperm production and infertility that can persist after stopping.

biomarkers.range: Elevated with fluid retention / breast tissue changes
biomarkers.why: Oxymetholone has estrogenic activity despite not aromatizing and can cause gynecomastia and water retention; the DHT-derived agents (drostanolone, methenolone) do not aromatize but co-administered compounds often do.

biomarkers.range: Rising PSA; BP trending into hypertensive range
biomarkers.when: Baseline and periodically, older users especially
biomarkers.why: Androgens stimulate prostate tissue and can raise blood pressure and promote left-ventricular hypertrophy; both are markers of cumulative harm.

contra.advice: Absolutely contraindicated: androgens are teratogenic and virilize a female fetus. Not to be used by anyone who is or may become pregnant.

contra.flag: Known or suspected prostate cancer, or male breast cancer
contra.advice: These cancers are androgen-driven; anabolic steroids can accelerate their growth and must be avoided.

contra.flag: Existing liver disease or abnormal liver enzymes
contra.advice: 17-alpha-alkylated orals such as oxymetholone are hepatotoxic and can precipitate cholestasis, peliosis hepatis and liver tumors; avoid entirely with any hepatic impairment.

contra.flag: Cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension or high cardiac risk
contra.advice: Adverse lipid shifts, raised blood pressure, polycythemia and cardiomyopathy compound existing cardiovascular disease and raise the risk of clot, stroke and heart attack.
Androgens further raise red-cell mass and blood viscosity, increasing thrombotic risk; avoid.
Androgens can cause premature closure of growth plates and permanent stunting, plus irreversible virilization; must not be used.
High risk of irreversible virilization (voice deepening, hair growth, clitoral enlargement, menstrual disruption); these agents are inappropriate for non-medical use in women.

andarine-s4-yk-11-s-23.accessNote: Andarine (S4), YK-11 and S-23 are experimental selective androgen receptor modulators sold as "research chemicals" or "not for human consumption" powders; none has ever completed human clinical trials or been approved by any drug regulator for medical use.

overdose.line: There is no established human toxic dose because none was ever set in trials. Andarine produces dose-dependent vision changes — a yellow-green tint to sight and poor dark adaptation (night blindness) from binding retinal androgen receptors — that worsen as intake rises and typically clear a few days after stopping. Across the SARM class, higher intake and stacking drive acute drug-induced liver injury (jaundice, dark urine, right-upper-quadrant pain, raised ALT/AST and often markedly elevated bilirubin); reported cases include cholestatic injury requiring hospitalisation, with enzymes taking months to normalise. Steeply suppressed testosterone, mood disturbance, and (with S-23, shown in animals) collapse of sperm production are also reported.

misuse.line: There is no safe do-it-yourself dose: these compounds never passed human safety testing, so no threshold for organ harm is known. Independent lab analyses of products sold as SARMs repeatedly find mislabelling — wrong compound, wrong amount, undeclared other drugs or none at all — so a user cannot know what they are taking. Documented harms include acute liver injury, lasting suppression of natural testosterone, adverse lipid shifts, and (Andarine) visual disturbance; all three are also WADA-banned.

biomarkers.range: normal ALT ~7–56 U/L; SARM-associated hepatotoxicity can push these into the hundreds
biomarkers.why: Detects drug-induced liver injury, the most consistently reported serious SARM harm.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if any jaundice, dark urine, nausea or abdominal pain appears

biomarkers.range: normal ~0.1–1.2 mg/dL; cholestatic SARM injury can raise it several-fold
biomarkers.why: Rising bilirubin with jaundice signals cholestatic liver injury, a documented SARM pattern.

biomarkers.range: suppressed well below the normal adult male range (~300–1000 ng/dL total)
biomarkers.why: SARMs strongly suppress the body's own testosterone production.
Shows the degree of hypothalamic-pituitary shutdown driving testosterone suppression.

biomarkers.range: HDL often falls sharply; LDL may rise
biomarkers.why: Androgenic compounds worsen the lipid profile, an adverse cardiovascular signal.

biomarkers.range: can fall toward severe oligospermia/azoospermia (S-23 was studied in animals as a male contraceptive candidate)
biomarkers.why: S-23 in particular suppressed spermatogenesis in animal studies, a signal of possible effects on fertility.

contra.flag: Not approved for anyone — no human use has an established safety basis
contra.advice: Because these compounds never passed human safety testing, no group has a demonstrated safe use; this is general educational information, not a green light for any person.

contra.flag: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, or trying to conceive
contra.advice: Androgenic compounds risk fetal harm and disrupt sperm production; must be avoided by anyone pregnant, nursing, or planning conception.
Virilising effects in women and disruption of ongoing hormonal development in under-25s make exposure especially hazardous.

contra.flag: Existing or prior liver disease / drug-induced liver injury
contra.advice: Given the documented hepatotoxicity of SARMs, anyone with liver disease or a past drug-induced liver injury faces amplified risk.
The HDL-lowering, pro-atherogenic lipid shift adds risk for those with existing heart disease or dyslipidaemia.

contra.flag: Pre-existing eye or vision disorders (Andarine specifically)
contra.advice: Andarine's retinal effects on colour and night vision make it particularly ill-advised where vision is already compromised.
All three are prohibited by WADA and trigger anti-doping sanctions.

cardarine-gw-501516.accessNote: GlaxoSmithKline abandoned it in 2006–2007 after long-term rodent studies showed it caused cancers in multiple organs; it now exists only as a grey-market "endurance" and fat-loss chemical and is banned in sport.

overdose.line: There is no characterised human overdose syndrome — it never reached the trials that would define one. The defining hazard is not acute collapse but cumulative harm: rodents dosed at roughly 3 mg/kg/day and above developed tumours across many organ sites (liver, stomach, bladder, colon, skin, tongue, and others) over long-term dosing, so 'more' or 'longer' pushes carcinogenic risk, not a survivable ceiling. Reported human effects from grey-market use are non-specific — nausea, stomach pain, headache — and give no reliable warning before serious damage.

misuse.line: There is no established safe human dose because no dose was ever proven safe in people — development was stopped precisely because the animal cancer signal was severe. Grey-market vials are unregulated: purity, actual content, and contamination with other research chemicals are unverified, so even a 'known' amount is a guess. It is prohibited at all times under the WADA code (S4), and stacking it with SARMs or stimulants — the common pattern — compounds liver and metabolic strain with no medical oversight.

biomarkers.range: ALT >2x upper limit of normal warrants stopping and medical review
biomarkers.why: Liver stress and injury are among the most consistently reported human concerns with prolonged use, including a documented hepatotoxicity case; rodent liver was a tumour site.
biomarkers.when: If use is disclosed to a clinician, at baseline and periodically

biomarkers.why: Screens for cholestatic or broader hepatobiliary injury alongside transaminases.

biomarkers.range: Track LDL, HDL, triglycerides against baseline
biomarkers.why: The compound alters lipid handling via PPAR-delta; changes are a marker of its metabolic activity, not evidence of safety.

contra.advice: It is not approved for human use anywhere and was discontinued for multi-organ carcinogenicity; there is no population for whom a benefit has been shown to outweigh that risk.

contra.flag: Personal or family history of cancer
contra.advice: Absolutely avoid — the core documented hazard is tumour promotion across multiple organ types.
Avoid entirely — no reproductive safety data exist and PPAR-delta signalling is critical in development.

contra.flag: Existing liver disease or raised transaminases
contra.advice: Avoid — liver injury is among the most reported human effects and the liver was a rodent tumour site.
Prohibited at all times under the WADA code (metabolic modulators, S4); a positive test is among the more common findings in its class.

stenabolic-sr9009.accessNote: A REV-ERB nuclear-receptor agonist that has only ever been tested in rodents and cell lines; it is sold grey-market as an "exercise mimetic" but has never entered a human trial.

overdose.line: There is no established human toxic dose because SR9009 has never undergone human pharmacokinetic or safety testing, so any dose is uncharacterised. Rodent data came from intraperitoneal injection at up to 100 mg/kg — not oral use — and because the compound is barely absorbed by mouth, people chasing an effect tend to escalate blind into an unknown range. Compounding the risk, in vitro work shows SR9009 is roughly 100x more potent at the LXR-alpha receptor than at REV-ERB and produces many effects that persist even when REV-ERB is genetically removed, so overexposure drives uncharacterised off-target signalling on hepatic lipid metabolism and the circadian clock rather than a single predictable poisoning syndrome.

misuse.line: There is no safe do-it-yourself dose: no human study has ever defined an effective or tolerated amount, the parent molecule has very poor oral bioavailability and high metabolic clearance, and grey-market powders and capsules carry no verified identity, purity or content. Because documented effects are substantially REV-ERB-independent and hit LXR-alpha and the circadian machinery, self-experimentation gambles on off-target actions that were never intended or tested in people, with no dose-finding, no monitoring standard, and no antidote.

biomarkers.why: No human hepatic-safety data exist and REV-ERB/LXR pathways sit on hepatic lipid handling, so a clinician assessing anyone who has used it would baseline and recheck liver enzymes for injury.
LXR-alpha agonism — at which SR9009 is far more potent than at REV-ERB — drives hepatic lipogenesis, so lipids are the most plausible place off-target effects would surface.
The marketed rationale is metabolic (glucose and fat handling); any real or off-target metabolic action would be tracked here, and there is no human evidence it is benign.

contra.flag: Everyone — it is not approved for human use
contra.advice: No human has been shown a safe dose, indication or monitoring plan; there is no medical scenario in which taking it is a validated choice.
REV-ERB and LXR pathways govern circadian and lipid biology with no reproductive safety data whatsoever; exposure must be avoided entirely.

contra.flag: Existing liver disease or elevated liver enzymes
contra.advice: Hepatic metabolism and LXR-driven lipogenesis are directly in the firing line and there is no human hepatotoxicity data to reassure, so a compromised liver is at unquantified risk.

contra.flag: Anyone taking medications with narrow therapeutic windows or hepatic metabolism
contra.advice: Its metabolism, transporter and enzyme interactions in humans are entirely uncharacterised, so interaction risk cannot be predicted or managed.

tesamorelin-egrifta.accessNote: It is prescribed to reduce excess visceral abdominal fat in people with HIV-associated lipodystrophy, and requires a prescriber because it stimulates the body's own growth hormone axis.

typicalDose.line: In its approved use a prescriber typically directs 2 mg once daily by subcutaneous injection into the abdomen. This is reference information only, not a recommendation or instruction; the dose, duration, and whether the drug is appropriate at all are decisions only a treating doctor can make after evaluation.

overdose.line: There is limited overdose data, but because tesamorelin drives pituitary growth hormone (and thus IGF-1) release, excess or over-frequent dosing produces growth-hormone-excess effects: fluid retention, peripheral edema, joint pain and stiffness, muscle pain, carpal tunnel-type numbness and tingling, and worsening blood sugar. Sustained IGF-1 elevation carries the acromegaly-associated concerns of the growth hormone class. Seek medical care for severe swelling, breathing trouble, or signs of a hypersensitivity reaction.

misuse.line: Skipping the daily injection lets visceral fat re-accumulate, since the effect reverses when treatment stops; there is no benefit to 'catching up' by doubling a dose, which only raises IGF-1 and fluid-retention risk. It is not a general fat-loss, muscle-building, or anti-aging drug and is misused as one. Self-dosing without IGF-1 and glucose monitoring, or using it despite an undiagnosed tumor or pituitary problem, is the core danger — the prescription framework exists to catch exactly those risks.

biomarkers.range: kept within the age- and sex-specific reference range; persistent elevation above normal prompts dose reduction or stopping
biomarkers.why: Tesamorelin works by raising growth hormone and therefore IGF-1; sustained elevation is the main marker of over-stimulation and drives the growth-hormone-excess risks.

biomarkers.range: fasting glucose roughly 4.0-5.5 mmol/L (70-99 mg/dL); HbA1c below ~5.7%
biomarkers.why: Growth hormone opposes insulin, so the drug can worsen glucose tolerance and unmask or aggravate diabetes.

biomarkers.range: qualitative; clinical relevance assessed if response wanes
biomarkers.why: Some patients develop antibodies to the peptide, which may reduce effect over time.

contra.flag: Disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis (hypopituitarism, pituitary tumor or surgery, prior head irradiation or head trauma)
contra.advice: Contraindicated — tesamorelin acts on the pituitary, so it should not be used when that axis is compromised.

contra.flag: Active malignancy (or, per prescriber judgment, a history of cancer)
contra.advice: Contraindicated in active cancer; growth-hormone/IGF-1 stimulation could promote tumor growth, and any pre-existing cancer must be evaluated and cleared first.
Contraindicated — no benefit in pregnancy and potential fetal harm; treatment should be stopped if pregnancy occurs.

contra.flag: Known hypersensitivity to tesamorelin or mannitol
contra.advice: Do not use; serious hypersensitivity reactions have been reported.

contra.flag: Active proliferative or severe non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy
contra.advice: Avoid — the growth hormone class can worsen retinopathy; eye status should be assessed in people with diabetes.

sermorelin.accessNote: A GHRH(1-29) analog that physicians prescribe to stimulate the pituitary's own pulsatile growth hormone release; the branded product was discontinued in 2008 (for commercial, not safety, reasons), so it is now dispensed through licensed compounding pharmacies and is also sold on the grey market as an unregulated "research peptide."

typicalDose.line: For reference only and always set by a prescriber: adult off-label protocols commonly use roughly 200-300 mcg by subcutaneous injection at bedtime (to mimic the natural nighttime GH pulse), with the dose titrated against IGF-1 levels. This is not a recommendation or a target to self-administer.

overdose.line: Acute overdose is rarely life-threatening but produces marked facial flushing, headache, dizziness, nausea, a metallic taste, warmth/tightness in the chest and injection-site pain; very large boluses can cause transient hypotension and rapid heartbeat. Sustained over-dosing drives excess GH/IGF-1, producing acromegaly-like effects: fluid retention and edema, hand/foot swelling, carpal-tunnel-type numbness and tingling, joint and muscle aches, and worsening insulin resistance with elevated blood sugar. Because it acts through the pituitary with intact negative feedback, true GH excess is harder to reach than with injected GH, but it is still possible with high sustained dosing.

misuse.line: The core danger is bypassing a prescriber: grey-market vials are unregulated, so purity, sterility, dose and even peptide identity are unverified, and improper reconstitution or non-sterile technique risks contamination and infection. Self-dosing without IGF-1 and glucose monitoring, stacking it with GHRPs or GH secretagogues to push levels higher, or continuing despite a contraindication (especially any cancer history) can drive sustained IGF-1 excess and its harms. Skipping the prescribed monitoring means rising blood sugar, fluid retention or an unmasked tumor can go undetected.

biomarkers.range: kept within the age-adjusted reference range (roughly 100-260 ng/mL depending on age/sex), not pushed above it
biomarkers.why: Primary efficacy and safety marker; IGF-1 above the age-appropriate range signals GH excess and its risks
GH/IGF-1 elevation reduces insulin sensitivity and can raise blood sugar
GH-axis stimulation can alter thyroid hormone metabolism and unmask hypothyroidism, though this effect is uncertain at sermorelin's modest GH elevation

contra.flag: Active malignancy or recent cancer history
contra.advice: Contraindicated; IGF-1 is a growth-promoting signal, so use requires oncology input and is generally avoided

contra.flag: Pituitary tumor or other active pituitary/hypothalamic disease
contra.advice: Contraindicated; stimulating the pituitary is unsafe with underlying tumor or structural disease
Not to be used; safety is not established
Correct thyroid status first, as it blunts the GH response and confounds treatment

contra.flag: Known hypersensitivity to sermorelin or GHRH
contra.advice: Do not use

contra.flag: Severe untreated liver or kidney disease
contra.advice: Evaluate and manage before any use, as clearance and IGF-1 production are affected

cjc-1295-dac.accessNote: A research-grade GHRH analog with no FDA or EMA approval for human use; sold only as a "research chemical" of unverified purity, and clinical development was halted at an early phase and never resumed.

overdose.line: Excess dosing drives supraphysiologic growth hormone and IGF-1, producing acromegaly-like effects: fluid retention with swelling of hands, feet and face, carpal-tunnel numbness and tingling, joint and muscle aches, and headaches. Higher exposures can cause flushing, a systemic vasodilatory reaction, raised heart rate and blood pressure, and rising fasting glucose from acute insulin resistance. The ~6-8 day albumin-bound half-life of the DAC form means an excessive dose keeps stimulating GH for over a week and cannot be quickly reversed.

misuse.line: There is no established safe dose because the compound was never approved and has no validated human dosing; grey-market vials vary in purity, concentration and sterility, so the actual amount injected is unknown. Sustained self-administration pushes GH and IGF-1 above physiologic range, and because IGF-1 is a potent mitogen the main documented concerns are insulin resistance progressing toward diabetes, and a theoretical increase in tumour promotion. Stacking with a ghrelin peptide amplifies GH output and these risks. Long-term safety has never been studied.

biomarkers.range: keep within the age/sex-adjusted reference range; supraphysiologic values signal GH excess
biomarkers.why: Primary downstream marker of GH stimulation and the main safety signal for acromegalic and mitogenic risk.
GH stimulation induces insulin resistance; rising fasting glucose is the earliest metabolic warning.
Captures sustained glycemic drift from GH-driven insulin resistance that a single fasting glucose can miss.
Detects developing insulin resistance before fasting glucose rises.

contra.flag: Active cancer or a history of malignancy
contra.advice: IGF-1 is a growth-promoting mitogen, so raising it is contraindicated where it could drive tumour growth.

contra.flag: Active acromegaly or existing GH excess
contra.advice: Further stimulating GH compounds the disease and its cardiovascular and metabolic complications.

contra.flag: Uncontrolled or poorly controlled diabetes / insulin resistance
contra.advice: GH stimulation worsens insulin resistance and can destabilize glucose control.
GH and IGF-1 can promote abnormal retinal vessel growth and worsen sight-threatening disease.
Effects on the fetus or infant are unstudied and the compound is unapproved for any use.

ipamorelin.accessNote: A ghrelin-receptor (GHS-R1a) agonist that reached only Phase II trials for post-surgical ileus before development was abandoned; it exists today only as a grey-market injectable "research" peptide with no pharmaceutical-grade sterility, purity, or dosing oversight.

overdose.line: There is no established human toxic dose. Excess ghrelin-receptor stimulation drives a supraphysiologic growth-hormone pulse, so acute over-exposure shows up as fluid retention and peripheral edema, puffy hands/face, joint aches, and carpal-tunnel-type numbness or tingling from nerve compression, often with headache, facial flushing, sweating and light-headedness. Sustained GH excess antagonises insulin and can push blood sugar up toward insulin resistance and glucose intolerance. Because the product is unregulated, a further and sometimes larger danger is the injection itself: bacterial contamination or endotoxin causing fever, chills and injection-site infection.

misuse.line: There is no safe DIY dose because there is no approved human dose, no quality control, and no long-term human safety data. Grey-market vials are typically unsterile, mislabelled, or under/over-concentrated, and self-reconstitution invites dosing errors and contamination. The core biological risk is sustained elevation of GH and IGF-1: IGF-1 is mitogenic, so it can accelerate growth of occult or existing tumours, and chronic GH excess promotes insulin resistance, glucose intolerance, soft-tissue and possibly cardiac overgrowth, and fluid retention. None of these effects can be safely titrated at home.

biomarkers.why: The main downstream readout of GH-axis stimulation; a level pushed above the age-adjusted reference range signals excess exposure and the mitogenic/insulin-resistance risk that follows.
biomarkers.range: Interpreted against age- and sex-specific reference range; values above it are the concern
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if exposure is ongoing

biomarkers.why: GH excess antagonises insulin and can raise fasting glucose toward or into the impaired/diabetic range.
Captures the sustained insulin-resistance / glucose-intolerance effect of chronic GH-axis stimulation better than a single glucose.
Rises early as GH-driven insulin resistance develops, often before glucose or HbA1c move.
Co-regulated with IGF-1 by GH; helps confirm genuine GH-axis over-stimulation when IGF-1 is borderline.

contra.advice: IGF-1 is a growth factor that can promote proliferation of malignant cells; anyone with active, treated, or suspected cancer must avoid GH-secretagogue peptides entirely.
No safety data exist and the GH/IGF-1 axis is tightly regulated in pregnancy; exposure is contraindicated.

contra.flag: Children and adolescents with open growth plates
contra.advice: Uncontrolled GH/IGF-1 stimulation outside specialist care risks abnormal skeletal and organ growth.
GH excess worsens insulin resistance and can destabilise blood-sugar control.
GH/IGF-1 elevation can drive proliferative retinal changes and worsen existing retinopathy.
GH secretagogues have been linked to worse outcomes in acutely/critically ill patients and should not be used in that setting.

hexarelin-ghrp-2-ghrp-6.accessNote: First-generation ghrelin/GH-secretagogue research peptides sold grey-market with no pharmaceutical quality control, purity assurance, or human safety data.

overdose.line: No human overdose threshold has ever been established. Acute excess typically produces intense hunger, head-rush and facial flushing, tingling or numbness in the hands, water retention and puffiness, headache, and lethargy. Because these peptides push growth-hormone output above physiologic levels, heavy or sustained use can drive acromegaly-like effects (joint pain, soft-tissue and hand/foot swelling, carpal-tunnel symptoms) and worsening insulin resistance with rising blood glucose. GHRP-2 and hexarelin also raise cortisol and prolactin, and hexarelin in particular causes rapid receptor desensitization so escalating the dose stops working while side effects accumulate.

misuse.line: There is no safe do-it-yourself dose because these compounds are not approved for human use and no therapeutic dose has been validated for safety. Product sold as these peptides is unregulated: content, purity, sterility, and actual identity are frequently wrong, and injectable use of non-sterile material risks infection. Stimulating the GH/IGF-1 axis carries documented harms — insulin resistance and higher blood glucose, sodium and water retention, and prolactin elevation that can cause breast tissue growth in men. Raising IGF-1 signaling can also accelerate growth of an occult or existing tumor. Combining with insulin, other GH secretagogues, or anabolic agents compounds the metabolic and cardiovascular risk.

biomarkers.why: Downstream marker of GH output; supraphysiologic levels signal excessive stimulation and raise proliferation risk.
GH excess drives insulin resistance and can push glucose into the impaired or diabetic range.
Captures sustained glucose elevation that fasting glucose can miss.
Detects developing insulin resistance before fasting glucose rises.
GHRP-2 and hexarelin can raise prolactin, contributing to gynecomastia and sexual dysfunction.
These peptides can stimulate ACTH and cortisol release, especially at higher doses.

contra.advice: GH and IGF-1 signaling promote cell proliferation and could accelerate tumor growth; must not be used.
GH-driven insulin resistance worsens glycemic control.
Further GH stimulation is directly harmful.
IGF-1 elevation can worsen retinal neovascularization.
No safety data; the GH/IGF-1 and prolactin effects are inappropriate in this setting.

contra.flag: Children and adolescents with open growth plates
contra.advice: Unsupervised GH-axis stimulation can disturb normal growth and metabolism.

mk-677-ibutamoren.accessNote: An investigational oral ghrelin-receptor agonist that Merck tested for GH deficiency, muscle wasting and frailty but never brought to market; it is sold only as an unregulated research chemical and used off-label in bodybuilding.

overdose.line: There is no established human toxicity threshold, and grey-market products are unregulated for dose and purity. Higher exposure exaggerates the known effects: marked fluid retention with lower-limb edema, sharply raised fasting glucose, lethargy, muscle and joint pain, and elevated blood pressure. In vulnerable people the fluid-loading effect matters most — a clinical trial in elderly hip-fracture patients was halted after congestive heart failure occurred in 6.5% on MK-677 versus 1.7% on placebo, mostly in those over 80 with prior cardiac history.

misuse.line: There is no safe DIY dose because the compound was never approved and self-sourced product is untested for identity, dose and contaminants. The core harm is not acute but chronic: sustained GH and IGF-1 elevation drives insulin resistance and pushes fasting glucose and HbA1c toward prediabetic territory within weeks, causes sodium-and-water retention and raised blood pressure, and carries an unquantified long-term concern that persistently high IGF-1 is mitogenic (a theoretical cancer-promotion risk). Stacking with insulin-affecting or cardiac drugs compounds these dangers.

biomarkers.why: Rises measurably (roughly 5-20 mg/dL / 0.3-1.1 mmol/L) as GH/IGF-1 antagonize insulin; can cross into impaired-fasting-glucose range within weeks.
Reflects sustained glycemic drift; short trials showed movement toward prediabetic levels, though long-term data are limited.
Direct downstream readout of GH secretagogue activity; rose ~70% at 25 mg/day in trials, and supraphysiologic levels are the mitogenic concern.
biomarkers.range: Age/sex-specific reference; elevation above upper limit is the red flag

biomarkers.why: Captures the fall in insulin sensitivity (reduced ~15-34% in studies) that precedes glucose changes.
biomarkers.range: HOMA-IR rising above ~2.0-2.5 suggests insulin resistance

biomarkers.why: Ghrelin-receptor stimulation can produce mild, non-specific increases in these pituitary-axis hormones.

contra.advice: The reliable rise in fasting glucose and fall in insulin sensitivity can worsen glycemic control and precipitate hyperglycemia.

contra.flag: Heart failure or significant cardiac disease (especially elderly)
contra.advice: Sodium and water retention increase cardiac load; a trial in older patients was stopped for excess congestive heart failure.
Sustained supraphysiologic IGF-1 is mitogenic, raising a theoretical risk of promoting tumour growth.
No safety data exist and hormonal manipulation of the GH/IGF-1 axis is not studied in this setting.

contra.flag: Children and adolescents (outside specialist endocrine care)
contra.advice: Manipulating the GH axis during growth carries unstudied developmental risks.

human-growth-hormone-somatropin.accessNote: Approved to treat diagnosed growth hormone deficiency and specific conditions (e.g. Turner syndrome, chronic kidney disease in children, Prader-Willi, short-bowel/AIDS wasting); dispensed and titrated by an endocrinologist, not for physique goals.

typicalDose.line: There is no self-dosing formula. In adult replacement a doctor starts very low and titrates to keep IGF-1 in the age-appropriate range; children are dosed by weight and growth response. Adult daily amounts are typically a small fraction of a milligram, individualized by blood levels, age, sex and side effects — set and adjusted only by the prescriber.

overdose.line: Acute excess can cause hypoglycemia first, then rebound hyperglycemia. Sustained excess produces acromegaly-like effects: fluid retention and swelling, joint and muscle pain, carpal tunnel syndrome, enlargement of hands, feet and facial bones, headaches, high blood pressure, insulin resistance, and over time heart enlargement (cardiomegaly). Some effects of long-term over-dosing do not fully reverse.

misuse.line: Self-injecting without a diagnosed deficiency, chasing muscle or anti-aging effects, or doubling and stacking doses drives IGF-1 into supraphysiologic ranges and commonly causes edema, carpal tunnel, joint pain, new insulin resistance or frank type 2 diabetes, and acromegalic tissue changes. Unregulated/black-market vials carry contamination and mislabeled-potency risk, and there is a biologically plausible concern that chronically elevated IGF-1 could promote existing tumors. Skipping prescribed monitoring means these harms go undetected until advanced.

biomarkers.why: Primary titration target; kept within the age- and sex-adjusted reference range. Values above range signal over-dosing.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, ~6-8 weeks after any dose change, then periodically

biomarkers.why: GH antagonizes insulin; excess causes insulin resistance and can unmask or worsen diabetes.
GH can unmask central hypothyroidism, lowering free T4; thyroid status must be replete for accurate GH titration.
biomarkers.range: Free T4 within lab reference range

biomarkers.why: Monitored as part of the metabolic picture in GH replacement.
In pediatric use, growth response and skeletal maturation guide dosing and timing of stopping.

contra.advice: Growth hormone raises IGF-1, which can promote proliferation; treatment is withheld until malignancy is excluded or in remission and managed by specialists.

contra.flag: Acute critical illness (e.g. major surgery, trauma, respiratory failure, ICU)
contra.advice: High-dose GH increased mortality in critically ill patients in controlled trials; it must not be used in this setting.
GH can worsen retinopathy; it is contraindicated.

contra.flag: Prader-Willi syndrome with severe obesity, uncontrolled sleep apnea, or respiratory impairment
contra.advice: Sudden deaths have been reported; requires sleep and airway assessment and specialist oversight before and during use.

contra.flag: Closed growth plates when the goal is height
contra.advice: GH cannot increase height once epiphyses have fused; using it for that purpose only exposes the person to risk.

contra.flag: Known hypersensitivity to somatropin or excipients
contra.advice: Do not use.

igf-1-lr3.accessNote: It exists only as an unregulated "research use only" peptide — there is no pharmaceutical-grade human product, no approved indication, and no established human dosing standard.

overdose.line: Long R3 IGF-1 resists the IGF binding proteins that normally buffer native IGF-1 and has a much longer half-life, so its insulin-like, glucose-lowering effect is potent and prolonged. Overdose presents as severe hypoglycemia: sweating, tremor, palpitations, intense hunger, blurred vision, confusion and slurred speech, progressing to seizures, loss of consciousness and, untreated, death. Because the effect outlasts a single dose of carbohydrate, hypoglycemia can recur for many hours after it first appears to resolve.

misuse.line: There is no safe DIY dose. The compound is sold as unregulated powder of unverified identity, purity and concentration, so any self-measured amount is a guess. Its long half-life means a miscalculation cannot be quickly reversed, making the resulting hypoglycemia dangerous and difficult to manage. Separately, chronic IGF-1 receptor stimulation is mitogenic — sustained exposure can accelerate the growth of existing or undiagnosed tumours and drive organ and soft-tissue overgrowth (acromegaly-like changes, including cardiac enlargement).

biomarkers.range: Normal 4.0-5.5 mmol/L (70-99 mg/dL); hypoglycemia <3.9 mmol/L (<70 mg/dL), severe <2.8 mmol/L
biomarkers.when: The acute danger; low readings signal the insulin-like effect driving symptomatic hypoglycemia.
biomarkers.why: Detects the primary acute toxicity — profound and prolonged hypoglycemia.

biomarkers.range: Adult reference roughly 100-300 ng/mL, strongly age-dependent (higher in young adults, lower after ~50)
biomarkers.when: Supraphysiologic total IGF-1 activity reflects excess growth-factor signalling.
biomarkers.why: Marks the systemic overdrive of the growth-factor axis linked to tissue overgrowth and mitogenic risk.

biomarkers.when: Glycemic instability over weeks of repeated exposure.
biomarkers.why: Captures longer-term disturbance of glucose regulation from repeated insulin-like stimulation.

contra.flag: Any current, prior, or strong family history of cancer
contra.advice: IGF-1 receptor signalling is mitogenic and can promote proliferation of malignant or pre-malignant cells; exposure is contraindicated.

contra.flag: Diabetes, or anyone using insulin or sulfonylureas
contra.advice: Additive glucose-lowering effect can precipitate severe, hard-to-reverse hypoglycemia.
A potent growth factor with no human safety data; effects on the fetus or infant are unknown and potentially harmful.
IGF-1 is implicated in retinal neovascularization and can worsen proliferative eye disease.
Excess IGF-1 signalling during growth risks abnormal skeletal and organ development; use outside a specialist medical setting is contraindicated.

follistatin-myostatin-inhibitors-ace-031.accessNote: Neither compound is approved for human use: ACE-031's Duchenne trials were halted for vascular/bleeding safety signals, and YK-11 is a grey-market steroidal SARM that never passed preclinical screening or reached any human trial.

overdose.line: There is no established human dose, so any amount is effectively an overdose of an untested agent. ACE-031 (an ActRIIB ligand trap) also blocks BMP9/BMP10 vascular signalling — trial subjects developed nosebleeds, gum bleeding and spider-vein telangiectasias, and higher exposure risks worse abnormal vessel growth and bleeding. YK-11, a steroidal SARM, concentrates supraphysiologic androgenic and hepatic stress: acute liver injury (jaundice, dark urine, right-upper-quadrant pain, nausea), sharp testosterone suppression, and cardiovascular strain. Seek urgent care for uncontrolled bleeding, jaundice, or chest pain.

misuse.line: There is no safe DIY dose because neither compound has any validated human dosing, purity standard, or safety margin — grey-market vials are unregulated and frequently mislabelled or contaminated. ACE-031's own supervised trial was stopped early for bleeding and vascular events driven by off-target BMP9/BMP10 inhibition, so no self-administered regimen can be considered safe. YK-11 never advanced past early lab screening — never tested in animals or humans for muscle growth, safety, or dose — and carries documented hepatotoxicity, testosterone suppression and hair loss from user reports. Combining them, or stacking with other SARMs or anabolics, compounds liver and cardiovascular risk.

biomarkers.range: ALT typically <40 U/L; abused YK-11 can drive marked elevations
biomarkers.why: YK-11 is a steroidal SARM associated with hepatocellular injury and enzyme spikes.
Rising bilirubin with jaundice reflects SARM-driven liver damage.

biomarkers.range: Suppressed testosterone with low LH/FSH indicates axis shutdown
biomarkers.why: YK-11 suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis.
ACE-031-class ActRIIB traps caused epistaxis and mucosal bleeding via BMP9/10 inhibition.

biomarkers.range: Suppressed HDL is typical of androgenic SARMs
biomarkers.why: YK-11's androgenic activity worsens lipids and cardiovascular risk.

contra.advice: Neither ACE-031 nor YK-11 is approved for human use; there is no clinical scenario in which self-administration is safe.

contra.flag: Bleeding disorders, anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, or history of telangiectasia/vascular malformation
contra.advice: ActRIIB ligand traps promoted abnormal vessels and mucosal bleeding — bleeding risk is amplified.

contra.flag: Liver disease or elevated liver enzymes
contra.advice: YK-11's hepatotoxicity can precipitate serious liver injury.

contra.flag: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, or trying to conceive (either partner)
contra.advice: Untested developmental toxicity plus androgenic/hormone-axis effects; avoid entirely.

contra.flag: Hormone-sensitive cancer, cardiovascular disease, or adolescents
contra.advice: Androgenic SARM activity and unknown vascular/growth effects make exposure unacceptably risky.

bpc-157.accessNote: A synthetic "body protection compound" peptide sold as a research chemical; it has no completed human clinical trials establishing safety or efficacy and is not approved for human use by any regulator. The FDA placed it on its Category 2 compounding list (substances flagged for significant safety risks) in 2023, then removed it from that list in April 2026 and referred it for compounding review — but removal is not approval, and it remains an unapproved, investigational compound.

overdose.line: There is no human toxicology data, so no overdose threshold or antidote is known — the concrete acute danger comes from injecting unregulated grey-market product: injection-site pain, redness, swelling, sterile or bacterial abscess, cellulitis, and in the worst case bloodstream infection or sepsis presenting as fever, chills, rapid heartbeat and low blood pressure. Because the peptide is strongly angiogenic (blood-vessel-promoting) in animals, a separate theoretical concern is accelerated growth of any existing tumour, though this has not been quantified in people.

misuse.line: There is no established safe do-it-yourself dose because the compound was never tested to a human safety standard — dosing is done blind, with no approved product, no pharmacological reference range, and no medical monitoring. Grey-market vials carry documented risks of underdosing, overdosing, wrong peptide, bacterial contamination, endotoxin, and heavy-metal or solvent residues, and self-injection adds infection, nerve and vascular injury risk. Athletes should also treat it as a non-approved substance that is prohibited at all times under anti-doping rules.

biomarkers.why: Screen for injection-related infection or systemic inflammatory response if an injected user develops fever, spreading redness or feeling unwell.
biomarkers.when: Only if symptoms of infection appear

biomarkers.marker: Liver panel (ALT, AST) and renal panel (creatinine, eGFR)
biomarkers.why: General safety screen — organ handling and toxicity of chronic dosing in humans are simply unstudied, so baseline and periodic checks are the only way to catch unexpected harm.
biomarkers.range: ALT/AST within lab reference; eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m^2

contra.flag: Active cancer or a personal history of cancer
contra.advice: Must avoid — the peptide promotes new blood-vessel and tissue growth in animal models, a mechanism that could theoretically feed tumour growth; there is no human safety data to exclude this.
Must avoid — there are no reproductive or developmental safety studies in humans.
It is not approved for human use and has no verified purity, dose or safety profile; any use is experimental and unsupervised.
Must avoid — it is a non-approved (WADA S0) substance prohibited at all times, in and out of competition, and its use can trigger a sanction even without a positive test.

tb-500-thymosin-beta-4-fragment.accessNote: A synthetic actin-binding fragment related to thymosin beta-4, sold only as a research chemical with no pharmaceutical-grade oversight; nearly all safety data comes from animals or from the full-length parent peptide, and it is banned at all times in tested sport under WADA (non-approved substances S0 and peptide/growth-factor class S2).

overdose.line: There is no established human toxic dose, so anyone injecting it is guessing. Anecdotal reports at higher amounts describe head-rush, flushing, lightheadedness, and lethargy shortly after a dose. The more concrete acute danger is the injected product itself: unregulated vials frequently carry bacterial endotoxin, which can cause fever, chills, rigors, hypotension and, in the worst case, sepsis within hours of an injection.

misuse.line: No human dose has ever been validated for the TB-500 fragment, so no DIY amount can be called safe. Grey-market vials are frequently mislabeled, under- or over-filled, and contaminated with bacteria or endotoxin, meaning the person injecting has no idea of the true dose or purity. On top of that, thymosin beta-4 is overexpressed in several cancers and promotes angiogenesis, tumor-cell migration and metastasis in lab and animal models, so chronic self-injection carries an unquantified risk of feeding an undetected tumor.

biomarkers.why: Rising white-cell count after injection points to a contaminated product and infection, the most likely acute harm.
biomarkers.when: If fever, chills or injection-site redness develop

biomarkers.why: A non-specific inflammation and infection marker that rises with an endotoxin reaction or injection-site cellulitis.
biomarkers.when: With any systemic reaction after dosing

biomarkers.why: Abscess, cellulitis or granuloma from non-sterile self-injection is a documented harm of grey-market peptides; there is no blood test that confirms the peptide is safe.

contra.flag: Active cancer or a personal history of cancer
contra.advice: Thymosin beta-4 drives angiogenesis and cell migration and is overexpressed in several tumor types; exogenous use could theoretically accelerate tumor growth or metastasis and must be avoided.
No human safety data exist in pregnancy or lactation for a peptide that manipulates cell growth and vascularization; it must not be used.
Prohibited at all times under WADA (S0/S2); a positive test carries disqualification and a multi-year ban.

contra.flag: Anyone unable to verify product sterility and content
contra.advice: Because it is only available as an unregulated research chemical, the realistic risk is contamination, mislabeling and infection rather than a known therapeutic effect.

ghk-cu-copper-peptide.accessNote: Topical GHK-Cu is a well-studied cosmetic ingredient, but there is no injectable or systemic product cleared for human use — that route relies on grey-market research-grade peptide of unverified purity and sterility.

overdose.line: The peptide itself has low acute toxicity in animal studies; the real hazard on repeated or high-dose injection is the copper it carries. Copper overload causes nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain and diarrhoea, and in more severe toxicity, hemolytic anemia, liver injury (jaundice, rising transaminases) and, in acute copper poisoning, acute kidney injury and multi-organ failure. Non-sterile grey-market vials add risk of injection-site infection, abscess and endotoxin/pyrogen reactions (fever, rigors). No toxic threshold has been established for injected GHK-Cu in humans.

misuse.line: There is no clinically validated injectable dose, so any self-administered amount is guesswork on top of material of unknown concentration, purity and sterility. Repeated dosing delivers a cumulative copper load the body must clear, and GHK broadly upregulates gene expression including angiogenic and tissue-remodeling pathways — a theoretical concern with any undiagnosed or active tumor. Documented DIY harms track with unsterile injection (infection, abscess) and with copper burden rather than a known 'safe' peptide window.

biomarkers.why: Directly tracks the copper load delivered by the complex; rising levels signal accumulation toward toxicity.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if systemic exposure is suspected

biomarkers.why: Assesses copper-handling capacity and helps distinguish overload from a Wilson's-type disorder that would make any copper exposure dangerous.
Liver is a primary site of copper-related injury; transaminase rises flag hepatocellular damage.
Copper toxicity can trigger hemolysis; falling hemoglobin with low haptoglobin indicates red-cell breakdown.
Severe copper toxicity can precipitate acute kidney injury.
biomarkers.range: eGFR >90 normal; rising creatinine is a warning

contra.flag: Wilson's disease or any copper-metabolism / copper-overload disorder
contra.advice: Must not use — the body cannot clear the copper, so even small copper-carrying doses risk rapid accumulation and organ damage.
Should avoid — GHK upregulates angiogenic and tissue-remodeling genes, a theoretical risk with tumor tissue that has not been shown safe.
Should avoid — there are no human safety data for systemic GHK-Cu in pregnancy or lactation.
Must not use — risk of allergic and injection-site reactions.

kpv.accessNote: Available only as a grey-market "research" peptide; no drug regulator has cleared KPV for human use and no completed human trial has defined a safe dose or duration.

overdose.line: No human toxicity threshold has been established; animal models show low acute toxicity, so the real acute dangers of grey-market vials are not a classic 'overdose' but bacterial contamination, endotoxin and sterility failures causing fever, injection-site infection, abscess or sepsis.

misuse.line: Because no human dosing has ever been validated, every self-administered amount is a guess; unregulated vials vary in purity and may be underdosed, mislabelled or contaminated, and reconstituting or injecting without sterile technique risks serious infection. There is no established safe DIY protocol.

biomarkers.why: No KPV-specific lab exists, so a clinician tracks non-specific inflammatory markers to catch an infection or paradoxical inflammatory response.
Screens for infection (raised WBC) introduced by a non-sterile injectable and for unexpected haematological effects.
Baseline organ function when exposing the body to an unapproved compound with no human long-term data.

contra.advice: An immune-modulating, anti-inflammatory peptide combined with a non-sterile injection route can worsen or mask infection; avoid.
KPV alters NF-kB and inflammatory signalling; its effect on human tumour biology is unknown, so it should be avoided.
No reproductive or developmental safety data exist; do not use.

contra.flag: Known hypersensitivity to the peptide or vial excipients
contra.advice: Prior reaction is a contraindication to re-exposure.
No safety data in this group; not appropriate.

thymosin-alpha-1-zadaxin.accessNote: Approved and dispensed as thymalfasin in 35+ countries for chronic hepatitis B and as an immune adjunct in cancer/infection care; not FDA-approved in the US, where any supply is grey-market research peptide.

typicalDose.line: Where approved for chronic hepatitis B, a doctor typically prescribes 1.6 mg by subcutaneous injection twice weekly (injections ~3–4 days apart) for roughly 6–12 months; the exact regimen, indication and duration are set by the prescriber, not chosen by the patient. Reference figure only, not a recommendation.

overdose.line: No distinct acute-overdose toxidrome is documented; animal studies tolerated large multiples of the human dose without organ damage, and human adverse events are usually limited to injection-site pain, low-grade fever and flu-like aches. The real hazard of excess is immune over-activation — case reports of severe immune-related inflammatory events (e.g. when combined with checkpoint-inhibitor cancer drugs) mean any new autoimmune-type or systemic inflammatory symptoms warrant urgent medical review.

misuse.line: Because this is used as a prescribed course, the danger is self-directed use: dosing outside medical supervision, stacking with immunotherapy or vaccines, or continuing during an autoimmune flare can drive harmful immune over-activation, while grey-market vials carry no guarantee of identity, sterility or endotoxin control — injecting non-pharmaceutical peptide risks contamination, sepsis and unknown impurities. It is not a substitute for prescribed antiviral therapy, and stopping or altering a real hepatitis regimen on your own can let viral disease progress.

biomarkers.why: Tracks hepatic inflammation and treatment response in its primary hepatitis-B indication.
biomarkers.when: Baseline then periodically through a course

biomarkers.why: Direct measure of antiviral effect in chronic hepatitis B.

biomarkers.range: Goal: HBeAg loss with anti-HBe seroconversion
biomarkers.why: Seroconversion is the key efficacy endpoint the prescriber monitors.
Screens for marrow/immune effects, especially when co-administered with interferon.
An immune-stimulating drug can unmask thyroid or other autoimmune activity; checked if symptoms arise.

contra.flag: Known hypersensitivity to thymosin alpha-1 or any excipient
contra.advice: Absolute contraindication — do not use.

contra.flag: Organ transplant recipients / deliberately immunosuppressed patients
contra.advice: Boosting T-cell activity can undermine anti-rejection therapy and trigger graft rejection; must not be used without transplant-team direction.
Enhancing immune function may theoretically worsen autoimmune activity; use only under specialist supervision.
Combination has been linked in case reports to serious immune-related adverse events; only under oncology oversight.
Insufficient safety data; avoid.

ll-37.accessNote: An endogenous human antimicrobial peptide (the cathelicidin cleaved from hCAP18) studied only in early lab and animal work; it has no approval for human use and is sold grey-market as an injectable "research chemical" with no purity, sterility, potency, or dosing standards.

overdose.line: There is no established human dose, so no defined toxic threshold exists, but in lab studies LL-37 turns cytotoxic to human cells above roughly 4 micromolar — permeabilising cell membranes, injuring mitochondria, and (at higher concentrations) lysing red blood cells — so systemic exposure could plausibly drive hemolysis, a broad inflammatory cytokine surge, and injection-site tissue injury or necrosis; no human toxicity or overdose data exist.

misuse.line: There is no safe DIY dose: LL-37 is a potent immune-signalling molecule and a known self-antigen — bound to your own DNA and RNA it switches on type-I interferon pathways and can ignite or worsen autoimmune inflammation (documented in psoriasis, lupus, rosacea and atherosclerotic plaque), and grey-market vials additionally carry the risks of endotoxin contamination, wrong or degraded peptide, and non-sterile self-injection.

biomarkers.why: General inflammatory response to an immune-activating peptide; would rise if a systemic inflammatory reaction is triggered. No validated monitoring protocol exists for this compound.

biomarkers.marker: CBC with haptoglobin, LDH, and bilirubin
biomarkers.why: Screens for the red-cell breakdown (hemolysis) that LL-37 causes in vitro at higher concentrations; low haptoglobin with high LDH and bilirubin signals hemolysis.
LL-37 acts as an autoantigen and drives interferon-mediated autoimmunity in lab and disease models; new positivity or a flare in a susceptible person is the relevant harm signal.

contra.flag: Not approved for human use in anyone
contra.advice: Because it has no approved indication, no established dose, and no human safety data, there is no population for which self-administration is considered safe.

contra.flag: Autoimmune or chronic inflammatory disease (psoriasis, lupus/SLE, rosacea, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis)
contra.advice: LL-37 is a self-antigen that amplifies interferon-driven autoimmune inflammation and is directly implicated in these conditions — exposure risks triggering or worsening disease.
Cathelicidin/LL-37 has been identified as a plaque autoantigen linked to vascular inflammation, so it is a plausible aggravator in people with existing arterial disease.

contra.flag: Active cancer or a personal cancer history
contra.advice: LL-37 has pro-tumour, pro-angiogenic effects in several cancers (e.g. ovarian, lung, breast), though effects are organ-dependent; the uncertainty alone warrants avoidance.
No safety data exist in pregnancy or lactation for an immune-active peptide, so exposure is not appropriate.

rapamycin-sirolimus.accessNote: FDA-approved as the immunosuppressant sirolimus to prevent kidney-transplant rejection and to treat lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM); its use for longevity is entirely off-label and investigational.

typicalDose.line: For reference only, and always set by a prescribing physician to measured blood levels — not a target to copy. In approved transplant/LAM use a doctor typically starts with a loading dose (around 6 mg) then roughly 2 mg once daily, adjusting to a whole-blood trough of about 5-15 ng/mL. Off-label longevity protocols use much lower intermittent (often weekly) dosing, but this is unproven, unlicensed, and a decision only a clinician can make and monitor.

overdose.line: Toxicity usually builds from cumulative over-exposure rather than a single large dose. Signs include painful mouth ulcers (stomatitis), interstitial pneumonitis (dry cough, breathlessness, fever — can be fatal), sharply raised triglycerides and cholesterol, low platelets and white cells, anemia, leg and face swelling, protein in the urine, impaired wound healing, and dangerously blunted immunity leading to serious bacterial, viral, or opportunistic infections. Acute single overdoses have been reported to trigger atrial fibrillation.

misuse.line: Self-dosing without a prescription is hazardous because the drug has a narrow therapeutic window and no blood-level monitoring is being done. It is a CYP3A4 substrate: grapefruit and strong inhibitors (clarithromycin, ketoconazole, ritonavir, diltiazem) push levels into the toxic range, while inducers (rifampin, St John's wort) cause loss of effect; combining with cyclosporine raises exposure further. Unsupervised use suppresses immunity, raises lipids and glucose, and impairs wound healing, and live vaccines become dangerous while it is active.

biomarkers.when: Steady state (check ~3-4 days after any loading dose, and allow 7-14 days after a dose change), then periodically
biomarkers.why: Narrow therapeutic index; the single most important safety check to keep exposure below toxic levels
Hypertriglyceridemia and hypercholesterolemia are common, dose-related class effects
Thrombocytopenia, leukopenia/neutropenia, and anemia are recognised dose-related effects
mTOR inhibition can impair glucose tolerance and unmask hyperglycemia

biomarkers.range: eGFR stable; urine protein:creatinine within normal limits
biomarkers.why: Sirolimus can cause or worsen proteinuria
Hepatic impairment alters clearance and requires dose adjustment and monitoring

contra.flag: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, or trying to conceive
contra.advice: Avoid — effective contraception is required during and for a period after use; it can harm a developing fetus and impair male and female fertility.
Do not use — immunosuppression can allow bacterial, viral, fungal, or opportunistic infections to become life-threatening.

contra.flag: Lung transplant recipients / recent major surgery or unhealed wounds
contra.advice: Contraindicated early after lung transplant (risk of bronchial anastomotic dehiscence) and cautioned around surgery because it impairs wound healing.
Live vaccines (e.g. MMR, yellow fever, live varicella) should be avoided while the drug is active due to blunted immune response and infection risk.
Do not use if there is a documented allergy to sirolimus or formulation components.

contra.flag: Concurrent strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers
contra.advice: Combinations (e.g. certain azole antifungals, macrolides, HIV protease inhibitors, rifampin, St John's wort, grapefruit) can push levels to toxic or subtherapeutic — only a prescriber can manage these.
Reduced clearance requires dose reduction and closer monitoring; unsupervised use is unsafe.
Use with caution and specialist oversight, as the drug can worsen lipids and glucose control.

metformin.accessNote: It is the standard first-line oral medicine for type-2 diabetes, and the same molecule is being studied off-label for healthspan (e.g. the TAME trial) — a doctor decides if and how it is used.

typicalDose.line: For reference only, and always set by a prescribing doctor: immediate-release metformin is usually started low (around 500 mg once or twice daily, taken with food to limit gut upset) and titrated slowly, with a typical maintenance range of roughly 1500–2000 mg/day and a usual ceiling near 2000–2550 mg/day depending on the formulation and the person's kidney function. Extended-release versions are dosed differently. This is background information, not a recommendation or a dosing instruction.

overdose.line: The signature danger of metformin toxicity or overdose is lactic acidosis (MALA): nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, muscle aches, unusual tiredness, deep rapid breathing, feeling cold, low blood pressure, drowsiness and confusion that can progress to coma. It carries a high fatality rate and needs emergency care (often dialysis). Note that metformin alone rarely causes low blood sugar, so hypoglycaemia is not the typical overdose picture.

misuse.line: Not following the prescription is where harm concentrates: doubling up or self-escalating doses, or continuing during dehydration, vomiting/diarrhoea, acute illness, sepsis, heavy alcohol use, or around iodinated-contrast scans and surgery, all raise the risk of lactic acidosis — especially if kidney function has quietly declined. Taking it without the renal and B12 monitoring a doctor would order removes the safety net. Skipping or abruptly stopping instead loses glycaemic control.

biomarkers.range: Contraindicated below eGFR 30 mL/min/1.73m²; not started and dose reviewed between 30–45
biomarkers.why: Metformin is cleared by the kidneys; impaired clearance is the main driver of lactic-acidosis risk, so renal function is checked before starting and periodically after
biomarkers.when: Baseline and at least annually, more often if kidney function is borderline or declining

biomarkers.why: Long-term use reduces B12 absorption and can cause deficiency and neuropathy over years
biomarkers.when: Periodically with long-term use, or if anaemia or neuropathy symptoms appear

biomarkers.range: Individualised, commonly a target near <7% (53 mmol/mol) in type-2 diabetes
biomarkers.why: Tracks whether glycaemic control is adequate and guides dose in the diabetes indication

biomarkers.range: Normal <2 mmol/L; markedly elevated (often >5) in lactic acidosis
biomarkers.why: Confirms suspected metformin-associated lactic acidosis when a person is acutely unwell
biomarkers.when: When toxicity is suspected (acute illness, symptoms above)

contra.advice: Absolute contraindication — reduced clearance sharply raises lactic-acidosis risk

contra.flag: Acute metabolic acidosis, including diabetic ketoacidosis
contra.advice: Must not be used; requires emergency management instead

contra.flag: Conditions causing tissue hypoxia — decompensated heart failure, respiratory failure, recent MI, shock, sepsis
contra.advice: Avoid; these states independently promote lactic acidosis
Avoid — impaired lactate clearance raises risk

contra.flag: Acute dehydration or serious infection, and around iodinated-contrast imaging or major surgery
contra.advice: Temporarily withheld under medical guidance because of transient renal risk
Avoid — alcohol compounds lactic-acidosis and hypoglycaemia risk

acarbose.accessNote: Approved and prescribed for type-2 diabetes to blunt the post-meal glucose spike; it needs medical supervision to titrate against gut tolerance, liver enzymes, and interacting glucose-lowering drugs.

typicalDose.line: As a reference only, and never a recommendation: a doctor typically starts 25 mg taken with the first bite of each main meal (three times daily) and titrates upward over weeks against tolerance, up to 50-100 mg three times daily; body-weight and country labels cap the maximum (commonly 300 mg/day, and 50 mg three times daily in adults at or below 60 kg because of higher liver-enzyme risk). The dose, escalation, and ceiling are set by the prescriber, not the reader.

overdose.line: Acarbose is barely absorbed into the bloodstream, so an overdose taken alone does NOT cause hypoglycaemia or systemic toxicity. Taken with carbohydrate-containing food or drink, an excess dose causes marked flatulence, bloating, abdominal cramping, and osmotic diarrhoea as undigested carbohydrate ferments in the colon; symptoms are usually self-limiting once carbohydrate is withheld for several hours.

misuse.line: Taking it away from the first bite of a meal makes it useless, and pushing the dose above what was prescribed (to "cancel" more carbs) drives severe GI distress and dose-related liver-enzyme elevation. Critically, when combined with insulin or a sulfonylurea it can provoke hypoglycaemia that must be treated with glucose (dextrose) tablets or gel, NOT table sugar or sucrose-containing sweets, because acarbose blocks sucrose breakdown and the sugar will not raise blood glucose fast enough.

biomarkers.why: Tracks whether the drug is actually improving glycaemic control and guides dose changes.

biomarkers.range: target set by prescriber, commonly <10 mmol/L / <180 mg/dL
biomarkers.why: Acarbose's main effect is flattening the post-meal spike, so this is the most direct measure of its action.

biomarkers.range: normal ~<40 U/L; sustained rise >3x upper limit prompts dose cut or stop
biomarkers.when: every 3 months during the first year, then periodically
biomarkers.why: Dose-related transaminase elevation and rare hepatotoxicity occur, more likely above 50 mg three times daily; monitoring catches it before jaundice.

biomarkers.range: caution as eGFR falls; avoided in severe impairment (CrCl <25 mL/min)
biomarkers.why: Drug and metabolites accumulate to much higher plasma levels in renal failure, which is why it is contraindicated there.

contra.flag: Inflammatory bowel disease, colonic ulceration, partial intestinal obstruction or predisposition to it, or chronic disorders of digestion/absorption
contra.advice: Contraindicated; the increased colonic gas and fermentation can worsen the bowel condition or precipitate obstruction.
Contraindicated; the liver is the site of reported drug injury and impaired clearance raises the risk.
Contraindicated; the poorly-absorbed drug and its metabolites accumulate to markedly higher plasma concentrations.
Not recommended; adequate human safety data are lacking.
Contraindicated; this is a medical emergency requiring insulin, not a meal-timed oral agent.

contra.flag: Conditions worsened by increased intestinal gas (e.g. large hernias)
contra.advice: Contraindicated; predictable bloating and gas can aggravate the condition.

quercetin-dasatinib-d-q.accessNote: Dasatinib is an approved tyrosine-kinase-inhibitor chemotherapy for chronic myeloid leukaemia and Ph+ acute lymphoblastic leukaemia; its senolytic use with quercetin is investigational and studied only under physician supervision in clinical trials.

typicalDose.line: Reference only, always set by a physician. In leukaemia, dasatinib is prescribed at 100 mg once daily (chronic-phase CML) up to 140 mg daily (advanced disease). In senolytic research it has been given very differently — roughly 100 mg dasatinib plus about 1000-1250 mg quercetin for 2-3 consecutive days, given as short intermittent courses separated by weeks — but this is an unapproved investigational protocol used only inside supervised trials, not a validated or recommended regimen.

overdose.line: Toxicity is dose- and exposure-driven: severe myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anaemia) raising infection and bleeding risk; serious bleeding including GI or intracranial haemorrhage; fluid retention with pleural or pericardial effusion and pulmonary oedema causing breathlessness and cough; QT-interval prolongation and arrhythmia risk; and, rarely, pulmonary arterial hypertension. Warning signs include unusual bruising or bleeding, fever, new shortness of breath, chest pain, swelling, palpitations or fainting — all needing urgent medical assessment.

misuse.line: Taking dasatinib without prescription and monitoring is dangerous. It is a CYP3A4 substrate, so strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, some macrolides, grapefruit) and even quercetin itself can raise its blood levels and toxicity; inducers can make it ineffective. Combining it with anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents sharply increases bleeding, and stacking QT-prolonging drugs risks dangerous arrhythmia. Acid-suppressing drugs (PPIs, H2 blockers, antacids) cut its absorption. Skipping trial-style safety bloodwork and ECGs means effusions, low counts and rhythm changes go undetected until they are serious.

biomarkers.why: Detects dasatinib myelosuppression — falling neutrophils, platelets and haemoglobin.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and periodically during any course
biomarkers.range: Neutrophils >1.5 x10^9/L, platelets >100 x10^9/L before dosing

biomarkers.why: Dasatinib can prolong ventricular repolarisation and provoke arrhythmia.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if on other QT-prolonging drugs

biomarkers.why: Low levels amplify QT-prolongation risk and are corrected before dosing.
biomarkers.range: K 3.5-5.0 mmol/L; Mg within lab reference

biomarkers.marker: Chest X-ray / clinical review for effusion
biomarkers.why: Fluid retention with pleural or pericardial effusion is a characteristic dasatinib toxicity.
biomarkers.when: If dyspnoea, cough or chest discomfort develop

biomarkers.why: Monitors for hepatotoxicity and informs dosing.
biomarkers.range: Transaminases within ~3x upper limit of normal

biomarkers.why: Screens for the rare but serious pulmonary arterial hypertension linked to dasatinib.
biomarkers.when: If unexplained breathlessness or signs of right-heart strain

contra.advice: Dasatinib is fetotoxic and contraindicated; effective contraception is required and breastfeeding should be avoided.

contra.flag: Pre-existing pleural or pericardial effusion, heart failure or fluid-retention disorders
contra.advice: Must not use — dasatinib strongly promotes serous effusions and fluid overload.

contra.flag: Bleeding disorders or use of anticoagulants/antiplatelet drugs
contra.advice: Avoid — platelet dysfunction and thrombocytopenia raise haemorrhage risk.

contra.flag: Congenital long QT syndrome, significant arrhythmia, or uncorrected low potassium/magnesium
contra.advice: Avoid — additive QT prolongation can trigger dangerous arrhythmias.
Do not use until counts recover — dasatinib deepens cytopenias.
Contraindicated — dasatinib is a recognised cause and can worsen it.

contra.flag: Concurrent strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, certain macrolides, grapefruit)
contra.advice: Avoid the combination — dasatinib levels and toxicity rise substantially.

sglt2-inhibitors-empagliflozin-canaglifl.accessNote: Prescribed for type 2 diabetes, chronic heart failure (reduced and preserved ejection fraction), and chronic kidney disease, based on a clinician's assessment of kidney function and cardiovascular risk.

typicalDose.line: For reference only, and set by a prescribing doctor — not a recommendation. Empagliflozin is typically prescribed at 10-25 mg once daily and canagliflozin at 100-300 mg once daily, usually taken in the morning, with the dose and eligibility governed by kidney function (eGFR) and the specific condition being treated.

overdose.line: These drugs rarely cause dangerous hypoglycaemia on their own, so acute overdose is mostly about fluid and acid-base effects: heavy urination, dehydration, low blood pressure, dizziness and acute kidney injury. The most dangerous complication is euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis — ketoacidosis with only mildly raised or even near-normal blood glucose, presenting as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, deep rapid breathing, fruity breath and confusion. It is easy to miss because glucose looks 'fine', and it is a medical emergency.

misuse.line: Stopping abruptly removes the heart- and kidney-protective effect these drugs provide. Doubling doses or continuing them through acute illness, vomiting, fasting, dehydration, heavy alcohol use, crash/keto dieting or around surgery sharply raises the risk of euglycaemic ketoacidosis — which is why doctors give 'sick-day' pause instructions and hold the drug before operations. Combining with diuretics can cause dangerous volume depletion, and self-dosing without periodic kidney-function checks means declining eGFR or a brewing genital infection can go unnoticed.

biomarkers.why: Determines eligibility and dosing; an expected small early dip occurs, but a sustained fall or acute kidney injury must be caught.
biomarkers.range: eGFR generally >20-30 mL/min/1.73m2 for continuation depending on indication

biomarkers.why: Tracks glycaemic effect when used for diabetes; glucose can look near-normal even during ketoacidosis, so it is not a safety marker on its own.
Detects euglycaemic ketoacidosis during illness, fasting or unexplained nausea/vomiting.
biomarkers.when: During acute illness or ketoacidosis symptoms
biomarkers.range: Blood BHB >0.6 mmol/L warrants attention; >3 mmol/L is high-risk

biomarkers.why: Diuretic-like effect can cause dehydration, hypotension and electrolyte shifts, especially with other diuretics.
SGLT2 inhibitors can modestly raise LDL, relevant in high cardiovascular-risk patients.

contra.flag: Type 1 diabetes or prior diabetic ketoacidosis
contra.advice: Not appropriate for routine glucose control in type 1 diabetes and avoided with a history of DKA, because of high euglycaemic ketoacidosis risk.
Not recommended; should be stopped when pregnancy is planned or confirmed.

contra.flag: Recurrent genital or urinary infections, or prior Fournier's gangrene
contra.advice: Glucose in the urine promotes genital yeast and urinary infections, and rare necrotising perineal infection (Fournier's gangrene) has occurred; a history of these weighs against use.

contra.flag: Acute illness, dehydration, fasting or upcoming surgery
contra.advice: Should be paused during significant illness, volume depletion or around major surgery (typically held several days before) to avoid ketoacidosis and kidney injury.
The glucose-lowering effect fails and initiation is limited at very low eGFR; use at the margins is decided case-by-case by a specialist.

Canagliflozin has been linked with signals for lower-limb amputation and bone fracture (the original amputation boxed warning was later removed by the FDA after further data), so caution still applies in those with prior amputation, peripheral vascular disease or high fracture risk.

mots-c.accessNote: Sold only as a research chemical with no completed human safety or efficacy trial, and the single MOTS-c analog tested in people had its clinical development discontinued.

overdose.line: No human toxicology or overdose threshold exists, so the effects of excessive dosing are genuinely uncharacterised. Because it activates AMPK and drives glucose into skeletal muscle, an exaggerated response could plausibly cause hypoglycaemia (shakiness, sweating, palpitations, confusion), and injecting an unregulated vial can trigger acute endotoxin-driven fever, chills, and injection-site reactions.

misuse.line: There is no validated human dose, so every DIY regimen is guesswork with no safety margin behind it. Grey-market peptide vials are unregulated and have repeatedly been found to contain inaccurate or absent active peptide, bacterial contamination, and endotoxin; non-sterile self-injection risks abscess, cellulitis, and bloodstream infection, and the long-term effects of a mitochondrial signalling peptide in humans are simply unknown.

biomarkers.when: Baseline and if symptoms of a low occur
biomarkers.why: Its AMPK / GLUT4 action can push glucose down; watch for hypoglycaemia, especially alongside any glucose-lowering medication.
Tracks the metabolic effect over months and flags meaningful glycaemic shifts.

biomarkers.when: If fever, redness or pain follows an injection
biomarkers.why: Rises with injection-site infection, abscess, or endotoxin reaction from a contaminated product.
Elevated with cellulitis or bloodstream infection from non-sterile injecting.
General safety screen for hepatic injury given an unstudied injected compound of unknown purity.

contra.advice: No safety data of any kind exists; an unapproved injected peptide must not be used.

contra.flag: Diabetes treated with insulin or sulfonylureas
contra.advice: The insulin-sensitising, glucose-lowering action is additive and could provoke dangerous hypoglycaemia.

contra.flag: Active infection or a compromised immune system
contra.advice: Injecting an unsterile-risk product raises the chance of serious systemic infection.

contra.flag: Anyone unable to verify a sterile, correctly identified product
contra.advice: There is no approved, quality-controlled human source, so purity, dose accuracy, and sterility cannot be assured.
Effects of a proliferation- and metabolism-modulating mitochondrial peptide on tumour biology in humans are unstudied; avoid.

elamipretide-ss-31.accessNote: Granted FDA accelerated approval in September 2025 to improve muscle strength in patients weighing at least 30 kg with the rare mitochondrial disorder Barth syndrome; every anti-aging or general-longevity use is off-label, unproven, and typically supplied through unregulated grey-market channels rather than a pharmacy.
