typicalDose.line: In its one approved use, Barth syndrome, the label sets the dosage at 40 mg once daily as a subcutaneous injection in patients weighing at least 30 kg (reduced to 20 mg daily in severe renal impairment); the exact regimen is decided by the prescriber. This is a reference figure for education only, not a recommendation, and no dose is established for longevity or healthspan use.

overdose.line: No overdose was reported in the clinical trials and there is no well-characterised human overdose syndrome; the manufacturer notes that overdose signs are likely histamine-related, mainly a drop in blood pressure and light-headedness or presyncope. The serious acute danger with any injected peptide is a hypersensitivity or anaphylactic reaction, which can occur within minutes of a dose: spreading hives, swelling of the lips or throat, wheeze, or a sudden drop in blood pressure is a medical emergency.

misuse.line: Because it is an injectable peptide, the real harm comes from using it outside medical supervision: grey-market 'longevity' vials carry no guarantee of purity, dose, or sterility, and non-sterile self-injection can cause injection-site abscesses and bloodstream infection. Skipping, stopping, or self-adjusting a prescribed course removes the cardiac and functional monitoring its narrow approval depends on, and its benefit for anti-aging or general healthspan has never been demonstrated.

biomarkers.why: Injection-site reactions (erythema, induration, pruritus, pain) are the most common adverse effect and occurred in essentially all treated patients in the pivotal trial; a hot, spreading, or increasingly painful site can signal infection needing treatment.
biomarkers.when: With each injection / at every clinic visit

biomarkers.why: Barth syndrome causes cardiomyopathy, so ejection fraction tracks the underlying disease and cardiac status; a sensible check given the drug's mitochondrial/cardiac target.
Improvement in knee extensor muscle strength is the intermediate endpoint the accelerated approval rests on; a doctor uses it to judge whether treatment is actually helping.
The label reduces the dose in severe renal impairment, so kidney function is checked to set and adjust dosing.
Rise if a self-injection site or deeper tissue becomes infected, the main lab signal of harm in unsupervised grey-market use.
biomarkers.when: If an injection site looks infected or fever develops

contra.flag: Known serious hypersensitivity to elamipretide or the injection's excipients
contra.advice: Contraindicated on the label; do not use because of the risk of a serious allergic reaction.
Not approved in neonates; the benzyl alcohol content can cause fatal toxicity.
No adequate human safety data; avoid outside a formal clinical trial.

contra.flag: Anyone below the approved weight (~30 kg) or without the Barth-syndrome indication
contra.advice: Dose and safety are unestablished; this is not a validated longevity therapy and should not be self-sourced off-label.

contra.flag: Anyone unable to inject under sterile, medically supervised conditions
contra.advice: Non-sterile self-injection of unregulated grey-market product risks serious skin and bloodstream infection.

17-estradiol.accessNote: Its lifespan evidence comes only from male mice in the NIA Interventions Testing Program; there are no controlled human longevity trials and no established systemic human dose or safety profile. A separate low-dose topical formulation is an approved prescription hair-loss treatment in parts of Europe, South America and Korea, but that does not validate any systemic or longevity use.

overdose.line: The 'non-feminizing' label reflects that 17a-estradiol is a weak activator of estrogen receptor alpha compared with 17b-estradiol; selectivity is relative, not absolute, and estrogenic effects could plausibly emerge as the dose rises. Because it is a weak estrogen, feminizing effects (such as gynecomastia, reduced libido or testicular changes in men) and the recognised dangers of potent estrogens - venous thromboembolism (leg swelling/pain, sudden breathlessness or chest pain from DVT/PE), stroke, and cholestatic liver injury - are extrapolated from stronger estrogens and are not documented for 17a-estradiol in humans; there is no human overdose data. As an unregulated research chemical, unknown identity and purity add unpredictable toxicity on top of this.

misuse.line: There is no safe do-it-yourself dose. The only efficacy data is a fixed dietary concentration fed to mice, which does not translate to a human dose, and the survival benefit appeared in males only and vanished in females and castrated males - so the effect may not apply to a given person at all. Bought as a research chemical it carries no purity or identity guarantee. Because it is estrogenic, even if weakly, self-dosing could affect the testosterone/LH/FSH axis and carries theoretical clot risk, with no medical monitoring to catch harm early.

biomarkers.why: Estrogenic activity can suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, potentially lowering testosterone.

biomarkers.range: e.g. LH ~1.7-8.6 IU/L; suppression signals HPG shutdown
biomarkers.why: Estrogen negative feedback can lower gonadotropins, an early sign of endocrine disruption.
Estrogens characteristically raise SHBG, lowering free testosterone even when total looks normal.
Estrogens can cause cholestatic or hepatocellular injury; rising enzymes or bilirubin flag liver stress.
Standard assays measure 17-beta-estradiol and may not reflect 17-alpha levels, so a 'normal' result can be falsely reassuring.

contra.flag: Personal or family history of estrogen-sensitive cancer (e.g. breast)
contra.advice: This compound has estrogenic activity, which could stimulate hormone-sensitive tumours; avoid it.

contra.flag: History of venous thromboembolism, known thrombophilia, or prior DVT/PE/stroke
contra.advice: Estrogens can raise clotting risk; anyone with a clotting history or disorder should avoid it as a precaution.
Hormonally active estrogens are contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal risk.
Estrogens are metabolised hepatically and can cause cholestasis, worsening existing liver impairment.

contra.flag: Established cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, or smokers over 35
contra.advice: These sharply amplify the thromboembolic and vascular risks of estrogen exposure.

modafinil-armodafinil.accessNote: Approved for narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnoea residual sleepiness and shift-work sleep disorder; a Schedule IV controlled substance widely taken off-label for wakefulness and focus.

typicalDose.line: As a reference only, and always decided by the prescribing doctor: modafinil is usually 200 mg once in the morning (up to 400 mg has no proven added benefit); armodafinil is usually 150 mg once daily. Shift-work dosing is taken about 1 hour before the shift. Doses are reduced in severe liver impairment and in older adults.

overdose.line: Overdose is dominated by over-stimulation: agitation, insomnia, anxiety, confusion, irritability, tremor, headache, nausea, palpitations, tachycardia, raised blood pressure and sometimes chest pain. Ingestions of several grams have caused marked restlessness and cardiovascular stimulation; deaths from modafinil alone are rare, but it becomes far more dangerous combined with other stimulants or in people with heart disease.

misuse.line: Not following the prescription carries real risk. Taking it too late in the day wrecks sleep; doubling doses or stacking it with other stimulants raises blood pressure, heart rate and anxiety toward the overdose picture. It is an enzyme inducer that lowers the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives (pill, patch, ring, implant) during use and for a month after, causing unplanned pregnancies, and it alters levels of other CYP3A4/CYP2C19 drugs. As a controlled substance it carries dependence and misuse potential, and any spreading rash is a medical emergency, not a dose to push through.

biomarkers.marker: Blood pressure and resting heart rate
biomarkers.why: Modafinil and armodafinil can raise both; monitored at baseline and periodically, with dose or drug review if hypertension or tachycardia develops.
biomarkers.range: Target generally <140/90 mmHg; resting HR ~60-100 bpm

biomarkers.why: Screens for and monitors cardiac risk in anyone with a history of left ventricular hypertrophy, arrhythmia, valve disease or prior stimulant-related ischaemic changes.
biomarkers.range: No new ischaemic changes or significant arrhythmia
biomarkers.when: Baseline if cardiac history; as clinically indicated

biomarkers.why: Clearance is hepatic; severe impairment requires a halved dose, so liver status guides prescribing.
biomarkers.range: ALT/AST roughly within lab reference (~<40 U/L)
biomarkers.when: Baseline in known or suspected liver disease

biomarkers.why: Linked to major congenital malformations, and it reduces hormonal contraceptive efficacy, so pregnancy status and reliable non-hormonal or added barrier contraception are checked before and during use.
biomarkers.when: Before starting in people who can become pregnant

contra.flag: Prior serious skin or hypersensitivity reaction to modafinil or armodafinil
contra.advice: Absolute contraindication. Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, DRESS and multi-organ hypersensitivity have occurred; any rash, mucosal blistering, fever or facial swelling means stop immediately and seek urgent care, and the drug must never be restarted.

Contraindicated. Registry and label data show increased major congenital malformations (including cardiac and hypospadias); it is avoided in pregnancy and reliable contraception is used, remembering it weakens hormonal contraception.

Used with great caution or avoided with left ventricular hypertrophy, mitral valve prolapse syndrome, recent myocardial infarction, unstable angina, serious arrhythmia or uncontrolled hypertension, because of its blood-pressure and heart-rate effects.

contra.flag: History of psychosis, mania or severe anxiety
contra.advice: Can trigger or worsen psychosis, mania, hallucinations, aggression, suicidal ideation and severe anxiety; used with great caution or avoided in these conditions.
Not a safe standard dose; the dose must be halved and use reconsidered, since the liver clears the drug.

semax-selank.accessNote: Registered as prescription nasal-spray medicines only in Russia (Semax for stroke/cognition, Selank for anxiety); everywhere else, including Singapore, the US and EU, no regulator has reviewed them and they circulate as grey-market research chemicals with no purity, sterility or potency guarantee.

overdose.line: No characterised human overdose syndrome exists — short Russian trials report low acute toxicity, so the real danger of a large or repeated dose is not a known toxic threshold but the unregulated product itself: over-concentrated or mislabelled vials can deliver far more peptide than intended, and non-sterile material can trigger fever, chills and systemic infection. Acute effects reported at higher intranasal strengths include headache, marked nasal-mucosa irritation, and over-stimulation or agitation. Any fever, spreading redness, confusion or breathing difficulty after use is a medical emergency.

misuse.line: There is no validated safe do-it-yourself dose outside Russia's specific clinical formulations — grey-market vials are frequently mislabelled, under- or over-dosed, and are not sterile-manufactured, so reconstituting and self-administering (intranasally or by injection) risks bacterial contamination, endotoxin reactions and injection-site infection. Long-term human safety is essentially unstudied, interactions with stimulants, SSRIs and other CNS drugs are uncharacterised, and 'stacking' the two compounds compounds all of these unknowns.

biomarkers.when: if fever, malaise or local reaction follows use
biomarkers.why: The main measurable harm from unregulated peptides is infection or endotoxin reaction from non-sterile product; a rising CRP/WBC is the earliest lab signal.

biomarkers.when: baseline and if unwell after use
biomarkers.why: Not because the peptides are known hepato- or nephrotoxic, but to detect harm from solvents, preservatives or adulterants in unverified grey-market vials.

contra.advice: Absolute contraindication — no reproductive safety data exist in humans.

contra.flag: Known hypersensitivity to either peptide or spray excipients
contra.advice: Do not use; risk of allergic reaction.
Not for non-medical use; no safety data outside supervised Russian paediatric protocols.
Avoid — blood-pressure changes have been reported and are unmonitored in self-use.

contra.flag: Serious or unstable psychiatric illness (e.g. bipolar disorder, psychosis)
contra.advice: Avoid — CNS-active peptides can worsen mood instability or agitation, and interactions with psychiatric medication are unstudied.

nicotine-non-combusted.accessNote: Approved for human use as a smoking-cessation aid (gum, patch, lozenge, pouch), often available over the counter; using it purely for focus is off-label and carries high dependence risk.

typicalDose.line: As a clinician- or label-directed cessation aid only: transdermal patches are typically stepped down over weeks (commonly 21 mg, then 14 mg, then 7 mg per 24 h), and gum/lozenges come as 2 mg or 4 mg units with a stated daily piece cap. These are reference cessation figures a prescriber or pharmacist sets against smoking history, not a recommendation and not a cognitive-use dose.

overdose.line: Acute nicotine toxicity is biphasic: early nausea, vomiting, salivation, sweating, abdominal cramps, headache, tremor, hypertension and fast heart rate, then weakness, low blood pressure, slow heart rate, confusion, seizures, and in severe cases respiratory muscle failure and coma. Overlapping products (patch plus pouches plus continued smoking) is a common route to poisoning; swallowed gum, lozenges, or e-liquid are especially dangerous to children and pets. The classic '30–60 mg is lethal' figure is now considered too low, but any ingestion still warrants urgent poison-control contact.

misuse.line: Nicotine is powerfully addictive, and self-dosing for concentration reliably escalates into tolerance and daily dependence; abruptly stopping brings irritability, anxiety, poor focus, and cravings that drive relapse. Stacking forms or continuing to smoke while patched raises toxicity risk, it acutely stresses the cardiovascular system, and adolescent or young-adult use can durably alter the developing brain.

biomarkers.marker: Blood pressure and resting heart rate
biomarkers.why: Nicotine is a sympathomimetic that acutely raises both; a clinician watches these especially with any cardiovascular history.
The main nicotine metabolite; confirms exposure and rough intake level and distinguishes ongoing use from abstinence.
Nicotine can worsen insulin sensitivity; emerging signal, monitored mainly in those already at metabolic risk.

contra.flag: Recent heart attack, unstable angina, or serious/unstable arrhythmia
contra.advice: The sympathomimetic load can precipitate ischemia or dangerous rhythms; use only under direct cardiology guidance.
Nicotine crosses the placenta and enters breast milk and is toxic to the developing nervous and cardiovascular system; avoid.
Highly vulnerable to poisoning and to lasting effects on the developing brain; not appropriate outside specialist care.
Acute pressor effect can worsen control; blood pressure should be managed first.
Nicotine can trigger catecholamine release and dangerous surges; avoid.

dihexa.accessNote: An experimental angiotensin-IV-derived compound (a metabolically stable HGF/c-Met activator) tested only in rodents; sold as a research chemical with no regulatory approval or human clinical data anywhere, and its foundational Washington State University mechanism papers were retracted in 2025 for research misconduct.

overdose.line: No human toxicity or overdose data exist, so a harmful dose is genuinely unknown and any acute reaction would be unpredictable; the specific mechanistic hazard is that its potent hepatocyte growth factor (HGF)/c-Met signalling drives cell proliferation and could accelerate the growth of an existing or occult tumour rather than cause a classic acute overdose. Rodent work also reported hepatotoxicity at doses only modestly above the effective range.

misuse.line: There is no established safe human dose: no clinical trials, no human pharmacokinetics, and no purity, content or contaminant guarantees from research-chemical supply, so any use is uncontrolled self-experimentation. The central concern is activation of the HGF/c-Met pathway, a well-recognised driver of tumour growth and metastasis, which is why a theoretical cancer risk is repeatedly flagged for this compound.

biomarkers.why: There is no dihexa-specific lab abnormality validated in humans; a baseline haematology screen is only a general safety reference given unknown-purity research-chemical material, not evidence that use is safe.
Baseline hepatic function reference; rodent studies showed hepatotoxicity just above the effective dose and the compound's human metabolism and clearance are entirely uncharacterised.
HGF/c-Met-driven proliferation would not be reliably caught by any routine blood test, so the theoretical tumour risk cannot be monitored away with labs.

contra.flag: Active cancer, prior cancer, or strong family/genetic cancer risk
contra.advice: HGF/c-Met signalling is an established promoter of tumour growth and metastasis, so activating it is a specific hazard for anyone with current, past, or elevated cancer risk.
No reproductive or developmental safety data exist, and a potent growth-factor-signalling agent carries obvious theoretical fetal and infant risk.

contra.flag: Anyone seeking a proven or approved treatment
contra.advice: With zero human safety or efficacy data and no approval, there is no context in which use is medically supported; effects and long-term risks are unknown.

pt-141-bremelanotide-vyleesi.accessNote: A melanocortin-receptor agonist approved as an on-demand subcutaneous injection for acquired, generalized low sexual desire (HSDD) in premenopausal women; the use discussed elsewhere (men, postmenopausal women) is off-label and outside its tested population.

typicalDose.line: As a reference only, the approved regimen a prescriber decides on is a single 1.75 mg subcutaneous injection taken as-needed at least 45 minutes before anticipated activity, with a hard ceiling of one dose per 24 hours and no more than 8 doses per month — this is not a recommendation to dose yourself, and the limits exist because of blood-pressure and skin effects.

overdose.line: Exceeding the dose or frequency amplifies its known effects: severe, sometimes protracted nausea and vomiting (nausea already affects ~40% at the normal dose), marked flushing, and a larger transient spike in blood pressure with a compensatory drop in heart rate. Because each dose raises BP for several hours, closely-spaced or doubled dosing risks dangerous hypertensive elevations, especially in anyone with cardiovascular disease.

misuse.line: The main harm is redosing outside the prescription — taking more than one dose in 24 hours, stacking doses to chase an effect, or exceeding 8 per month — which drives cumulative blood-pressure elevation and focal darkening of the skin, face, gums and breasts (melanocortin-driven hyperpigmentation that may not fully reverse). Grey-market or 'research' vials of uncertain concentration make the delivered dose unknowable, and combining it with other vasoactive drugs compounds the cardiovascular risk. It is not a chronic daily medication.

biomarkers.range: Each dose transiently raises systolic BP by roughly 6 mmHg and lowers heart rate for several hours (resolving within about 12 hours); a prescriber screens for and excludes uncontrolled hypertension before use
biomarkers.when: Baseline before starting and if cardiovascular symptoms arise
biomarkers.why: Its most clinically important effect is a transient pressor response, so uncontrolled or high-risk blood pressure is the key thing a doctor checks

biomarkers.marker: Skin and mucosal exam for hyperpigmentation
biomarkers.range: Clinical inspection (face, gums, breasts) rather than a lab value; risk rises with more frequent dosing and in darker skin
biomarkers.why: Melanocortin agonism can cause focal darkening that may persist after stopping

contra.flag: Uncontrolled hypertension or known cardiovascular disease
contra.advice: Must not use — the transient pressor effect of each dose can be dangerous; a prescriber excludes these before starting.

contra.flag: Outside the approved population (men, postmenopausal women)
contra.advice: Not established as safe or effective in these groups; any such use is off-label and unstudied for safety.
Should not be used; it is not established as safe in pregnancy.
Caution or avoidance — severe impairment can worsen nausea and alter drug exposure, so this is a prescriber's judgement, not a self-decision.

pde-5-inhibitors-sildenafil-tadalafil.accessNote: Prescribed for erectile dysfunction, benign prostatic symptoms and pulmonary arterial hypertension; a doctor must first check cardiovascular status because these drugs can collapse blood pressure when combined with nitrates.

typicalDose.line: For reference only, and always set by a prescriber rather than the reader: sildenafil for erectile dysfunction is usually 25–100 mg taken as needed about one hour beforehand, at most once in 24 hours; tadalafil is usually 10 mg (range 5–20 mg) as needed or a low 2.5–5 mg once-daily regimen. Pulmonary-hypertension dosing is different and separately supervised (for example sildenafil 20 mg three times daily, tadalafil 40 mg once daily). A doctor lowers the dose for kidney or liver impairment, older age, and interacting drugs. This is not a recommendation to take it or a self-dosing guide.

overdose.line: In overdose or excessive dosing the ordinary side effects intensify: severe headache, facial flushing, nasal congestion, dizziness, blurred or blue-tinged vision, indigestion, and marked drops in blood pressure causing fainting. The dangerous outcomes are priapism — a painful erection lasting over 4 hours, a urological emergency that can permanently scar erectile tissue if untreated — and profound, sometimes fatal hypotension when taken with nitrates or 'poppers'. Rare but serious events include sudden loss of vision in one eye (non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy) and sudden hearing loss, both of which warrant stopping the drug and seeking urgent care.

misuse.line: The lethal mistake is combining a PDE-5 inhibitor with any nitrate heart medicine or recreational nitrite 'poppers' — together they can drop blood pressure to a fatal level, which is exactly why a prescription and cardiac review exist. Other harms come from stacking or repeating doses to chase a stronger effect (raising priapism and hypotension risk), skipping the cardiovascular evaluation that screens for unstable heart disease, and buying unregulated 'herbal' or online products frequently spiked with undeclared, unmeasured drug or contaminants.

biomarkers.range: assessed before and during use; caution if resting BP is below ~90/50 mmHg or the patient is on multiple antihypertensives/alpha-blockers
biomarkers.why: The drug's core action is vasodilation; a doctor confirms the cardiovascular system can tolerate a further fall in pressure.
biomarkers.when: Before starting and if dizziness or fainting occurs

biomarkers.range: eGFR below ~30 mL/min/1.73m² prompts a lower starting dose
biomarkers.why: Impaired clearance raises drug levels and side-effect risk, so dosing is reduced.
biomarkers.when: Before starting; periodically in ongoing use

biomarkers.range: significant hepatic impairment (e.g. Child–Pugh B/C) prompts dose reduction
biomarkers.why: The liver metabolises these drugs; impairment increases exposure.

contra.flag: Taking any nitrate (nitroglycerin, isosorbide mono-/dinitrate) or recreational nitrite 'poppers'
contra.advice: Absolute contraindication — the combination can cause fatal hypotension; the two must never be used together.

contra.flag: Taking a soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator such as riociguat
contra.advice: Contraindicated — additive vasodilation and dangerous blood-pressure drops.

contra.flag: Recent heart attack or stroke, unstable angina, severe heart failure, or uncontrolled arrhythmia
contra.advice: Generally contraindicated until a cardiologist judges the heart can tolerate sexual activity and vasodilation.

contra.flag: Severe low blood pressure, or severe uncontrolled high blood pressure
contra.advice: Contraindicated — further vasodilation is unsafe at these extremes.

contra.flag: Severe liver impairment or end-stage kidney disease
contra.advice: Use is restricted and, if considered at all, only under specialist supervision with adjusted dosing.

contra.flag: Prior non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION) or a hereditary degenerative retinal disorder such as retinitis pigmentosa
contra.advice: Contraindicated or strongly cautioned because of the risk to vision.

melanotan-ii.accessNote: A synthetic melanocortin-receptor agonist sold only as a grey-market "research" peptide for self-injection; its close relative bremelanotide is an approved medicine, but Melanotan II itself has never passed human safety trials, and its long-term melanoma risk is unknown.

overdose.line: Because vials are dosed by eye and true potency is unverified, taking too much triggers intense nausea and vomiting, facial flushing, dizziness and loss of appetite; it also commonly causes prolonged painful erections (priapism), which is a medical emergency that can permanently damage erectile tissue if not treated within a few hours. Sharp rises in blood pressure and heart rate and, in isolated case reports, rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown) with acute kidney injury have occurred.

misuse.line: There is no established safe dose. The peptide is unregulated, so purity, sterility and actual peptide content vary widely between and even within vials, and home reconstitution and measurement errors are common. Documented harms include a rapid increase in the number, size and darkness of moles, new atypical (dysplastic) nevi, and published case reports of melanoma in users; the melanoma link is unproven but the tumour-promoting concern is why long-term safety is called unknown. Non-sterile self-injection additionally risks abscesses and bloodborne infection.

biomarkers.range: baseline and any new or changing lesion
biomarkers.why: MT-II darkens existing moles and drives new and atypical (dysplastic) nevi; serial skin exams are how early melanoma is detected
biomarkers.when: before and during any exposure, and for any changing lesion

biomarkers.why: melanocortin agonism raises blood pressure and heart rate via sympathetic outflow

biomarkers.range: markedly elevated (e.g. >1000 U/L) suggests muscle injury
biomarkers.why: rare rhabdomyolysis has been reported; checked if severe muscle pain or dark urine occurs

contra.flag: Personal history of melanoma, atypical/dysplastic nevus syndrome, or numerous moles
contra.advice: Must not use it; MT-II darkens and multiplies nevi and melanoma has been reported in users, making pre-existing mole risk especially dangerous.

contra.flag: Family history of melanoma, or fair skin with heavy freckling/many nevi
contra.advice: Avoid entirely; baseline melanoma risk compounds the unknown long-term skin-cancer concern.
Do not use; there is no human safety data.
Avoid; the drug can raise blood pressure and heart rate.

contra.flag: Conditions predisposing to priapism (e.g. sickle cell disease, myeloma, prior priapism)
contra.advice: Avoid; MT-II commonly causes prolonged erections that can become an emergency.

cagrilintide-cagrisema.accessNote: An investigational long-acting amylin analog still in phase 3 obesity trials, tested both alone and stacked with semaglutide; it has no regulatory approval for human use, so anything obtained outside a trial comes from unregulated grey-market peptide suppliers of unknown identity and purity.

overdose.line: Excess dosing drives the drug's own effects to extremes: relentless nausea, repeated vomiting, and diarrhea that can cause dehydration, electrolyte loss, and pre-renal acute kidney injury; protracted vomiting also carries an aspiration risk. Because it profoundly slows gastric emptying, an overdose can leave the stomach retaining food and fluid for many hours. Risk of dangerously low blood sugar is low with the amylin analog alone but rises sharply when it is combined with semaglutide or any other glucose-lowering drug.

misuse.line: There is no established safe do-it-yourself dose: trial regimens rely on slow week-by-week titration under supervision precisely because the gastrointestinal effects are severe (gastrointestinal adverse events affected roughly 80% of participants on the combination in phase 3), and skipping that titration invites intractable vomiting and dehydration. Grey-market vials carry no guarantee of identity, concentration, sterility, or endotoxin content, so a self-injector cannot know the true dose or whether the product is contaminated. Combining it with semaglutide or insulin without medical oversight compounds both the volume-depletion and hypoglycemia danger.

biomarkers.why: Repeated vomiting and diarrhea can cause dehydration and pre-renal acute kidney injury; a rising creatinine or falling eGFR flags volume depletion.
biomarkers.range: eGFR below ~60 mL/min/1.73m2, or creatinine rising >0.3 mg/dL above baseline, signals concern
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if persistent GI symptoms occur

biomarkers.why: Protracted vomiting drives hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis; low sodium can accompany dehydration.
biomarkers.range: Potassium <3.5 mmol/L or bicarbonate abnormal indicates vomiting-related disturbance
biomarkers.when: If persistent vomiting or diarrhea occurs

biomarkers.why: Acute pancreatitis is a monitored class-level precaution for incretin/amylin agents; severe persistent abdominal pain plus a raised lipase warrants stopping and evaluation.
biomarkers.range: >3x upper limit of normal with symptoms suggests pancreatitis

biomarkers.why: Amylin and incretin therapies can raise heart rate; cagrilintide's own effect is modest (mean ~2-3 bpm) but larger or symptomatic increases should be checked.
biomarkers.range: Sustained rise well above the typical few-bpm range warrants review

biomarkers.why: Hypoglycemia risk is meaningful when the amylin analog is combined with semaglutide, insulin, or a sulfonylurea.
biomarkers.when: Especially relevant with any concurrent glucose-lowering drug

contra.advice: Weight-loss agents are not used in pregnancy or lactation; anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or nursing must not take it.

contra.flag: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2
contra.advice: This is a hard contraindication for the semaglutide component when stacked, and is applied cautiously to the class.
A history of pancreatitis is a precautionary contraindication given the incretin/amylin class signal and its effect on GI motility.

contra.flag: Severe gastroparesis or serious gastrointestinal disease
contra.advice: The drug markedly slows gastric emptying, which can dangerously worsen pre-existing delayed emptying.

contra.flag: Type 1 diabetes or concurrent insulin/sulfonylurea use
contra.advice: Amylin analogs added to insulin have a documented severe-hypoglycemia risk; this combination must only ever be managed by a physician.

setmelanotide-imcivree.accessNote: Approved only for chronic weight management in rare genetic obesity of the melanocortin-4 pathway (POMC, PCSK1 or LEPR deficiency, Bardet-Biedl syndrome) and hypothalamic obesity, confirmed by genetic or clinical diagnosis and started under specialist supervision.

typicalDose.line: Given as a once-daily subcutaneous injection, always decided and titrated by the prescribing specialist. For reference only, in adults and children 12 and older it is typically started at 2 mg daily and adjusted after about 2 weeks up to a maximum of 3 mg daily; younger children start at a lower dose and are titrated in smaller steps. This is not a recommendation or a self-dosing instruction.

overdose.line: There is no antidote. Excess drug exaggerates melanocortin activation: marked skin darkening and new or darkening moles, severe nausea and vomiting, headache, and in males spontaneous or prolonged painful erections (an erection lasting over 4 hours is a urological emergency). Serious hypersensitivity, including anaphylaxis, has been reported and can be life-threatening. Any suspected overdose needs urgent medical care.

misuse.line: This is a specialist-supervised injection, not a general fat-loss shot. Using it without the confirmed genetic/clinical indication carries the risks with no proven benefit. Skipping the required baseline and periodic full-body skin exams can let a melanoma be missed among the drug-induced pigment changes; ignoring mood monitoring can miss emerging depression or suicidal thoughts. Stopping reverses the pigmentation and the appetite effect, so weight is typically regained. Never adjust the dose, double up for a missed day, or inject without the prescriber's oversight.

biomarkers.range: Baseline then periodically; dermatology referral for any changing lesion
biomarkers.why: The drug commonly darkens skin and existing moles by activating MC1R; regular exams are needed so a melanoma is not masked.
biomarkers.when: Before starting and periodically during treatment

biomarkers.marker: Depression / suicidality screening (e.g. PHQ-type mood review)
biomarkers.range: No new-onset or worsening depressive symptoms or suicidal ideation
biomarkers.why: Depression and suicidal ideation are recognised adverse effects; discontinuation is considered if they appear or persist.

biomarkers.range: Response typically assessed at ~12-16 weeks; continue only if clinically meaningful loss
biomarkers.why: Confirms the treatment is actually working; non-responders are stopped rather than dose-escalated indefinitely.
Children need monitoring of growth and development on chronic therapy.

contra.flag: Prior serious hypersensitivity to setmelanotide or any excipient
contra.advice: Contraindicated; anaphylaxis and other serious hypersensitivity reactions have occurred.
Not to be used; the benzyl alcohol preservative can cause fatal 'gasping syndrome' in this group.

contra.flag: Obesity not caused by the approved conditions (ordinary or lifestyle obesity)
contra.advice: Not indicated; benefit is unproven outside the specified melanocortin-pathway diagnoses and the risks still apply.

contra.flag: History of depression or suicidal ideation
contra.advice: Increased risk of recurrence; use only with close psychiatric monitoring and prescriber judgement.
Weight loss offers no benefit during pregnancy and is not recommended; discuss contraception and infant exposure with the prescriber.

t3-t4-thyroid-liothyronine-levothyroxine.accessNote: Prescribed to replace missing thyroid hormone in hypothyroidism and after thyroid removal, with the dose titrated to blood tests by a doctor.

typicalDose.line: As a reference only, doctors titrate levothyroxine (T4) to blood tests, with full daily replacement commonly falling in the range of about 50-200 mcg once daily (started lower in older or cardiac patients), while liothyronine (T3) is usually 25-75 mcg/day in divided doses; the exact dose is a medical decision, not something to self-set.

overdose.line: Excess thyroid hormone causes thyrotoxicosis: fast or pounding heartbeat, palpitations, atrial fibrillation, tremor, anxiety, insomnia, sweating, heat intolerance, weight loss and diarrhea; severe overdose can progress to thyroid storm with high fever, dangerous arrhythmia, heart failure and delirium. T3 (liothyronine) acts faster and peaks sooner than T4, so overdose signs appear more abruptly.

misuse.line: Stopping abruptly or skipping doses lets hypothyroidism return (fatigue, and at the extreme myxedema), while doubling doses or self-dosing for fat loss pushes the body into thyrotoxicosis, driving arrhythmia, muscle wasting and accelerated bone loss; absorption and effect are also altered by calcium, iron and antacids (reduced) and by warfarin (potentiated), so changes belong with a doctor and blood tests, not guesswork.

biomarkers.why: Primary marker doctors use to titrate the dose; a suppressed (low) value signals over-replacement, a high value under-replacement.
biomarkers.when: 6-8 weeks after a dose change, then periodically

biomarkers.why: Confirms circulating hormone is in range and cross-checks TSH.
Most relevant when liothyronine (T3) is used; an elevated level flags over-dosing and toxicity risk.
Over-replacement raises heart rate and the risk of atrial fibrillation, especially in older adults.
biomarkers.range: Resting HR roughly 60-100 bpm; watch for new irregular rhythm

biomarkers.why: Chronic over-replacement (suppressed TSH) accelerates bone loss, notably in postmenopausal women.
biomarkers.when: With long-term over-replacement or risk factors

contra.advice: Cortisol deficiency must be corrected first, or starting thyroid hormone can precipitate an adrenal crisis.
The body already has too much thyroid hormone, so adding more is contraindicated until treated.
Raising metabolic and cardiac demand soon after a heart attack can worsen ischemia; only under specialist care.

contra.flag: Uncontrolled coronary artery disease or arrhythmia
contra.advice: Increased cardiac workload can trigger angina or arrhythmia; requires cautious, doctor-supervised titration.

synephrine-higenamine-orlistat-brief.accessNote: Higenamine is a WADA-banned beta-agonist sold only as a grey-market "research"/supplement stimulant, and bitter-orange synephrine is an unregulated adrenergic supplement ingredient — neither is an approved human medicine; orlistat is a separately approved lipase-inhibitor drug (some strengths prescription, some over-the-counter) and is not the same thing as the stimulants it is grouped with here.

overdose.line: Stimulant toxicity from synephrine or higenamine presents as chest pain, severe hypertension, a racing or irregular heartbeat, palpitations, agitation, tremor, headache and overheating; documented severe cases in synephrine-supplement users include heart attack, ischaemic stroke and ventricular fibrillation, and risk is sharply amplified when combined with caffeine or other stimulants. Orlistat itself has low acute toxicity — overdose effects are mainly severe gastrointestinal (oily diarrhoea, cramping, urgency).

misuse.line: There is no established safe do-it-yourself dose: independent testing found higenamine supplements mislabeled from trace amounts up to 62 mg per serving (potential intake ~110 mg/day), so the actual amount ingested is unknowable. Stacking these stimulants with caffeine, other pre-workout agents or decongestants multiplies cardiac and blood-pressure risk, and combining synephrine with an MAO inhibitor can trigger a hypertensive crisis. Orlistat misuse also blunts absorption of fat-soluble vitamins and of co-taken medicines (e.g. thyroid hormone, ciclosporin, anticoagulants, antiepileptics).

biomarkers.marker: Resting blood pressure and heart rate
biomarkers.why: Synephrine and higenamine are adrenergic stimulants that raise both; sustained elevation (e.g. >140/90 mmHg or resting HR >100 bpm) signals cardiovascular strain.
Synephrine shows proarrhythmic effects in cardiomyocyte studies; QTc prolongation and arrhythmia are the mechanism behind reported syncope and sudden cardiac events.
biomarkers.when: If palpitations, syncope or chest pain

biomarkers.why: Rises with acute cardiac injury — used to confirm myocardial infarction reported after synephrine-containing products.
biomarkers.range: Elevated above assay upper reference limit
biomarkers.when: During chest pain / suspected cardiac event

biomarkers.why: Orlistat blocks absorption of fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E and K; deficiency develops over months of use.
Orlistat reduces vitamin K absorption and can destabilise warfarin, raising bleeding risk.
Rare but serious hepatocellular injury and acute liver failure have been reported with orlistat (FDA review: 13 severe cases, 2 deaths, 3 transplants).
biomarkers.when: If jaundice, dark urine or right-upper-quadrant pain

biomarkers.marker: Serum creatinine / eGFR and urinary oxalate
biomarkers.why: Orlistat increases enteric oxalate absorption, causing calcium-oxalate stones and oxalate nephropathy with acute kidney injury in postmarketing reports.
biomarkers.range: Creatinine ~60-110 umol/L; rising level flags injury
biomarkers.when: In those at renal risk or with stone history

biomarkers.why: Orlistat lowers levothyroxine absorption, which can worsen hypothyroidism in treated patients.

contra.flag: Cardiovascular disease — hypertension, coronary artery disease, arrhythmia, or prior heart attack or stroke
contra.advice: The stimulant components raise heart rate and blood pressure and are linked to angina, infarction, arrhythmia and stroke; these conditions are a hard stop.

contra.flag: Concurrent caffeine, other stimulants, decongestants, or an MAO inhibitor
contra.advice: Combinations sharply amplify cardiovascular risk, and synephrine with an MAO inhibitor can provoke a hypertensive crisis.
Adrenergic stimulation compounds an already catecholamine-driven or hypermetabolic state.
None of these agents is established as safe; orlistat's vitamin malabsorption and the stimulants' cardiovascular effects are both unsuitable in pregnancy.
These are explicit contraindications to orlistat, which further impairs nutrient absorption.

contra.flag: History of calcium-oxalate kidney stones or chronic kidney disease
contra.advice: Orlistat increases oxalate absorption and can precipitate stones or oxalate nephropathy with kidney failure.

contra.flag: Organ-transplant or on ciclosporin, warfarin, levothyroxine or antiepileptics
contra.advice: Orlistat lowers absorption of these narrow-margin drugs, risking transplant rejection, clotting instability or loss of seizure/thyroid control.
Fat-loss agents can entrench disordered eating and purging behaviour and carry no benefit that offsets that harm.

insulin-anabolic-misuse.accessNote: A hormone prescribed to control blood glucose in type 1 and type 2 diabetes; it is diverted and injected by some bodybuilders to drive nutrient uptake into muscle, where a single dosing error can be fatal.

typicalDose.line: There is no standard dose — an endocrinologist individually titrates insulin against a person's own blood-glucose readings, body weight, diet, and remaining pancreatic function, usually as a basal (long-acting) plus mealtime (rapid-acting) regimen adjusted continuously. The correct amount for one person can be lethal for another, so no reference figure can be applied without medical supervision.

overdose.line: Excess insulin drives blood glucose dangerously low (hypoglycemia): early sweating, tremor, hunger, palpitations and anxiety, progressing to confusion, slurred speech, blurred vision and aggression, then seizures, loss of consciousness, coma and death. In someone without diabetes — the anabolic-misuse scenario — even a few extra units taken without eating enough carbohydrate can be fatal, and rebound spikes plus neuroglycopenia can cause permanent brain injury. Hypoglycemia can recur for hours after the injectable's action outlasts a single meal.

misuse.line: For a prescribed patient, skipping or under-dosing risks dangerous hyperglycemia and ketoacidosis, while doubling up, injecting without eating, mixing up rapid- and long-acting types, or stacking on alcohol or a missed meal causes severe hypoglycemia. Non-medical anabolic use — injecting insulin without diabetes to force muscle glucose uptake — is a leading cause of hypoglycemic death in bodybuilding: there is no monitored feedback, action peaks unpredictably, and users often inject alone. Never abruptly change, self-adjust, or combine insulin regimens outside a doctor's direction.

biomarkers.range: target ~4.0–7.0 mmol/L (72–126 mg/dL); <3.9 mmol/L is hypoglycemia
biomarkers.why: The direct, minute-to-minute safety measure — the value that tips into life-threatening lows on overdose.
biomarkers.when: Multiple times daily on insulin; before/after meals and exercise

biomarkers.why: Reflects average glucose control over ~3 months and guides whether a prescribed dose is right.
Insulin shifts potassium into cells; large or excess doses can drop serum potassium enough to cause cardiac arrhythmia.
biomarkers.when: During high-dose or acute treatment and in overdose management

biomarkers.why: In a suspected non-diabetic misuse or overdose, a low C-peptide alongside high measured insulin points to injected exogenous insulin.

contra.advice: Injecting insulin without a genuine medical indication has no safe dose and is a documented cause of fatal hypoglycemia — this is exactly the misuse pattern to avoid.
Insulin must never be given during a low, and people who cannot feel their lows are at very high risk of a severe unheralded episode.

contra.flag: Inability to reliably monitor glucose or access fast carbohydrate
contra.advice: Safe use depends on frequent glucose checks and immediate treatment of lows; without both, a routine dose can become lethal.
Insulin lowers potassium further and can precipitate dangerous arrhythmias until the level is corrected.
Reduced insulin clearance prolongs its action and markedly raises hypoglycemia risk, requiring specialist dose adjustment.

letrozole-raloxifene-cabergoline-pregnen.accessNote: Each is an approved medicine for a distinct condition — letrozole for hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer, raloxifene for osteoporosis, cabergoline for high prolactin, and mesterolone as an androgen where approved — and none is intended for the physique or "cycle-support" uses they get diverted to.

typicalDose.line: Reference ranges a physician selects and monitors, never a target to self-set: letrozole is commonly 2.5 mg once daily for breast cancer; raloxifene 60 mg once daily; cabergoline is started low (about 0.25 mg twice weekly) and titrated slowly; oral mesterolone is often 25-75 mg daily in divided doses in countries where it is approved. The correct drug, dose, and duration depend entirely on the specific diagnosis and cannot be generalised.

overdose.line: Harm is drug-specific: excess cabergoline can cause orthostatic hypotension and fainting, severe nausea, hallucinations, compulsive/impulse-control behaviour, and — with sustained high doses — fibrotic heart-valve damage. Over-suppressing estrogen with letrozole produces crippling joint and bone pain, accelerated bone loss, and adverse lipid shifts. Raloxifene excess raises the risk of venous clots (DVT/PE) and stroke. Too much androgen from mesterolone drives acne, aggression, thickened blood, and worsened cholesterol. There is no simple antidote — treatment is supportive.

misuse.line: Skipping labs, self-adjusting, or stacking these to chase a physique strips away the monitoring that makes them safe: driving estradiol toward zero with letrozole wrecks joints, libido, mood, and bone density; unsupervised cabergoline risks valve fibrosis and dangerous blood-pressure drops; combining any of them with other hormones multiplies clot and cardiac risk. Doubling a dose, stopping abruptly, or using them without a diagnosis can rebound the very hormone being controlled and cause lasting harm.

biomarkers.range: kept within a physiologic range (roughly 10-40 pg/mL in men), not crushed toward zero
biomarkers.when: before and periodically during letrozole use
biomarkers.why: letrozole blocks aromatase; over-suppression causes joint pain, bone loss, and lipid and mood problems

biomarkers.when: at baseline and to titrate cabergoline
biomarkers.why: cabergoline is dosed to normalise an elevated prolactin, the actual therapeutic target

biomarkers.range: no new or progressive valve regurgitation/fibrosis
biomarkers.when: baseline and periodically on longer-term or higher-dose (higher cumulative) cabergoline
biomarkers.why: ergot dopamine agonists are linked to fibrotic valvular heart disease; the risk is cumulative-dose and duration related and is mainly seen at high exposure, less certain at usual prolactin-treatment doses

biomarkers.range: clinician-defined targets; watch for falling HDL
biomarkers.why: aromatase inhibitors and androgens both worsen the lipid profile and can suppress HDL

biomarkers.range: T-score above -2.5; track change over time
biomarkers.when: before and during prolonged letrozole or in osteoporosis on raloxifene
biomarkers.why: estrogen suppression accelerates bone loss; raloxifene is used partly to protect bone

biomarkers.when: baseline and periodically on oral androgen use
biomarkers.why: oral androgens can raise transaminases; mesterolone is not 17-alpha-alkylated and is less hepatotoxic than alkylated oral steroids, so this is a general precaution rather than a strong signal for this drug
androgens stimulate red-cell production, thickening blood and raising clot risk

biomarkers.range: interpreted against age and clinical context
biomarkers.why: exogenous androgen and estrogen manipulation suppress the HPT axis and gonadal output

contra.advice: Letrozole is teratogenic, raloxifene is contraindicated in pregnancy, and androgens virilise a female fetus — none should be used by anyone who is or may become pregnant without a specific, supervised indication.

contra.flag: History of venous thromboembolism or stroke (DVT, PE, clotting disorder)
contra.advice: Raloxifene increases clot and stroke risk and is contraindicated with a prior clotting event; disclose any such history to a prescriber.

contra.flag: Existing heart-valve disease or fibrotic disorders
contra.advice: Cabergoline can worsen valvular and other fibrosis and should be avoided in people with valvulopathy or a fibrotic condition.

contra.flag: Prostate cancer or male breast cancer
contra.advice: Androgens such as mesterolone can stimulate hormone-sensitive prostate and breast cancers and are contraindicated in these conditions.
Cabergoline can cause marked blood-pressure changes and needs blood pressure controlled and monitored before and during use.

contra.flag: No medical diagnosis / normal hormone levels
contra.advice: Using these to manipulate hormones without a diagnosis removes any therapeutic rationale and exposes the person to all the risks with none of the benefit — a doctor's assessment is required first.

epo-erythropoietin.accessNote: A biologic (epoetin) prescribed to correct anemia in chronic kidney disease, chemotherapy, and select other conditions; also a banned performance-enhancing drug in sport.

typicalDose.line: For reference only, not a recommendation: a physician individualizes the dose by body weight and lab values, starting low and titrating to the lowest dose that avoids the need for a blood transfusion. Doctors deliberately keep hemoglobin below roughly 11-12 g/dL, because pushing higher raises the risk of clots and death. Route (IV or subcutaneous), frequency, and iron support are all set and adjusted by the prescriber based on labs.

overdose.line: Excess EPO overshoots red-cell production and thickens the blood (polycythemia / high hematocrit), causing hyperviscosity: severe hypertension, headache, blurred vision, chest pain, and greatly increased risk of clots, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, heart attack, and seizures. Dehydration compounds it. In endurance athletes, dangerously high hematocrit has caused fatal clots, sometimes during sleep when heart rate is low. A separate danger is pure red cell aplasia from anti-EPO antibodies, which paradoxically leaves the person severely and transfusion-dependently anemic.

misuse.line: Not following the prescription is dangerous: overshooting the hemoglobin target, self-adjusting to chase a higher number, or continuing without monitoring drives hematocrit into thrombotic territory and can cause stroke or heart attack. Skipping iron support blunts the effect and prompts risky dose escalation; abrupt changes destabilize hemoglobin. Non-medical use for sport or 'endurance' has no legitimate dose, is done without lab monitoring, and has directly killed athletes via clots; it is also banned in all sanctioned sport.

biomarkers.why: Primary safety and efficacy monitor; kept controlled (commonly Hb ~10-11 g/dL) and never allowed to rise too fast or too high, as overshoot drives clot and death risk.
biomarkers.range: Hb target roughly 10-11 g/dL; reduce/interrupt if approaching or exceeding 11 g/dL
biomarkers.when: Weekly at start/after dose change, then monthly once stable

biomarkers.why: EPO commonly raises BP; uncontrolled hypertension is a contraindication and a red flag for hypertensive crisis/seizure.
Adequate iron is needed for the response; low iron blunts EPO and leads to unsafe dose escalation.
Confirms marrow response; a falling count on adequate dosing can signal pure red cell aplasia from anti-EPO antibodies.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if response is lost

biomarkers.why: Monitored particularly in kidney disease as red-cell mass and dialysis dynamics shift.

contra.advice: Must not start until blood pressure is controlled; EPO raises BP and can precipitate hypertensive crisis or seizures.

contra.flag: Pure red cell aplasia that began after EPO or other erythropoietin agents (anti-EPO antibodies)
contra.advice: Must not take EPO; antibodies neutralize the drug and the body's own erythropoietin.

contra.flag: Serious hypersensitivity to the product or its components
contra.advice: Anaphylaxis and severe skin reactions have occurred; do not use.

contra.flag: Active cancer where the goal is cure and anemia is not chemo-induced
contra.advice: ESAs have shortened survival and hastened tumor progression in several cancers; use only under strict oncology criteria, never off-label.

contra.flag: History of stroke, heart attack, or clotting disorder
contra.advice: Higher hemoglobin targets increase thrombotic events; requires the lowest possible dose and close specialist oversight.

contra.flag: Anyone seeking it for athletic performance
contra.advice: No safe non-medical dose exists; unmonitored use has caused fatal clots and it is banned in sport.

aod-9604-fragment-176-191.accessNote: A synthetic fragment of human growth hormone (residues 176-191) studied for obesity; its Phase IIb trial failed the primary endpoint in 2007 and development was abandoned, so it has never been approved as a medicine anywhere and is sold only as a grey-market research/injectable peptide.

overdose.line: There is no established human toxic threshold: across roughly 900 trial subjects it was tolerated much like placebo with no dose-limiting toxicity, so an isolated large dose of pure peptide has no well-defined poisoning syndrome. The concrete acute danger comes almost entirely from the unregulated injectable itself — bacterial or endotoxin contamination and non-sterile reconstitution can cause fever, chills, injection-site infection, abscess, and in severe cases bloodstream infection (sepsis), regardless of the peptide dose.

misuse.line: There is no safe DIY dose because no dose was ever approved and, unlike the pharmaceutical-grade product used in trials, grey-market vials have unverified identity, purity, and actual peptide content, and are reconstituted and injected without sterile technique. Anyone using it is taking on injection and contamination risk for a compound that failed to produce meaningful fat loss in its pivotal trial — real risk with no demonstrated benefit.

biomarkers.why: Trials showed no adverse effect on glucose metabolism, but any GH-axis peptide warrants monitoring for impaired glucose handling.
The fragment was designed not to raise IGF-1 (confirmed in trials); a rise would signal contamination with full-length GH or mislabelled product.

biomarkers.when: If fever, redness, or pain after injection
biomarkers.why: Detects injection-site or systemic infection from a non-sterile or contaminated preparation.

contra.advice: No human safety data exist in pregnancy or lactation; an unapproved injectable should be avoided entirely.
Growth-hormone-axis peptides are theoretically undesirable with active malignancy; avoid without oncology input.
No data in the developing; an unapproved GH fragment must not be used.

contra.flag: Anyone unable to inject sterilely or verify the product
contra.advice: Because it is only available as an unregulated injectable of unknown purity, the contamination and infection risk applies to every user.

mgf-peg-mgf.accessNote: A synthetic splice variant of IGF-1 (IGF-1Ec); the product sold as "MGF/PEG-MGF" is its C-terminal E-domain peptide, marketed only as a "research chemical." It has never completed human clinical trials, so no safety, dosing, or purity standards exist for people.

overdose.line: No toxic threshold has ever been characterised in humans — the peptide has never been formally studied for safety, so any "overdose" is entirely uncharted and its systemic effects are unknown. The most concrete and immediate danger is not the peptide's pharmacology but the injection of an unregulated product: research-grade vials can carry bacterial contamination or bacterial endotoxin, which can trigger fever, rigors, injection-site abscess and, rarely, sepsis — and there is no way to verify what a grey-market vial actually contains.

misuse.line: There is no safe do-it-yourself dose because none has ever been established in a controlled human study, and the purity, actual peptide content and sterility of research-grade vials are unverified — self-reconstitution with non-sterile technique risks contamination, endotoxin reactions and abscesses. Mechanistically, MGF is a growth- and proliferation-promoting peptide, so chronic use carries a plausible but unquantified concern about stimulating the growth of undiagnosed or pre-existing abnormal cells; this risk cannot be dosed away.

biomarkers.why: MGF belongs to the IGF-1 family, so the IGF axis is the lab most relevant to any growth-related harm — though MGF's effect on circulating IGF-1 is poorly characterised, making this a mechanistic rather than validated marker.

biomarkers.when: If fever, chills or injection-site pain appear
biomarkers.why: Rising inflammatory markers flag injection-related infection or an endotoxin reaction from a contaminated vial.

contra.flag: Any current or past cancer, or strong family cancer history
contra.advice: Must not use — MGF is a growth- and proliferation-promoting peptide and could accelerate the growth of malignant or premalignant cells.
Must not use — an unapproved growth-factor peptide with zero reproductive safety data.
Must not use — growth-factor signalling during development carries unquantified risk.
Must not use — injecting a non-sterile, unregulated product markedly raises infection and sepsis risk.

dsip-delta-sleep-inducing-peptide-emidel.accessNote: Sold only as a grey-market "research chemical" labelled not-for-human-use; no regulator (FDA, EMA, TGA, Health Canada) has approved it for sleep or any condition. The FDA had placed it in the compounding category reserved for substances raising significant safety concerns (citing immunogenicity); as of April 2026 it was removed from that category and is under FDA advisory (PCAC) review — it remains an unapproved substance, not a cleared medicine.

overdose.line: No human toxic dose has ever been defined, and animal studies show unusually low acute lethality — but that is not reassurance: grey-market vials contain unknown, unverified amounts of peptide, so any 'overdose' is unquantifiable. Reported dose-related effects include headache, nausea, dizziness or vertigo, excessive drowsiness and transient drops in blood pressure (best documented after intravenous use); the sharper acute danger is a febrile or anaphylactic reaction to bacterial endotoxin or impurities in non-sterile injectable material.

misuse.line: There is no safe DIY dose because DSIP has never completed the trials that would establish one. Grey-market product varies in purity, concentration and sterility, so every self-injection carries unknown-content, contamination and immunogenicity risk — the FDA has cited immunogenicity, meaning the body can mount an immune response against the injected peptide. Reconstituting and injecting an unapproved research chemical at home has no dosing standard, no quality control and no medical oversight.

biomarkers.when: if fever, injection-site reaction or feeling unwell after use
biomarkers.why: Rising inflammatory markers flag an infectious or inflammatory reaction to non-sterile injected material — the most realistic acute harm.

biomarkers.when: if signs of infection or systemic reaction
biomarkers.why: A raised or abnormal white-cell count points to infection or an immune response to contaminated material.
The FDA has cited immunogenicity concern for DSIP; antibodies raised against the peptide could in principle cross-react with the body's own signalling peptides. Testing is not routinely available.

biomarkers.when: baseline and if unwell after use
biomarkers.why: Not a known DSIP target-organ effect, but a general safety screen for injury from unknown contaminants in unregulated product.

contra.advice: No safety data exist in pregnancy or lactation; an unapproved injectable peptide must be avoided entirely.
No data in developing humans; must not be used.

contra.flag: History of anaphylaxis or hypersensitivity to peptides or injectables
contra.advice: Non-sterile grey-market peptides carry real anaphylaxis risk; prior severe allergic reactions make any use dangerous.
The documented immunogenicity concern is more hazardous where the immune system is already dysregulated.

contra.flag: Use of sedatives, opioids or other CNS depressants, or a tendency to low blood pressure
contra.advice: Reported drowsiness and blood-pressure lowering could compound with these; the interaction is uncharacterised, so avoid.

cerebrolysin-ara-290-brief.accessNote: Cerebrolysin is a porcine-brain-derived peptide mixture licensed only in certain countries (e.g. Russia, China, South Korea) for stroke and dementia and never FDA/EMA-approved, while ARA-290 (cibinetide) is an investigational Phase-2 EPO-derived peptide with no marketing approval anywhere.

overdose.line: Excess or rapid injection of Cerebrolysin can trigger hyperventilation, dizziness, agitation, flushing, chills and blood-pressure swings, and because it is a foreign porcine protein it can provoke hypersensitivity reactions up to anaphylaxis; it can also precipitate seizures in susceptible people. The dominant acute danger for both peptides is non-sterile, unverified grey-market vials, where injection can cause abscess, cellulitis, endotoxin/pyrogen fever-and-rigor reactions, and bloodstream infection.

misuse.line: There is no established safe DIY dose: human dosing for both compounds exists only inside monitored clinical settings or trials, and grey-market injectables are unregulated for identity, purity, dose and sterility. Self-injecting a foreign porcine-derived protein (Cerebrolysin) without oversight risks allergic reactions and seizures, ARA-290 human safety data come only from short supervised studies, and reconstituting or injecting unverified vials adds infection and immune-reaction risk on top.

biomarkers.when: if fever, or redness/pain/swelling at an injection site
biomarkers.why: Non-sterile grey-market injection is the leading harm; rising CRP and WBC flag injection-site or systemic infection.

biomarkers.range: baseline tryptase ~1-11.4 ng/mL; an acute rise of >20% + 2 ng/mL over baseline supports mast-cell activation
biomarkers.when: after any rash, wheeze, swelling or collapse post-injection
biomarkers.why: Cerebrolysin is porcine-derived; an acute tryptase or eosinophil rise confirms a hypersensitivity/anaphylactic reaction to the foreign protein.
Severe renal impairment is a labelled contraindication for Cerebrolysin; kidney function is checked before any clinical use because the peptide/amino-acid load is renally cleared.
ARA-290 itself is engineered to be non-erythropoietic and does not raise red-cell mass in trials, but a mislabelled or EPO-contaminated grey-market product could raise haematocrit and clotting risk.

contra.flag: Epilepsy, prior seizures, or status epilepticus
contra.advice: Cerebrolysin may increase seizure frequency, and epilepsy / active status epilepticus is a labelled absolute contraindication; these people must not use it.

contra.flag: Known allergy to porcine products or to the preparation
contra.advice: Hypersensitivity to any component is an absolute contraindication and risks anaphylaxis on injection.
Severe renal impairment is a labelled contraindication for Cerebrolysin; excluded from clinical use without specialist assessment.
No adequate safety data for either compound; should not be used.

contra.flag: Active cancer or high clotting/thrombotic risk
contra.advice: ARA-290 is designed to be non-erythropoietic and is not known to raise red-cell mass, so a direct proliferative/clotting risk is unproven; still, given its EPO-region origin and very limited human data, people with active malignancy or high thrombotic risk are best excluded as a precaution, and any mislabelled or EPO-contaminated grey-market vial would carry real clotting risk.

tretinoin-retinoids.accessNote: Topical tretinoin is prescribed for acne and photoaging; oral retinoids such as isotretinoin are prescribed for severe acne under a mandatory pregnancy-prevention and lab-monitoring program because they cause severe birth defects.

typicalDose.line: For reference only, and always set by a doctor, never self-selected: topical tretinoin is prescribed in strengths from roughly 0.025% to 0.1%, usually a pea-sized amount applied thinly at night, starting low to limit irritation. Oral isotretinoin is dosed to body weight and a cumulative target decided and adjusted entirely by a dermatologist. These figures describe how clinicians prescribe it and are not a recommendation to take it.

overdose.line: Overusing topical tretinoin does not usually cause systemic poisoning but produces chemical-burn-like injury: intense burning, redness, swelling, peeling, and blistering. Oral retinoid overdose or accumulation causes hypervitaminosis A — headache, nausea and vomiting, dizziness, drowsiness, unsteadiness, facial flushing, severe dry cracked lips and skin, and with sustained excess, markedly raised triglycerides (pancreatitis risk), raised liver enzymes, bone and joint pain, and raised pressure around the brain (pseudotumor cerebri) causing headache and visual changes.

misuse.line: The gravest misuse of oral retinoids is any use in pregnancy or without strict contraception — a single course causes severe, often fatal birth defects, which is why prescribed monthly pregnancy tests and two contraception methods are non-negotiable. Other documented harms: combining oral isotretinoin with tetracycline antibiotics (raised brain pressure), stacking it with vitamin A supplements or heavy alcohol (additive toxicity, soaring triglycerides), and self-escalating topical strength or frequency, which causes chemical burns rather than faster results. Never share a prescription or take someone else's.

biomarkers.why: Oral retinoids are potent teratogens; pregnancy must be excluded before, during, and after treatment
biomarkers.range: Negative required before dispensing, monthly during, and after stopping
biomarkers.when: Baseline, monthly, and post-treatment for oral isotretinoin/acitretin

biomarkers.why: Oral retinoids commonly raise triglycerides; very high levels risk pancreatitis
biomarkers.range: Normal <150 mg/dL (<1.7 mmol/L); clinicians act well before 500 mg/dL
biomarkers.when: Baseline then periodically on oral therapy

biomarkers.why: Oral retinoids can cause dose-related hepatocellular enzyme elevation
biomarkers.range: Roughly <40 U/L normal; treatment paused if persistently elevated
biomarkers.when: Baseline then periodically on oral therapy

biomarkers.why: Oral retinoids can raise cholesterol alongside triglycerides
biomarkers.range: Interpreted against standard lipid targets by the prescriber
biomarkers.when: Baseline and periodically on oral therapy

contra.advice: Absolute contraindication for oral retinoids and avoided for topical; causes severe birth defects — effective contraception is required with oral use
Oral retinoids are contraindicated; discuss any retinoid use while nursing with a doctor
Combined with oral isotretinoin this raises the risk of increased pressure around the brain (pseudotumor cerebri)

contra.flag: Significant liver disease or high triglycerides
contra.advice: Oral retinoids can worsen liver injury and lipid levels and are used with caution or avoided

contra.flag: Vitamin A supplementation or existing hypervitaminosis A
contra.advice: Additive toxicity with oral retinoids; supplemental vitamin A should be stopped
Topical tretinoin causes severe irritation on compromised skin and should not be applied there

contra.flag: History of depression or mood disorder
contra.advice: Mood should be monitored during oral isotretinoin; report new low mood, though a causal link remains debated

minoxidil.accessNote: The topical solution/foam is sold over the counter for pattern hair loss, but oral minoxidil is a doctor-prescribed vasodilator used off-label at low doses for hair loss and, at higher doses, for severe treatment-resistant hypertension.

typicalDose.line: For reference only, and always chosen and titrated by a physician: low-dose oral minoxidil used off-label for hair loss is generally prescribed in the range of roughly 0.25-5 mg once daily (often lower in women than men), while its original antihypertensive dosing historically ran higher (an effective range of about 5-40 mg/day, usually split). The topical form for hair loss is a 2-5% solution or foam applied to the scalp. This is educational reference, not a dose to self-administer.

overdose.line: Excess oral minoxidil produces exaggerated vasodilation: profound low blood pressure, dizziness or fainting, and a reflex fast heart rate. Marked salt and water retention causes rapid weight gain and swelling and can precipitate heart failure. A recognised serious complication is pericardial effusion (fluid around the heart) that can progress to cardiac tamponade, along with ECG changes and rarely myocardial ischemia. Overdose is a medical emergency.

misuse.line: Taking oral minoxidil without prescription and monitoring is dangerous: it is a potent vasodilator that on its own causes fluid retention and reflex tachycardia, which is why doctors typically co-prescribe a diuretic and often a beta-blocker. Self-dosing, doubling up, or combining it with other blood-pressure or heart medications can drop pressure dangerously or trigger fluid overload. Skipping the scheduled BP, weight and cardiac checks means the early warning signs of effusion or heart strain go undetected.

biomarkers.why: Primary effect is lowering BP; monitored to avoid dangerous hypotension and to guide titration.
biomarkers.range: Target individualized; symptomatic if systolic falls below ~90 mmHg
biomarkers.when: At initiation and each dose change

biomarkers.why: Reflex tachycardia is expected and may need a beta-blocker.
biomarkers.range: Concern if persistently >100 bpm at rest

biomarkers.why: Detects sodium and water retention and edema before overt heart failure.
biomarkers.range: Rapid gain of >1-2 kg or new edema is a flag

biomarkers.why: Screens for T-wave/ST changes and rhythm effects seen with minoxidil.
Screens for pericardial effusion, the key serious cardiac risk.
biomarkers.when: If fluid retention, breathlessness, or cardiac symptoms appear

biomarkers.why: Fluid balance and co-prescribed diuretics affect kidneys, sodium and potassium.
biomarkers.range: Creatinine, eGFR, Na+, K+ within lab reference

contra.advice: Contraindicated: minoxidil can provoke a catecholamine surge and dangerous BP spikes.

contra.flag: Recent heart attack or unstable/acute angina
contra.advice: Reflex tachycardia and increased cardiac workload can worsen ischemia; oral use is contraindicated or requires specialist care.

contra.flag: Significant heart failure or existing pericardial effusion/pericardial disease
contra.advice: Fluid retention and effusion risk can precipitate decompensation or tamponade.
Not recommended; systemic minoxidil is avoided due to fetal/infant risk and lack of safety data.
Do not use; risk of allergic reaction.

contra.flag: Uncontrolled or borderline low blood pressure
contra.advice: Additive hypotension can cause fainting and falls; requires physician oversight and often combination therapy.

estradiol-menopausal-hrt.accessNote: Prescribed for menopausal symptoms (hot flushes, night sweats, genitourinary atrophy) and prevention of osteoporosis; a progestogen is added when the uterus is intact to protect the endometrium.

typicalDose.line: Doctors individualise the dose to the lowest that controls symptoms. As reference only, transdermal estradiol patches are commonly prescribed in the 25-100 mcg/day range and oral micronised estradiol around 1-2 mg/day, with transdermal routes generally preferred because they avoid first-pass liver effects and carry lower clot risk. This is not a recommendation or a dosing instruction — the route, dose, and any progestogen are decided by a prescriber.

overdose.line: Acute estrogen overdose is rarely life-threatening but produces nausea, vomiting, breast tenderness, bloating/fluid retention, headache, and unscheduled vaginal bleeding (including withdrawal bleeding in women, and breast tenderness in men or children exposed accidentally). The more serious danger is sustained over-exposure, which raises the risk of venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) and, with unopposed estrogen in a woman with a uterus, endometrial hyperplasia and cancer.

misuse.line: Harm here comes from not following the prescription: taking estrogen without the prescribed progestogen when the uterus is intact drives endometrial hyperplasia and cancer; doubling doses or applying extra patches raises clot, stroke, and breast-tissue risk without added benefit; and self-sourcing or 'topping up' bypasses the screening (bleeding work-up, breast and cardiovascular assessment) that makes HRT safe. Abrupt stopping is not dangerous but hot flushes usually rebound. Any new leg swelling, chest pain, breathlessness, or unexpected vaginal bleeding needs urgent medical review.

biomarkers.why: Estrogen can raise blood pressure in some women; checked at baseline and follow-up.
Combined HRT modestly increases breast cancer risk over time, so screening is kept up to date.
biomarkers.when: per national screening schedule while on HRT

biomarkers.why: Any unscheduled or persistent bleeding is investigated to exclude endometrial hyperplasia or cancer.
Oral (not transdermal) estrogen raises triglycerides and can provoke pancreatitis if already high.
biomarkers.when: baseline and if on oral estrogen

biomarkers.why: Sometimes checked with transdermal patches or implants to confirm absorption and guide dosing.
biomarkers.range: therapeutic targets commonly cited around 200-600 pmol/L, but symptom control matters more than any single number
biomarkers.when: if response or absorption is uncertain

biomarkers.why: Estrogen is hepatically metabolised; monitored where liver disease is a concern.

contra.flag: Current or past estrogen-dependent cancer (breast or endometrial)
contra.advice: Systemic estrogen is contraindicated; discuss non-hormonal options with an oncologist/gynaecologist.
Must be investigated before any estrogen is started.

contra.flag: Active or previous venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) or known thrombophilia
contra.advice: Systemic estrogen raises clot risk; if HRT is considered, transdermal routes carry lower risk but need specialist assessment.

contra.flag: Active or recent arterial thromboembolic disease (heart attack, stroke, unstable angina)
contra.advice: Estrogen is contraindicated in the setting of recent arterial events.

contra.flag: Active liver disease or significantly deranged liver function
contra.advice: Avoid until liver function normalises.
HRT is not used in pregnancy.

amphetamine-adderall-lisdexamfetamine-vy.accessNote: Prescribed for ADHD and narcolepsy; a Schedule II controlled drug because of its high potential for dependence and abuse.

typicalDose.line: A doctor individually titrates from a low starting dose and adjusts to response and tolerability. As reference only, adult ADHD ranges are roughly 5–40 mg/day for mixed amphetamine salts or dextroamphetamine, and about 30–70 mg/day for lisdexamfetamine. This is set and monitored by a prescriber — not a self-dosing guide.

overdose.line: Overdose brings dangerous hypertension, fast or irregular heartbeat, chest pain, marked agitation, tremor, confusion, hallucinations or paranoia, dilated pupils, and high body temperature. Severe cases progress to seizures, heart attack, stroke, rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown that can cause kidney failure), circulatory collapse, and death. Combining with other stimulants or serotonergic drugs raises the risk sharply.

misuse.line: Taking more than prescribed, crushing or snorting for a faster hit, or redosing to stay awake rapidly builds tolerance, dependence, and cardiovascular strain, and can trigger stimulant psychosis. Stopping abruptly after regular use causes a withdrawal 'crash' — deep fatigue, hypersomnia, and depression that can include suicidal thoughts. Combining with an MAOI (or within 14 days of one) can cause a life-threatening hypertensive crisis; stacking with other stimulants or decongestants compounds cardiac risk.

biomarkers.range: Ideally <130/80 mmHg; treat/reassess if sustained higher
biomarkers.why: Amphetamines raise blood pressure; used to catch stimulant-induced hypertension
biomarkers.when: Baseline before starting, then periodically and after dose changes

biomarkers.why: Stimulants increase heart rate; persistent tachycardia signals a problem

biomarkers.range: Track trend vs. baseline / growth curve
biomarkers.why: Appetite suppression can cause weight loss and, in children, slowed growth

biomarkers.range: QTc <450 ms (men) / <470 ms (women) as a rough guide
biomarkers.why: Screens for structural or rhythm disease before/during stimulant use in those with cardiac risk factors or symptoms
biomarkers.when: If cardiac history, symptoms, or risk factors present

contra.flag: Structural heart disease, cardiomyopathy, serious arrhythmia, or coronary artery disease
contra.advice: Stimulants can precipitate sudden cardiac events; generally must not be used
Further blood-pressure elevation is dangerous; contraindicated until controlled

contra.flag: Use of an MAOI now or within the past 14 days
contra.advice: Risk of hypertensive crisis; must not be combined

contra.flag: Hyperthyroidism, phaeochromocytoma, or symptomatic cardiovascular disease
contra.advice: Amphetamine amplifies adrenergic effects; contraindicated

contra.flag: History of stimulant/amphetamine psychosis, or active substance-use disorder
contra.advice: High relapse and psychosis risk; avoid or use only under specialist supervision
